The Kentucky Legislative Research Commission is a joint legislative
committee of the General Assembly serving primarily as a fact-finding
and service agency both during and between sessions . Membership in-
cludes the leaders of both political parties .
The Commission by statute has the responsibility for legislative re-
search , statute revision including annotations , bill drafting , and for main-
taining a legislative reference library . Another service is the compiling and
publishing of administrative regulations . Also its professional staff ( 1)
compiles , edits , publishes and distributes the legislative journals , the session
acts , and the daily record of legislative activity during sessions ; (2 ) main-
tains custody of all General Assembly property and records between ses-
sions; and ( 3 ) furnishes the services and conducts the studies which the
General Assembly requests and those which , in the opinion of the Com-
mission , will facilitate the work of the Legislature .
Other Commission functions are to review the Constitution and to act
as Kentucky's Commission on Interstate Cooperation .

entries.ocr_text:
FOREWORD
Senate Resolution No. 16, 1956 Regular Session , directed the Legislative
Research Commission to conduct a study of the proposed Uniform Com-
mercial Cod , "with the view of determining generally its effect upon existing
Kentucky law and with the further view of collecting such information
concerning the Code as will enable the General Assembly to be fully
advised of the factors bearing upon the advisability and desirability of
enacting the Code into law in Kentucky ."
The Legislative Research Commission , in cooperation with the Kentucky
State Bar Association's Committee on the Uniform and Commercial Code ,
has prepared this detailed analysis of the proposed Code and its effect on
existing Kentucky law. This research report discusses every section of
the Code and compares it to the present law on the subject .
The 1957 Official Draft of the Uniform Commercial Code , together with
the 1952 Draft and Comments and Supplement No. 1, represents more than
1100 pages of material . This consolidation of the lengthy Code presentation
provides the members of the General Assembly a volume more suitable for
study of the Kentucky requirements .
As a "workbook ," the presentation here supplies the minimum amount
of material necessary for a proper consideration of the Code . Each section
of the Code is broken down into a format of three subsections. First , a
"Summary " gives a concise summation of the material covered by the
section. Next, a subsection labeled "Kentucky Statutes" lists the existing
statutes comparable to the proposed Code section. The third subsection ,
headed "Comments ," then gives a summation of the differences between
the proposed Code and the present Kentucky view on th subject . This latter
subsection includes a selective annotation of Kentucky decisions , general
authorities for the proposal and references to leading treatises on the
particular subject .
Mr. Alfonso L. Pisano , member of the Kentucky Bar and Senior Ad-
ministrative Analyst on the Commission staff , had primary responsibility
for the preparation of the study . Mr. William E. Johnson , staff Regulations
Compiler , prepared the draft on Article 9. Mr. George B. Akin on the staff
of the Statute Revision Division , compiled Article 10.
The Commission is indebted to the members of the Kentucky State Bar
Association Committee on the Uniform Commercial Code for the assistance
and cooperation they have rendered to the Commission in the development
of this study . Professor F. W. Whiteside , Jr. , of the University of Kentucky
College of Law , served the Committee as a consultant .
HARRY LEE WATERFIELD
Chairman and Director
Frankfort , Kentucky
December 16 , 1957
V

entries.ocr_text:
UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE
Analysis of Effect on Existing
Kentucky Law
PREFACE
I
The Uniform Commercial Code is the result of a project of the National
Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws and the American
Law Institute .
The National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Law was
organized in 1892. It is composed of from one to five commissioners from
each of the states , the District of Columbia , Alaska , Hawaii and Puerto Rico .
The commissioners are appointed either under express statutory authority
or general executive authority and they serve without compensation . The
commissioners from Kentucky are Senator Joe J. Grace , Paducah ; Repre-
sentative Robert L. Gullette , Nicholasville ; Orba F. Traylor and James T.
Fleming , Frankfort, of the Legislative Research Commission . Also Dee A.
Akers of the Commission is an associate member of the conference .
The purpose of the conference is best stated in Article 1 , Section 2, of its
constitution and bylaws:
"Its object shall be ( 1) to promote uniformity in state laws on
all subjects where uniformity is deemed desirable and practicable ;
(2) to draft model acts on (a) subjects suitable for interstate com-
pacts , and (b ) subjects in which uniformity will make more effec-
tive the exercise of state powers and promote interstate cooperation ;
and (3 ) to promote uniformity of judicial decisions throughout the
United States ."
Proposed subjects for legislation are submitted to standing and special
conference committees , which , after investigation and hearing report on the
desirability and feasibility of drafting a uniform law. Tentative drafts are
submitted from year to year and are considered section by section. Each act
is thus the result of tentative drafts subjected to the criticism and correction
of a select body of lawyers chosen from every part of the country .
To date the conference has drafted and approved one hundred and forty-
six uniform acts . Some of these acts have been withdrawn as obsolete . As
of the time of this writing, a total of eighty -six acts stand recommended for
adoption . Kentucky has to date adopted twenty -one of these acts . (Hand-
book of the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws ,
1955 , p . 322. )

entries.ocr_text:
II
The Commercial Code is the first effort of the Conference to promulgate
a comprehensive act covering the entire field of commercial transactions .
Prior acts have dealt with specific phases of commercial transactions . The
following list gives the prior acts and the date of promulgation by the Con-
ference together with the date of adoption in Kentucky where such is the
case .
UNIFORM ACTS
INCORPORATED INTO THE UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE
Act
Year
Promulgated
Year Kentucky
Adopted
Uniform Negotiable Instruments Law
Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act
1896 1904
1906 1937
Uniform Sales Act 1906 1928
Uniform Bills of Lading Act 1909
Uniform Stock Transfer Act 1909 1944
Uniform Conditional Sales Act 1918
Uniform Trust Receipts Act
Uniform Written Obligations Act
Uniform Fiduciaries Act
1933
1925
1922
There has been a widespread movement for a substantial revision of the
above acts and integrating them in view of present commercial practices .
The Uniform Commercial Code represents the commission's effort to meet
this need . This tremendous undertaking was financed largely by a grant
from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation of Pittsburgh , Pennsylvania ,
which was supplemented by contributions from the Beaumont Foundation
of Cleveland , Ohio , and from many business , financial and legal firms .
The actual drafting of the Code began January 1, 1945. Drafts were
considered by joint committees of both groups and discussed by the full
memberships at annual meetings until it was approved in 1952. In January ,
1955 , the joint committee issued Supplement No. 1 amending various sections .
During 1956 and 1957 , the entire Code was reconsidered in the light of recom-
mendations of various state agencies and private groups who undertook ex-
haustive studies of the Code. The result of this revaluation was the publica-
tion of the 1957 Official Edition of the Commercial Code which is now recom-
mended for adoption by all the states . This study is based on the 1957 Of-
ficial Edition of the Uniform Commercial Code .
The Chief Reporter of the Code was Professor Karl N. Lewellyn of the
University of Chicago Law School and the Associate Chief Reporter was
Professor Soia Mentschikoff . The drafting was done by practicing lawyers
and teachers in various law schools. In addition to members of the legal
profession , the drafters were aided and advised by many businessmen and
experienced bankers .
Before reaching its final form , each provision of the Code was given a
most careful and meticulous consideration . In most cases , even before any

entries.ocr_text:
draft was submitted to the general membership of the National Conference
and the Law Institute , it was approved by three groups : the advisers , the
Council of the American Law Institute and one of two sections of the Con-
ference of Commissioners .
After final approval by the Institute and the Conference , the Code was
approved by the House of Delegates of the American Bar Association .
As of this writing , Pennsylvania and Massachusetts are the only states
which have adopted the Code . It became effective in Pennsylvania on July
1, 1954 , and will become effective in Massachusetts in August 1958 .
III
The constitutional question most likely to be raised in connection with
the possible adoption of the Uniform Commercial Code in Kentucky is the
single subject provision . Sec. 51 of the Constitution provides :
"No law enacted by the General Assembly shall relate to more
than one subject , and that shall be expressed in the title , and no law
shall be revised , amended, or the provisions thereof extended or
conferred by reference to its title only, but so much thereof as is
revised , amended , extended or conferred , shall be re -enacted and
published at length ."
The Comments of the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform
State Laws and The American Law Institute state :
"The concept of the present Act is that 'commercial transactions '
is a single subject of the law, notwithstanding its many facets ."
"This Act purports to deal with all the phases which may or-
dinarily arise in the handling of a commercial transaction , from
start to finish ."
"Because of the close relationship of each phase of a complete
transaction to every other phase, it is believed that each Article of
this Act is cognate to the single broad subject 'Commercial Transac-
tions' , and that this Act is valid under any constitutional provision
requiring an act to deal with only one subject ."
From a review of the prior decisions of the Kentucky Court , it appears likely
that the Court would uphold the constitutionality of the Code on this basis .
The purpose of Sec . 51 of the Kentucky Constitution is fully discussed in
the leading case in this state on that subject , Phillips v . Covington and Cin-
cinnati Bridge Co. , 59 Ky . ( 2 Metc .) 219 ( 1859 ) , where the Court stated :
"A practice had become prevalent of uniting in the same act
of the legislature subjects which had no relation to each other , and
which were wholly dissimilar and unconnected . Hence it not un-
frequently happened that the title of an act gave no indication
whatever of some of the subjects to which its provisions related .
And by permitting amendments to be made to a bill , by which dis-
tinct and unconnected matters might be introduced into and made
a part of it , an improper influence was sometimes brought to aid in
its final passage . To remedy this evil the constitutional provision

under consideration was adopted. Such a construction should there-
fore be given to it as is necessary to render it effectual in accom-
plishing the object for which it was designed . But it should not be
so construed as to restrict legislation to such an extent as to render
different act necessary where the whole subject matter is con-
nected , and may be properly embraced in the same act.
"It is not necessary for the accomplishment of the purpose con-
templated to go from one extreme to the other , nor would such a
course be consistent with the intention of the framers of the con-
stitution . This prohibition should receive a reasonable and not a
technical construction ; and looking to the evil intended to be rem-
edied, it should be applied to such acts of the legislature alone as
are obviously within its spirit and meaning . None of the provisions
of a statute should be regarded as unconstitutional where they all
relate directly or indirectly to the same subject , have a natural con-
nection , and not foreign to the subject expressed in its title ." ( em-
phasis added )
Among the cases upholding this view are : Comm . v. Starr, 160 Ky . 260 ,
169 S.W. 743 ( 1914 ) ; Βοwman v . Hamlett , 159 Ky . 184, 166 S.W. 1008 ( 1914 ) ;
Talbott v . Laffoon , 257 Ky . 773 , 79 S.W. 2d 244 ( 1934 ) ; Fidelity & Columbia
Trust Co. v . Meek , 294 Ky . 122 , 171 S.W. 2d 41 ( 1943 ) ; Reed v . Comm ., 306
Ky . 295, 206 S.W. 2d 949 ( 1947 ) .
For a general discussion of this subject , see Crawford , The Construction
of Statutes , Thomas Law Book Co. , St. Louis ( 1940 ) . In sections 95-100 the
author cites Campbell v . Comm ., 229 Ky . 264, 17 S.W. 2d 227 ( 1929 ) , and
Owensboro v . Hazel , 229 Ky . 752 , 17 S.W. 2d 1031 ( 1929 ) .
The rule most analogous is that applied to codifications and revisions .
As stated in 82 C.J.S. , Statutes , Sec. 215c :
"A codification or revision of statutes under a comprehensive
title does not violate constitutional requirements that no bill shall
include more than one subject , expressed in its title ."
Among Kentucky cases in support of this rule is Fidelity & Columbia Trust
Co. v . Meek , 294 Ky . 122, 171 S.W. 2d 41 (1943 ) . This case held that an
act entitled "An Act revising the Statute laws of the Commonwealth ,"
enacting the revised statutes as the law of the Commonwealth , repealing all
prior statute laws of general and public nature and prescribing the effective
date of the act, does not violate the constitutional provision that no law
enacted by the General Assembly shall relate to more than one subject
and that shall be expressed in the title .
Among the cases from other jurisdictions are : Harris v. State , 228 Ala.
100 , 151 So. 858 ( 1934 ) ; Mathes v . State, 31 Fla . 291, 12 So. 681 ( 1893 ) ; Wid-
ney v . Hess , 242 Iowa 342, 45 N.W. 2d 233 ( 1950 ) ; State v . Pete , 206 La. 1078 ,
20 So. 2d 368 ( 1944 ) ; State v . Brassfield , 81 Mo. 151 , 51 Am . R. 234 ( 1883 ) ;
Duke Power Co. v . Somerset County Board of Taxation , 125 N.J. Law 431 ,
15 A. 2d 460 ( 1940 ) ; Comm . v . Evans , 156 Pa. Super 321 , 40 A. 2d 137 ( 1945 ) ;
and Chumbly v . Peoples Bank & Trust Co. , 166 Tenn . 35 , 60 S.W. 2d 164
(1933) .

entries.ocr_text:
ARTICLE ONE
GENERAL PROVISIONS
Part 1
SHORT TITLE , CONSTRUCTION , APPLICATION AND
SUBJECT MATTER OF THE ACT
Introductory Comment
Part 1 provides general rules for the construction of the Code . While
many of them are in accord with general Kentucky rules of statutory con-
struction , some are at variance with these rules .
Those rules of the Code which would require the most important
changes in Kentucky law are in UCC 1-105 , relating to the applicability of
the Code ; UCC 1-107 , relating to the validity of a release without considera-
tion ; and UCC 1-109 , relating to the use of section captions as part of the
act.
Section 1-101 . Short Title.
Summary
This act shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial Code .
Section 1-102 . Purposes ; Rules of Construction ; Variation by
Agreement .
Summary
(1) This act shall be liberally construed and applied to promote its
underlying purposes and policies .
(2) Underlying purposes and policies are :
(a) to simplify , clarify and modernize the law governing com-
mercial transactions ;
(b) to permit the continued expansion of commercial practices
through customs , usage and agreement of the parties ;
(c) to make uniform the law among the various jurisdictions .
(3 ) The effect of provisions of this act may be varied by agreement,
except as otherwise provided in this act and except that the obligations of
good faith , diligence , reasonableness and care prescribed by this act may
not be disclaimed by agreement but the parties may by agreement determine
the standards by which the performance of such obligations are to be meas-
ured if such standards are not unreasonable .
(4) The presence in certain provisions of this act of the words " unless
otherwise agreed" or similar words does not imply that the effect of other
provisions may not be varied by agreement under subsection ( 3 ) .

(5) In this act unless the context otherwise requires :
(a) words in the singular number include the plural, and in the
plural include the singular ;
(b) words of the masculine gender include the feminine and the
neuter , and when the sense so indicates words of the neuter
gender may refer to any gender .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 274.190 , 358.570 , 361.740 , 446.080 .
Comment
Kentucky law is in accord with the doctrine of "liberal" construction of
statutes set forth in this section. KRS 446.080 ( 1) provides :
"All statutes of this state shall be liberally construed with a
view to promote their objects and carry out the intent of the legis-
lature , and the rule that statutes in derogation of the common law
are to me strictly construed shall not apply to the statutes of this
state ."
See also on subject of strict or liberal construction , Crawford , Statutory
Construction , Sec. 239 ; 82 C.J.S. , Statutes, Sec. 311 .
The rule of construction announced in subsection ( 1) finds support in
Schultz v . Ohio County , 226 Ку . 633, 11 S.W. 2d 702 ( 1928 ) :
"The intention of the Legislature in enacting a law must be
the controlling factor in its construction and interpretation ( cite) ,
and where there is doubt as to the meaning of such laws the courts
may look to 'the historical setting surrounding its enactment ; the
public policy of the state ; the condition of its laws ; the habits and
manners of its people ; and all other prior and contemporaneous
facts and circumstances that throw intelligent light on the intention
of the lawmaking body .' Sewell v . Bennett , 187 Ky . 626 , 220 S.W.
517 (1920) ; Commonwealth v . Barnett , 196 Ky . 731 , 245 S.W. 874
(1922) ....”
See also Crawford , op . cit . Sec. 209 et seq .
Section 1-103 . Supplementary General Principles of Law
Applicable .
Summary
Unless displaced by the particular provision of this act, the principles
of law and equity , including the law merchant and the law relative to
capacity to contract , principal and agent , estoppel, fraud , misrepresentation ,
duress , coercion , mistake , bankruptcy , or other validating or invalidating
cause shall supplement its provisions .

Comment
A provision similar to this section has been in many of the prior uniform
acts . See the above cited sections .
Section 1-104 . Construction Against Implicit Repeal .
Summary
This act being a general act intended as a unified coverage of its subject
matter , no part of it shall be deemed to be impliedly repealed by subsequent
legislation if such construction can be avoided .
KRS 446.130 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is a statutory statement of a generally accepted canon of
construction . Repeals by implication are not favored . Demunbrun υ .
Browning , 311 Ky . 71 , 223 S.W. 2d 372 ( 1949 ) , at page 374 states :
"Repeals by implication are not favored , and a statute will not
be construed as repealing a prior statute unless it is so clearly re-
pugnant as to admit of no other reasonable construction . Tipton v .
Brown , 277 Ky . 625 , 126 S.W. 2d 1067 ( 1939 ) ."
See also 82 C.J.S. , Statutes , Secs . 286 to 290 , wherein Kentucky case au-
thority is cited .
Section 1-105 . Territorial Application of the Act ; Parties ' Power
to Choose Applicable Law .
Summary
(1) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction
bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation
the parties may agree that the law either of this state or such other state or
nation shall govern their rights and duties . Failing such agreement this act
applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state .
( 2) Where one of the following provisions of this act specifies the
applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective
only to the extent permitted by the law ( including the conflict of laws
rules ) so specified :
Rights of creditors against sold goods . ( UCC 2-402 )
Applicability of the article on Bank Deposits and Collections . ( UCC
4-102)
Bulk Transfers subject to the article on Bulk Transfers . (UCC
6-102 )
Applicability of the articles on Investment Securities . ( UCC 8-106 )
Policy and scope of the article on Secured Transactions . (UCC 9-102
and 9-103 )

entries.ocr_text:
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section changes the common law rules as to conflict of laws . Com-
missioners Comment :
"The section as revised subjects this section to the firm rules
provided in six sections and provides affirmatively for freedom of
contract as to the applicable law in all other multi -state transac-
tions . Where there is no agreement, the revised section requests
the court to apply the act to multi- state transactions having an
'appropriate ' relation to any state which has enacted it."
The statement of the rule made in the leading case of Scudder v . Union
National Bank, 91 U. S. 406 , 23 L. Ed. 245 ( 1875 ) , is often cited as most
nearly correct :
"Matters bearing upon the execution , the interpretation , and the
validity of a contract are determined by the law of the place where
the contract is made. Matters connected with its performance are
regulated by the law prevailing at the place of performance . Mat-
ters respecting the remedy , such as the bringing of suits , admissi-
bility of evidence, statutes of limitation , depend upon the law of the
place where the suit is brought ."
This view finds support in several Kentucky decisions among which
are Moody v . Barker, 188 Ky . 401 , 222 S. W. 89 ( 1920 ) ; Fry Bros. v . Theobold ,
205 Ky . 146 , 265 S. W. 498 ( 1924 ) ; New Domain Oil & Gas Co. v . McKinney ,
188 Ky . 183 , 221 S. W. 245 (1920 ) .
For a complete discussion of this question see Goodrich , Conflict of
Laws , Secs . 106 through 114.
Section 1-106 . Remedies to be Liberally Administered .
Summary
(1) The remedies provided in this act shall be liberally administered
so that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as he would
have been in if the other party had fully performed , but consequential ,
special , or penal damages may not be had except as specifically provided in
this act, or by other rule of law .
(2) Any right or obligation declared by this act is enforceable by
action unless the provision declaring it specifies a different and limited
effect .
KRS 361.720 , 446.080 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Generally , statutes providing remedies are liberally construed . Nall v .
Comm ., 299 Ky . 792, 187 S. W. 2d 443 ( 1945 ) :

"... well -adopted principle and rule, not only of this court ,
but of almost universal ruling , that statutory enactment should be
liberally construed in respect of purposes for which it was enacted
and where there is a doubt as to the construction , the statute should
be construed always in favor of the remedy ."
This position is by far the weight of authority although there are cases
contra , among which is Norfolk & W. Ry . Co. v . McCoy , 288 Ky . 458, 156
S. W. 2d 493 ( 1941 ) :
"... well -known rule of statutory construction that a remedial
or curative statute which grants a right or provides a remedy , must
be strictly followed in all respects . Comm . v . Glover , 132 Ky . 588 ,
116 S. W. 769 ( 1909 ) ; Hurry Up Broadway Co. v. Shannon , Auditor ,
267 Ky . 302 , 102 S. W. 2d 30 ( 1937 ) .
For a good discussion of this point see 82 C.J.S., Statutes , Secs . 388 and 395 ;
Crawford , Statutory Construction , Secs . 251 through 255.
Generally , punitive damages are not recoverable for a breach of contract
action . Cumberland T & T Co. v . Sutton , 156 Ky . 191 , 160 S. W. 949 ( 1913 ) .
See also , Corbin, Contracts , Sec. 1077 .
Section 1-107 . Waiver or Renunciation of Claim or Right after
Breach .
Summary
Any claim or right arising out of an alleged breach can be discharged
in whole or in part without consideration by a written waiver or renuncia-
tion signed and delivered by the aggrieved party .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.119 (3 ) , 356.120 ( 2) , 356.122 .
Commissioners Comment :
Comment
"This section makes consideration unnecessary to the effective
renunciation or waiver of rights or claims arising out of an alleged
breach of a commercial contract where such renunciation is in writ-
ing and signed by the aggrieved party . Its provisions , however , must
be read in conjunction with the section imposing an obligation of
good faith . ( UCC 1-203 ) ."
Ordinarily for a release to be effective , it must be based on a con-
sideration of some sort . 45 Am . Jur., Release , Sec. 11. Bement v . Ohio
Valley Banking & T. Co. , 99 Ky . 109 , 35 S. W. 139 ( 1896 ) .
There has been a conflict of authorities on the question of whether Sec .
122 of the Uniform Negotiable Instruments Law was intended to cover re-
leases . In an excellent opinion written by Justice Drury discussing this
question in regard to Ky . Stat . Sec. 3720b - 122 ( KRS 356.122) , the court
adopted the majority view . Gannon v. Bronston , 246 Ky . 612, 55 S. W. 2d
358 (1933 ) :

entries.ocr_text:
"Renunciation : in general , the act of giving up a right , of re-
nouncing , or giving up something possessed , the giving up of a right ,
the legal act by which a person abandons a right acquired , but
without transferring it to another . The term imparts a gratuitous
abandonment or giving up of a right ; an express waiver without
consideration ; and it is said not to import a release or discharge for
a valuable consideration ." ( emphasis added )
For what constitutes consideration and a general discussion of releases
see 45 Am . Jur. , Release , Secs. 11 through 16 .
This section by allowing release without consideration changes present
law.
Section 1-108 . Severability .
Summary
If any provision or clause of this act or application thereof to any per-
son or circumstances is held invalid , such invalidity shall not affect other
provisions or applications of the act which can be given effect without
the invalid provision or application , and to this end the provisions of this
act are declared to be severable .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 446.090 .
Comment
This section states a well recognized canon of statutory construction .
See Crawford , Statutory Construction , Secs . 144 and 145. Owensboro v .
Hazel , 229 Ky . 752, 17 S. W. 2d 1031 ( 1929 ) .
This section is in accord with KRS 446.090 which provides that the
provisions of all statutes are severable .
Section 1-109 . Section Captions .
Summary
Section captions are parts of this act.
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The provision of this section is contrary to Kentucky law . KRS 446.140
provides :
"Title heads , chapter heads, section and subsection heads or
titles , and explantory notes and cross references, in the Kentucky
Revised Statutes , do not constitute any part of the law ."
There is authority for the view taken in this Code section. See Craw-
ford , Statutory Construction , Sec. 207, and cases cited .

Part 2
GENERAL DEFINITIONS
AND PRINCIPLES OF INTERPRETATION
Introductory Comment
Part 2 is comprised of definitions and rules that are applicable to the
entire Code and any transaction subject to it. The definitions are generally
in accord with Kentucky's present law. However , it should be noted that a
more comprehensive analysis of the definitions can be made only in con-
struing the sections in which the terms are used.
Section 1-201 . General Definitions .
Summary
(See comment below ) .
(See comment below ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) "Action" includes recoupment , counterclaim , set- off, suit in equity
and other proceedings . Similar definitions are contained in KRS 356.190,
358.580, 361.760, 446.010 .
(2) "Aggrieved party " , a party entitled to resort to a remedy . No
statutory definition .
(3) "Agreement " means the bargain of the parties in fact as found in
their language or by implication from other circumstances including course
of dealing or usage of trade or course of performance as provided in this
act (UCC 1-205 and 2-208 ) . Whether an agreement has legal consequences
is determined by the provisions of this act, if applicable ; otherwise , by the
law of contracts (UCC 1-103 ) . No comparable statutory definition . The
term should be compared with "contract ", (see ( 11 ) below ) .
(4) "Bank " , any person engaged in the busines sof banking . This
definition is broader tha nthe present definition in KRS 356.190, 287.010 ( 1)
( a) . "Person " ( see (30 ) below ) includes individuals and organizations
( q.v. ( 28 ) ) . Since 1906 , incorporation has been required of all banks in
Kentucky . ( 1906 , Ky . Acts c. 44 , Sec . 1 )
(5) "Bearer " , the person in possession of an instrument , document of
title or security payable to bearer or indorsed in blank . This extends the
present definition of "Bearer " contained in KRS 356.190 .
(6) "Bill of lading " , a document evidencing the receipt of goods for
shipment issued by a person in the shipping or forwarding business and in-
cludes an airbill . "Airbill" , a document serving air transportation as a bill
of lading does surface transportation and includes an air consignment note
or air waybill. No statutory definition . Bill of lading referred to in KRS
361.760 ( 1 ) and 276.470 . "Airbill " is new .

ownership rights or security interest of a third party in the goods buys in
ordinary course from a person in the business of selling goods of that kind
but does not include a pawnbroker . "Buying" may be for cash or by ex-
change of other property or on secured or unsecured credit and includes re-

does not include a transfer in bulk or as security for or in total or partial
satisfaction of a money debt .
(10) "Conspicuous " . A term or clause is conspicuous when it is so
written that a reasonable person against whom it is to operate ought to have
noticed it. A printed heading in capitals is conspicuous . Language in the
body is " conspicuous" if it is in larger or other contrasting type or color .
Any term in a telegram is conspicuous . Whether a term is "conspicuous" or
not is for the court to decide. The definition is new . It establishes some
standards but generally leaves the issue to the court .

come due in the ordinary course of business or cannot pay his debts as they

(24 ) "Money " , a medium of exchange authorized or adopted by a

Commissioners Comment :

by authorization before issue or adoption afterward , which recog-
nizes the circulating medium as part of the official currency of that

(25) A person has "notice " when :
(a) he has actual knowledge ; or
(b) he has received a notice or notification ; or

reason to know . A person "knows " or has "knowledge " of a
fact when he has actual knowledge of it . "Discover ” or “learn "
or a word of similar import refers to knowledge rather than to
reason to know . The time and circumstances under which a

ordinary course whether or not the other actually comes to know of it. This

(27) One "receives " a notice or notification when :
(a) it comes to his attention ; or

contract was made or at any other place held out by him as
place for receipt of such communications .
Notice , knowledge or a notice or a notification received by an organization

conducting the transaction , and in any event, from the time when it would

responds to KRS 274.220 ( 1 ) ( e) , 358.580 ( 1 ) ( j ) , 361.760 .
(34) "Remedy " , any remedial right to which an aggrieved party is
entitled with or without resort to a tribunal . No comparable statutory term .
(35) "Representative " , an agent, an officer of a corporation or associ-
ation , and a trustee , executor or administrator of an estate , or any other
person empowered to act for another . New definition .
(36 ) "Rights ", includes remedies . Commissioners Comment :
"The purpose is to make clear that both remedy and rights
(as defined ) include those remedial rights of 'self help ' which are
among the most important bodies of rights under this Act , remedial
rights being those to which an aggrieved party can resort on his
own motion ."
(37 ) "Security interest " , an interest in personal property or fixtures
which secures payment or performance of an obligation . The retention or
reservation of title by a seller of goods notwithstanding shipment or de-
livery to the buyer ( UCC 2-401 ) , is limited in effect to a reservation of a
"security interest ." The term also includes any interest of a buyer of
accounts, chattel paper , or contract rights which is subject to Article 9 .
The special property interest of a buyer of goods on identification of such
goods to a contract for sale under UCC 2-401 is not a "security interest " ,
but a buyer may also acquire a " security interest " by complying with
Article 9. Unless a lease or consignment is intended as security , reserva-
tion of title thereunder is not a "security interest" but a consignment is in
any event subject to the provisions on consignment sales ( UCC 2-326 ) .
Whether a lease is intended as security is to be determined by the facts
of each case ; however , (a) the inclusion of an option to purchase does not
of itself make the lease one intended for security , and (b ) an agreement

(39) "Signed " , any symbol executed or adopted by a party with
present intention to authenticate a writing. See Commissioners Comment
as to intended scope of this term . KRS 446.060 ( 1) . See also UCC 3-401
of the Code and comment as to signatures on instruments . The broadening

(40 ) "Surety ", includes guarantor . This definition is new . For the
purposes of this act, the technical distinctions between a "surety " and a
"guarantor " are ignored just as they often are in present practice .
(41) "Telegram " , a message transmitted by radio , teletype , cable ,
any mechanical method of transmission or the like. The present statutes
contain no similar definition .
(42) "Term " , that portion of an agreement which relates to a par-
ticular matter . This is a new definition but is in accord with accepted
understanding of the term . See Black's Law Dictionary : Webster's New
International Dictionary , (2d ed.) , "Term " 4.
(43) "Unauthorized " signature or indorsement , one made without
actual , implied or apparent authority , including a forgery . There is no
similar definition in the present statutes. See UCC 3-404 and comment as
to effect of an unauthorized signature on an instrument .
(44) "Value " . Except as otherwise provided with respect to negotiable
instruments and bank collections ( UCC 3-303 , 4-208 and 4-209 ) a person
gives " value" for rights if he acquires them :
(a) in return for a binding commitment to extend credit or for
the extension of immediately available credit whether or not
drawn upon and whether or not a charge -back is provided for
in the event of difficulties in collection ; or
(b) as security for or in total or partial satisfaction of a pre-
existing claim ; or
(c) by accepting delivery pursuant to a pre -existing contract for
purchase; or
(d) generally , in return for any consideration sufficient to sup-
port a simple contract .

Section 1-202 . Prima Facie Evidence by Third Party Documents .
Summary
A document in due form purporting to be a bill of lading , policy or
certificate of insurance , official weigher's or inspector's certificate , consular
invoice , or any other document authorized or required by the contract to
be issued by a third party shall be prima facie evidence of its own
authenticity and genuineness and of the facts stated in the document .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section gives statutory sanction to a well accepted rule of evidence .
See 32 C. J. S. , Evidence , Sec . 767 :
"Private writings are, as a rule , prima facie , but not con-
clusive , evidence of the facts stated or recited therein or shown
thereby ."
The documents mentioned may be impeached and disproved . The Com-
missioners Comment :
"Provisions go no further than establishing the documents
in question as prima facie evidence and leave to the court the
ultimate determination of the facts where the accuracy or authen-
ticity of the documents is questioned . In this connection , the section
calls for a commercially reasonable interpretation ."
Section 1-203 . Obligation of Good Faith .
Summary
Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good
faith in its performance or enforcement .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The good faith requirement stated in this section has long been implicit
in commercial dealing . One of the requirements for a holder in due course
under present Negotiable Instruments Law is that he take the instrument in
" good faith" KRS 356.052 ( 3 ) .

Section 1-204 . Time : Reasonable Time ; " Seasonably" .
Summary
(1 ) Wherever the action must be taken in a reasonable time , any
time that is not manifestly unreasonable may be fixed by agreement.
(2) What is a reasonable time depends on the nature , purpose and
circumstances of the action .
(3 ) An action is taken "seasonably" when taken within the time
agreed or absent agreement within a reasonable time .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.053 , 356.071 , 356.192 .
Comment
KRS 356.192 is in substantial accord with subsection ( 2 ) . The section
recognizes the right of the parties to make their own terms in the contract .
Only when the time is " manifestly " unreasonable is this right denied . See
Carhartt Holding Co., v . Mitchell, 261 Ky . 297, 87 S.W. 2d 360 ( 1935 ) .
Section 1-205 . Course of Dealing and Usage of Trade .
Summary
( 1) A course of dealing is a sequence of previous conduct between
the parties which is fairly regarded as establishing a common basis of
understanding for interpreting their expressions and other conduct .
(2) A usage of trade is any practice or method of dealing having
such regularity of observance in a place , vocation or trade as to justify an
expectation that it will be observed with respect to the transaction in
question. The existence and scope of such usage are to be proved as facts .
If it is established that such a usage is embodied in a written trade code
or similar writing, the interpretation of the writing is for the court .
(3) A course of dealing between parties and any usage of trade in
the vocation or trade in which they are engaged or of which they are or
should be aware give particular meaning to and supplement or qualify
terms of an agreement.
(4) The express terms of an agreement and any applicable course of
dealing or usage of trade shall be construed wherever reasonable as con-
sistent with each other ; but when such construction is unreasonable , express
terms control both course of dealing and usage of trade and course of
dealing controls usage of trade .
(5) An applicable usage of trade in the place where any part of
performance is to occur shall be used in interpreting the agreement as to
that part of the performance .
(6 ) Evidence of a relevant usage of trade offered by one party is not
admissable unless and until he has given the other party such notice as
the court finds sufficient to prevent unfair surprise to the latter .

Section 1-206. Statute of Frauds for Kinds of Personal Property
Not Otherwise Covered .
Summary
(1) Except in the cases described in subsection ( 2) of this section,
a contract for the sale of personal property is not enforceable by way of
action or defense beyond five thousand dollars in amount or value of
remedy unless there is some writing which indicates that a contract for
sale has been made between the parties at a defined or stated price , reason-
ably identifies the subject matter, and is signed by the party against whom
enforcement is sought or by his authorized agent.
(2) Subsection ( 1 ) of this section does not apply to contracts for
the sale of goods ( UCC 2-201 ) , nor of securities ( UCC 8-319 ) , nor to security
agreements ( UCC 9-203 ) .
KRS 371.010 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section makes the Statute of Frauds applicable to contracts
involving the sale of personalty where the amount exceeds five thousand
dollars . This fills up a gap in the Code and adds to KRS 371.010 .
Section 1-207 . Performance or Acceptance under Reservation of
Rights .
Summary
A party who with explicit reservation of rights performs or promises
performance or assents to performance in a manner demanded or offered
by the other party , does not thereby prejudice the rights reserved . Such
words as "without prejudice ", "under protest " , or the like are sufficient .
Kentucky Statutes
None.

Section 1-208 . Option to Accelerate at Will .
Summary
A term providing that a party or his successors in interest may accel-
erate payment or performance "at will " or "when he deems himself inse-
cure" or in words of similar import shall be construed to mean that he
shall have power to do so only if he in good faith believes that the prospect
of payment or performance is impaired . The burden of establishing lack of
good faith is on the other party .

Kentucky Statutes
Comment

can be so construed and which further might be held to make the
agreement void as against public policy or to make the contract
illusory or too indefinite for enforcement , the clause means that
the option is to be exercised only in the good faith belief that the
prospect of payment or performance is impaired ."

ARTICLE TWO
SALES
Introduction
The law of sales is largely a part of the law of contracts , albeit a very
special one . As it is generally known , all law of contracts springs from the
development of the action of assumpsit as a variation of an action on the
case in medieval England . At almost the same time , the city of London was
developing into a mercantile center with merchants from all parts of Europe
represented in a mercantile community . The merchants developed their own
customs of trading , importing many concepts from the commercial laws of
their homelands . The English courts recognized these customs of merchants
(known as the "law merchant " ) , as an important consideration in the evi-
dence of what a particular contract law generally was tempered by concepts
of commercial law wholly foreign to the common law, but by incorporation
at this early date into the body of law they became part of it. The common
law as thus modified , and as it further developed , was not wholly satisfac-
tory . It contained many fictions , sophistries and ambiguities . It regarded
all persons as dealing at arms length . This may be quite proper as between
merchants in the city of London , but as between a merchant and a casual
customer it was apparent that the rule of caveat emptor produced uncon-
scionable results . The concept of "title " , while apparently simple , is in fact
wholly incomprehensible to all but skilled lawyers and even they have con-
siderable difficulty in determining when title passes from one person to
another .
From time to time both the courts and parliament took a hand in at-
tempting to remedy various difficulties found to exist in the common law.
For their part the courts developed doctrines of warranties which would be
implied in various sales under certain circumstances . Parliament enacted a
statute of frauds as applicable to sales of personalty . But it was clear that
businessmen in a business community needed clearer and more simple rules
governing their everyday business transactions . In 1893 parliament enacted
the "Sales of Goods Act ." In the United States, the need was the more
pressing for the reason that the common law was applied differently in each
of their several jurisdictions and that varying statutory provisions in the
several jurisdictions also compounded the diversity . Hence the " Uniform
Sales Act" modeled largely upon the pattern of the English Sales of Goods
Act was approved by the Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State
Laws in 1906. Kentucky adopted the Uniform Sales Act in 1928 .
The Sales Article of the Uniform Commercial Code undertakes substan-
tial changes in the law of sales . Unlike the Uniform Sales Act and its Eng-
lish counterpart , the Sales of Goods Act, which were in essence a codifica-
tion of the common law, the Code Sales Article attempts to define the ele

Part 1
SHORT TITLE , GENERAL CONSTRUCTION
AND SUBJECT MATTER
Introductory Comment
Part 1 of this article , dealing with definitions and construction , sets out
a title for the article . Substantial and important provisions are contained in
the definitions .
Section 2-101 . Short Title .
Summary
This article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial
Code-Sales .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
As pointed out in the Commissioners Comment , this article is broader
in scope than the Uniform Sales Act (USA ) in that it includes rules which
reach into areas not usually thought of as sales law proper . For example , it
has special views on the formation of contracts , (UCC 2-204 , 2-205 , 2-206
and 2-207 ) ; the admissibility of evidence , (UCC 2-724 ) ; the parol evidence
rule , (UCC 2-202 ) ; matters of pre -trial procedure ( UCC 2-515 ) ; and many
others . The article is arranged in terms of contract for sale and the various
steps of its performance , and the legal consequences are stated as following
directly from the contract and action taken under it without resort to pas-
sage of property or title as the determining factor .
Section 2-102 . Scope ; Certain Security and Other Transactions
Excluded From This Article .
Summary
Unless the context otherwise requires , this article applies to transactions
in goods ; it does not apply to security transactions even though in the form
of a sale, nor does it affect any statute regulating sales to any specified class
of buyers .
KRS 361.750 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section continues the rule of KRS 361.750 of the Uniform Sales Act,
which excludes from coverage transactions which , although in the form of
a sale , are in fact "intended to operate by way of mortgage, pledge , charge ,
or other security ." Since transactions intended to operate " only " as security

Section 2-105 . Definitions : Transferability ; " Goods " ; " Future "
Goods ; " Lot" ; "Commercial Unit. "
Summary
( 1) "Goods" means all things which are movable at the time of iden-
tification to the contract other than the money in which the price is to be
paid , investment securities and things in action . "Goods " include the unborn
young of animals , and growing crops and other identified things attached to
realty as described in the section on goods to be severed from realty (UCC
2-107 ) .
(2) Goods must be both existing and identified before any interest can
pass . Goods which are not both are "Future " goods . A purported present
sale of future goods operates as a contract to sell .
(3 ) A part interest in existing identified goods may be sold .
(4) An undivided share in an identified bulk of fungibles is suf-
ficiently identified to be sold although the quantity of the bulk is not known .
An agreed amount or proportion of such a bulk may be sold and the buyer
becomes an owner in common .
(5) " Lot" means a parcel or article which is the subject of a separate
sale or delivery whether or not it is sufficient to perform the contract .
(6) " Commercial unit" means such a unit of goods as by commercial
usage is a single whole for purposes of sale and division of which materially
impairs its character or value . It may be a single article or a set of articles
of a quantity or any other unit treated in use or the market as a single whole .

ing crops , and things attached to or forming part of the land which are
agreed to be severed before sale or under the contract of sale." Subsection
(1) states as a base line definition that "goods " are those "things which are
movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale." Thus the USA
couched its definition largely in terms of common law legal concepts ,
whereas this subsection employs language which is much more factual in
content .
Subsection ( 1) makes clear that this definition includes (a) the unborn
young of animals , Maeze v . Bowman , 93 Ky . 205 , 19 S. W. 589 ( 1892 ) , ( b )
growing crops such as fruit , perennial hay , nursery stock , and other seasonal
crops , (c ) those things attached to realty , and (d) specially manufactured
goods .
Among the items excluded from the definition is money where it is
the price to be paid . It is only when money is the medium of payment
that it is excluded , but where money is treated as a commodity , such
as the sale of rare coins , then it is included . This subsection specifically
excludes "investment securities" covered in Article 8 .
Subsection ( 2 ) , in barring present sale or appropriation of unidenti-
fied goods , is substantially the same as KRS 361.170 . See Hutchinson
v . Ford, 72 Ky . ( 9 Bush ) 318, 15 A. R. 711 ( 1872 ) , McCall v . Hampton , 98
Ky. 166, 32 S. W. 406 ( 1896 ) , Wheeler v . Wheeler , 59 Ky . (2 Metc .) 474, 74
Am. Dec. 421 ( 1851 ) . The remaining provisions of the subsection which
draw the distinction between identified and "future " goods and preserve
as a contract the purported present sale of future goods , are substantially
the same as KRS 361.050 ( 1) and ( 3 ) .
Subsections ( 3 ) and ( 4) concerning sales of part interests and
"fungible " goods in substance carry forward the rules of KRS 361.060. See
Ferguson v . Northern Bk . of Ky ., 77 Ky. ( 14 Bush ) 555, 29 A. R. 418 ( 1879 ) .
For general discussion of points involved , see 46 Am. Jur., Sales , Secs .
20 through 31 ; 77 C. J. S. , Sales , Secs . 13 through 15; and Williston , Sales ,
Sec . 136 .
Section 2-106. Definitions . " Contract " ; " Contract for Sale" ;
"Agreement ” ; “Sale” ; “Present Sale ” ; " Conform-
ing " to Contract ; " Termination " ; " Cancellation " .
Summary
(1) In this article unless the context otherwise requires , " contract "
and " agreement" are limited to those relating to the present or future
sale of goods . "Contract for sale" includes both a present sale of goods
and a contract to sell goods at a future time . A "sale " consists of the
passing of title from the seller to the buyer for a price . A " present sale "
is a sale accomplished by the making of the contract .
(2) Goods or conduct are " conforming " when they are in accordance
with the obligations of the contract .

Subsection ( 2) is in general intended to continue the policy of re-

of the customs and usages of the particular trade .
Section 2-107 . Goods To Be Severed From Realty ; Recording .
Summary
(1) A contract for the sale of timber , minerals or the like or a
structure or its materials to be removed from realty is a contract for the
sale of goods under this article if they are to be severed by the seller ,
but until severance a purported present sale thereof which is not effective
as a transfer of an interest in land is effective only as a contract to sell .
(2) A contract for the sale of growing crops or other things not
described in subsection (1) is a contract within this article whether it is to
be severed by buyer or seller , and the parties can by identification effect
a present sale before severance .
(3) The provisions of this section are subject to any third party
rights provided by the law relating to realty records , and the contract
for sale may be executed and recorded as a conveyance of realty and
shall then constitute notice of the buyers rights under the contract for
sale .

sale of goods , under the Code, also include contracts to sell timber , minerals ,
or structures , without regard to the question of severance without material

The provision of this section that contracts for severance may be
recorded as an interest in land is an extension of existing law governing
recording insofar as such interests are not included within the phrase
"sale of real property or any interest therein ." KRS 382.100 . Query if under

Consideration should be given to amending the present recording
act to show the wider coverage given by the Code .
Part 2
FORM , FORMATION AND READJUSTMENT OF CONTRACT
Introductory Comment
The formal requisites for contracts , that are applicable to contracts
generally , are not all fitted to the case of contracts for sale. This is shown
by the fact that common usage in such contracts does not now require
their observance . Under the law the contracts may not be legally binding ,
but the parties thereto consider them to be " at least debts of honor ," and
no doubt ostracize any fellow merchant who fails to abide by the terms
of the contract . The Uniform Commercial Code sanctions many of the
usages now current and makes them legally enforceable . Significant
changes introduced by the Code are discussed in the section on the Statute
of Frauds. Also discussed herein is the making of certain firm offers
binding without consideration .
Section 2-201 . Formal Requirements ; Statute of Frauds .
Summary
(1) Except as otherwise provided , a contract for the sale of goods
of $500 or more is unenforceable by way of action or defense unless there
is a writing sufficient to indicate that a contract has been made and signed
by the party against whom enforcement is sought . The writing is not in-
sufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term but the contract
is not enforceable in excess of the quantity shown in the writing.
(2 ) Between merchants , if a written confirmation sufficient against
the sender is received within a reasonable time and the receiver has reason
to know its contents, it satisfies subsection (1) unless written objection is
given within ten days after receipt .

(a) if the goods are specially manufactured and are not suitable

Kentucky Statutes
Comment
UCC 2-201 in part follows the pattern of the Statute of Frauds pro-
visions of KRS 361.040 of the Uniform Sales Act , in requiring a writing
for the enforcement of sales for $ 500 or more, subject to exceptions in the
event of ( a ) special manufacture , ( b ) receipt , or ( c) payment . However ,
in a number of important respects the Code changes present rules .
(1) The Memorandum . The Code has substantially relaxed the rule
as to what constitutes a sufficient memorandum or note in writing. KRS
361.040 did not specify how complete the memorandum must be, referring
only to a "note or memorandum in writing of the contract of sale." Most
of the Kentucky cases concerning memorandums have been in connection
with sales of land . However the requisites as to a sufficient memorandum
would be the same . Judicial dicta abound to the effect that the writing
must contain all the "essential terms and conditions " of the contract . But
"essential terms and conditions " is itself a term of considerable flexibility
and the courts do not in fact blind themselves by excluding parol testimony
when it is a necessary aid to understanding . Ferguson v . Harris , 200 Ky .
146 , 254 S. W. 329 ( 1923 ) .
Subsection ( 1 ) above , it is believed , carries out the intention that
underlies the first Statute of Frauds . It is more specific than the original
statute; but it makes it clear that informal and incomplete writing is suffi-
cient , even though some of the important terms of the contract are not
expressed therein . The test applied is merely whether the writing indicates
that a contract was made . There are three definite requirements : ( 1) the
written document must "indicate " that a contract for sale of goods has
been made between the parties ; ( 2) it must bear a signature or other
reasonable authentication ; and ( 3) it must contain a statement of quantity
of goods . However many or important may be the other terms on which
the parties have agreed they need not be referred to in the writing . This
includes such important items as price , warranties , security , and terms of
payment and delivery . If the quantity of the goods to be supplied is shown
by the writing, the theory seems to be that the price may be shown by

How much is necessary to "indicate " that a contract has been made
between the parties ? Just as under earlier statutes the parties must be
named, or described , or otherwise so referred to as to aid materially in
their identification . ( See Corbin , Contracts , Sec. 500 ) . The writing must
evidence the fact that a transaction has occurred to which the law can
properly give effect as a contract for the sale of goods . The phrase is " a
contract , " not "the contract " ; and the writing must evidence a contract
"for the sale of goods ," not a contract to hire or to manufacture or to
transport .
Signatures . KRS 446.060 provides :
(1) When the law requires any writing to be signed by a party
thereto it shall not be deemed to be signed unless the signature is sub-
scribed at the end or close of the writing .
(2) Every writing contemplated by the laws of this state shall be
in the English language .
The Code is silent concerning these requirements .
Under the Code , as under present law, signature of an agent is
sufficient . McBrayer v . Cohen , 92 Ky . 479, 18 S. W. 123 ( 1892 ) ; Mallon v .
Buster , 121 Ky . 379 , 89 S. W. 257 ( 1905 ) . " Signed , " as used in the Code ,
includes any authentication which identifies the party to be charged . This
follows the general rule regarding the requirement of the Statute of
Frauds respecting signing written memorandum , that any signature of the
party to be charged made and affixed with intent and purpose of authenti-
cating the memorandum is sufficient regardless of the method or manner
with which it was affixed , whether signed by hand , typewritten , printed
or stamped , or the form in which it appears. See 49 Am. Jur., Statute of
Frauds , Secs . 376-381 .
(2) Special Manufacture for the Buyer . UCC 2-201 ( 3 ) ( a) for the
most part follows the Uniform Sales Act which excepted from the Statute
of Frauds contracts for goods which are "to be" manufactured by the seller
especially for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the
ordinary course of the seller's business. The USA did not specifically pro-
vide that the seller must have actually changed his position under a contract
which called for special manufacture . Under the Code , the agreement is
enforceable only if the seller has made a " substantial beginning " on manu-
facture or has made "commitments for their procurement ."
(3 ) Admissions in Court . The provisions of UCC 2-201 ( 3 ) ( b ) giving
effect to admissions in court is not found in the Sales Act but appears
to be consistent with Kentucky law . Notice that the contract is only
enforceable for the quantity of the goods admitted .

that is to be applicable only in transactions between two merchants . As

defined in UCC 2-104 , " merchant means a person who by his occupation
holds himself out as having knowledge or skill peculiar to the practices
or goods involved in the transaction or in any particular phase of it, or
to whom such knowledge or skill may be attributed by his employment
of an agent or broker or other intermediary having such knowledge or
skill ." In certain transactions a "financing agency" is also declared to
be "a merchant ." Throughout the Code, new rules applicable to "mer-
chants" and to such others as may reasonably be held to be within their
intent , have been enacted largely to give statutory effect to the usages
and customs that have actually developed among such persons . This
subsection takes account of the practice of merchants to send confirmatory
memoranda of bargains made informally by word of mouth , often over
the telephone . Such a written confirmation , if found to be correct is often
filed by the party receiving it without any further communication . Under
the older statute, such a confirmation is a sufficient memorandum to satisfy
the statutory requirements as against its signer . The present subsection
makes it sufficient as against the receiver also unless within ten days he
gives written notice of his objections to its contents . The new rule may
equally well be applied against any person who is not a "merchant " if he
is shown to be familiar with "confirmations " and the usage of either
accepting them as correct or giving prompt notice of objection . This new
rule might be called a contract by acquiescence , because it is a case in
which silence justly is held to cause the confirmation to satisfy the statute
as against the receiver if ( 1) it was sent within a reasonable time after the
transaction , and (2) he had reason to know its contents, and (3) he failed
to give written notice of his objections to it within ten days . It should be
noted that the confirmation is not made conclusive against either party
but is merely evidential as an admission . Mistakes can still be proved if
they exist , just as in the case of writings signed by both parties . The only
effect is to take away from the party who fails to answer the defense of
the Statute of Frauds ; the burden of persuading the trier of fact that a
contract was in fact made orally prior to the written confirmation is un-
affected . Compare the effect of a failure to reply under UCC 2-207 .
(5) Part Performance . Subsection ( 3 ) (c) provides for the upholding
of an oral contract "with respect to goods for which payment has been made
and accepted or which have been received and accepted ." Thus the pro-
vision in the present law that "part payment " or "something in earnest "
would take the entire contract out of the statute is abolished . The contract
is enforceable only to the extent that money or goods have actually been
received and accepted . On the issue of acceptance see Marilyn Shoe Co. v.
Martin's Shoe Store , Ky ., 253 S. W. 2d 18 (1952 ) , where the property was
returned within a few days without being opened or put on display , and
it was held there was no acceptance .

not be contradicted by evidence of prior agreement or of contemporaneous
oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented :
(a) by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of per-
formance ; and
(b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court
finds the writing was intended as the complete and exclusive
statement of the terms of the agreement .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
The parol evidence rule , so called , is an old and well established rule of
law generally accepted in all common law jurisdictions . In respect to pro-
visions covering the application of the "parol evidence rule " to contracts
of sale, where our present act embodied in KRS Ch. 361 is silent , the Code
is outspoken . Briefly the rule may be stated as follows :
When two parties have made a contract and have expressed
it in writing to which they have both assented as the complete
and accurate integration of that contract , evidence, whether parol
or otherwise , of antecedent understandings and negotiations will
not be admitted for the purpose of varying of contradicting the
writing . ( Corbin , Contracts , Sec. 573 )
The rule of the Code that those writings "intended by the parties as a
final expression of their agreement" shall "not be contradicted " appears
consistent with the prevailing concept of "integration ." See Restatement,
Contracts , Secs . 228 and 237 and Kentucky Annotations thereto . See also ,
Stallard v . Adams , 312 Ky . 532, 228 S. W. 2d 430 ( 1950 ) ( parol evidence
is not competent to contradict or vary terms of a written instrument ) ;
Fannin v . Williams , 231 Ky . 392, 21 S. W. 2d 482 ( 1929 ) ; (if document is
incomplete on its face and it is not an integration , parol evidence is ad-
missible ) ; McPherson v . Bacon's Ex'r ., 180 Ky . 773 , 203 S. W. 744 ( 1918 ) ,
(ambiguous and uncertain expressions in contracts may be explained by
the introduction of parol testimony ) .
Many Kentucky decisions adhere to the view that in the absence of
a claim of fraud or mistake , parol evidence is not admissible to contradict ,
vary , add to or take any terms from the contract , when a writing, upon its
face, purports to contain the entire agreement between the contracting
parties . See , Kreitz v . Gallenstein , 170 Ky . 16, 185 S. W. 132 ( 1916 ) ; Kirch-
dorfer v . Watkins , 198 Ky . 69, 248 S. W. 251 ( 1923 ) ; Treas . v . Bank of
Marshall County , 234 Ky . 376, 28 S. W. 2d 43 ( 1930 ) ; Goodin v. Page , 235
Ky. 54, 29 S. W. 2d 581 ( 1930 ) ; Eigelbach v . Roppel , 263 Ky . 604, 92 S. W.
2d 764 ( 1936 ) . For extensive citations see 8A Kentucky Digest 427. How-
ever , it must be pointed out that in these cases the admission of parol
evidence goes to the validity of the contract rather than a construction of
its terms .
33
entries.citation: Uniform Commercial Code: Analysis of Effect on Existing Kentucky Law, 1957, p. 68

It can be explained or supplemented by course of dealing, usage of trade, course of performance, or consistent additional terms, unless the writing is intended as the complete and exclusive statement of the terms.

entries.ocr_text:
The provision of the Code that a memorandum may be "explained "
or " supplemented " by course of dealing or usage of trade may be in con-
flict with Kentucky cases . Kentucky admits evidence of usage to the
following extent : Custom or usage of trade is allowed to explain an
ambiguous or obscure contract . Kendall v. Russell , 35 Ky . ( 5 Dana ) 501 ,
30 Am . Dec. 696 ( 1837 ) ; Thomas v . Charles , 168 Ky . 595, 119 S. W. 752
(1909 ) ; Hawkins v . Midland Flour Milling Co., 236 Ky . 803 , 34 S. W. 2d
439 ( 1930 ) . But a custom cannot enter into a contract , if the parties did
not contract with reference to it. Caldwell v . Dawson , 61 Ky . ( 4 Metc .) 121
(1862 ) ; Castleman v. Southern Mutual Life Insurance Co. , 77 Ky . ( 14 Bush )
197 ( 1878 ) . And evidence of a custom or usage is not allowed to vary
or contradict the terms of a plain , unambiguous contract . National Seed
Co. v . Leavell , 202 Ky . 438, 259 S. W. 1035 ( 1924 ) ; Hawkins v . Midland Flour
Milling Co. , 236 Ky . 803 , 34 S. W. 2d 439 ( 1930 ) ; Huber v . Kerrick , 251 Ky.
439, 65 S. W. 2d 449 ( 1933 ) .
Under paragraph ( b ) consistent additional terms , not reduced to
writing , may be proved unless the court finds that the writing was intended
by both parties as a complete and exclusive statement of all the terms .
The use of parol evidence to "explain " the written agreement is in accord
with past decisions . See Kentucky Annotations to Sec. 238 (a) of the
Restatement , Contracts . The cases seem in conflict on the question whether
the parol evidence may " supplement " the writing. See Kentucky Annota-
tions to Secs . 238, 239 and 240 of the Restatement , Contracts .
Section 2-203 . Seals Inoperative .
Summary
The affixing of a seal to a writing evidencing a contract for sale or
an offer to buy or sell does not make the writing a sealed instrument and
the law as to sealed instruments does not apply to such contract or offer .
KRS 371.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Under the Code , a seal has no effect . KRS 371.010 places sealed and
unsealed instruments on the same footing and attaches absolutely no sig-
nificance as to whether or not an instrument is sealed . Bronston v Lakes ,
135 Ky . 173 , 121 S. W. 1021 ( 1909 ) .
The Code provides other devices reaching some of the results of a
seal . Under UCC 2-205 legal effect is given to a written statement by a
merchant that an offer is firm for a limited period , without regard to con-
sideration or the affixing of a seal . See also UCC 2-209 ( 1) on modification
of contracts without consideration .

Section 2-204. Formation in General .
Summary
(1) A contract for sale may be made in any manner sufficient to
show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the
existence of such a contract .
(2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be
found even though the moment of making is undetermined .
(3) Even though terms are left open a contract does not fall for
indefiniteness if the parties intended to contract and there is a reasonably
certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy .
KRS 361.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Manner of Formation . Accord with subsection ( 1 ) : Uniform
Sales Act Sec . 3, KRS 361.030 (contract for sale "may be made in writing
or by word of mouth ... or may be inferred from the contract of the
parties" ) .
(2) Conduct Recognizing Existence . Accord with subsection ( 2) :
Uniform Sales Act , Sec. 3, KRS 361.030 (contract may be "inferred from
the conduct of the parties " ) ; Kellum v . Browning's Adm'r , 231 Ky . 308 ,
21 S. W. 2d 459 ( 1929 ) ; Restatement, Contracts , Secs . 5 and 21 , and Kentucky
Annotations .
(3) Omitted Terms ; Indefiniteness . See annotation to UCC 2-305 ,
Open Price Term , and UCC 2-311 , Options and Cooperation Respecting Per-
formance . Fowler's Bootery v . Selby Shoe Co., 273 Ky . 670, 117 S. W. 2d
931 ( 1938 ) . ( A promise to sell only the shoes of a certain manufacturer
but which does not include a promise to buy any shoes at all is too indefinite
to be a basis for a contract ) . Compare Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 32 , 33 ,
and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-205 . Firm Offers .
Summary
An offer by a merchant to buy or sell in a signed writing which by
its terms gives assurance that it will be held open is not revocable for lack
of consideration , during the time stated or if no time is stated for a reason-
able time , but in no event may such period of irrevocability exceed three
months; but any such term of assurance on a form supplied by the offeree
must be separately signed by the offeror .

KRS 361.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section giving legal effect for a limited time to a "firm" or
" irrevocable " offer signed by a merchant has no counterpart in the Uniform
Sales Act , and would modify present rules as to "consideration ."
The section obviates the necessity for a consideration to support an
irrevocable offer made by a merchant , as "merchant" is defined in Part 1 ,
when the offer is to remain open for a period of not more than three
months .
Commissioners Comment reveals that the time limit of three months
was inserted in order that the procedure would be limited to ordinary
business transactions ; and to prevent its application in lieu of regular long-
term options . The provision that a clause in a form supplied by the offeree
to the offeror must be separately signed by the offeror was intended as a
safeguard against unconscionable advantage being taken of this section.
Kentucky cases hold that a bare promise to keep an offer open is
without legal effect and the offer may be withdrawn . Noble v. Mann, 32
K. L. R. 30 , 105 S. W. 152 ( 1907 ) ; Stamper v . Combs , 164 Ky . 733 , 176 S. W.
178 ( 1915 ) . See Restatment , Contracts , Secs . 35 (1 ) , 47 and 48 , and Ken-
tucky Annotations thereto .
Section 2-206 . Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract .
Summary
(1) Unless the contrary is clearly indicated :
(a) an offer invites acceptance in any manner and by any medium
reasonable in the circumstances ;
(b) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current
shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by
a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current ship-
ment of conforming a non - conforming goods , but such a
shipment of non - conforming goods does not constitute an
acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the
shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer .
(2 ) Where the beginning of performance is a reasonable mode of
acceptance an offeror may treat the offer as lapsed if not notified within
a reasonable time .
KRS 361.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) (a) Manner and Medium of Acceptance . Subsection ( 1) ( a) in
authorizing acceptance by a medium other than that used by the offeror
is similar to the rule of Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 66 ( acceptance may be

the means used by the offeror "or customary in similar transactions " ) . See
Kentucky Annotations to Sec. 66 of Restatement , Contracts .
(b) Acceptance by Shipment . On acceptance by action rather
than promise , see Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 29 and 63 and Kentucky
Annotations .
(2) Non -conforming Shipments . On the distinction between ship-
ments which purport to be an acceptance and those offered as an accom-
modation see Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 63 ( a tender of performance
requested "operates as a promise to render complete performance " ) , and
Kentucky Annotations .
(3 ) The provision of subsection (3 ) that "the beginning of a requested
performance can be a reasonable mode of acceptance " follows Sec. 45 of
the Restatement , Contracts , with the addition of a requirement of notice
within a reasonable time . Kentucky cases have applied this principle where
there has been a part performance by the offeree . The explanation is that
since part performance creates reciprocal obligations the offer is thereafter
irrevocable and it is further said that both parties are bound to perform .
Louisville and Nashville R. Co. v . Coyle , 123 Ky . 854, 97 S. W. 772, 99 S. W.
237 ( 1906 ) ( an offer to buy all ties delivered at a certain place within the
next twelve months can not be withdrawn after some ties were delivered
and accepted ) ; Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 54 (notice required where the
offeror "has no adequate means of ascertaining " that performance has been
completed ) . Kentucky decisions as to notice requirement are in conflict .
The question usually arises in connection with guaranties of future credits .
In early cases reasonable notice of advances made under a general letter
of credit was required . Kincheloe v . Holmes , 46 Ky . ( 7 B. Mon. ) 5 ( 1846) .
This case resulted in the requirement that there be notice of the intention
to accept. However , other cases held that notice as to specific advances
was not required , the rationale being that a promise to pay on the default
of the principle is an absolute guaranty and no notice of acceptance is
required . See White Sewing Mach . Co. v . Powell, 25 K. L. R. 97 , 74 S. W.
730 ( 1903 ) ; McGowan v . Wells ' Trustee , 184 Ky . 772 , 213 S. W. 573 ( 1919 ) .
See Kentucky Annotations to Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 45, 54 and 56 .
Section 2-207 . Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation .
Summary
( 1 ) A definite and seasonable expression of acceptance or a written
confirmation which is sent within a reasonable time operates as an accept-
ance even though it states terms additional to or different from those
offered or agreed upon , unless acceptance is expressly made conditional
on assent to the additional or different terms .
(2) The additional terms are to be construed as proposals for addition
to the contract . Between merchants such terms become part of the con-
tract unless :

(a) the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the
offer ;
(b) they materially alter it ; or
(c) notification of objection to them has already been given or is
given within a reasonable time after notice of them is received .
(3) Conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of a con-
tract is sufficient to establish a contract for sale although the writings of
the parties do not otherwise establish a contract . In such cases the terms
of the particular contract consist of those terms on which the writings of
the parties agree, together with any supplementary terms incorporated
under any other provisions of this act. This section is an effort to deal with
the situation . The basic rule is that a definite and seasonable expression of
acceptance or a written confirmation sent within a reasonable time operates
as an acceptance even though it contains terms additional to those offered
as previously agreed upon unless acceptance is expressly made conditional
on assent to the additional or different terms . Between merchants , the
additional terms become part of the contract unless the offer expressly
limits acceptance to the terms of the offer ; they materially alter it, or ob-
jection to them has been given or is given within a reasonable time after
notice of them is received .
(4) Contract by Conduct of Parties . Even though the writings of the
parties do not establish a contract , if the parties recognize a contract
between them , then a contract will be established . The terms of the con-
tract will be those agreed upon by the parties , whether in writing or supple-
mentary terms incorporated under other provisions of this Act .
Comment
( 1 ) Additional or Different Terms . Subsection ( 1) of the Code ap-
pears to have changed the orthodox rule that the acceptance of an offer
must be absolute and identical with the offer . See Restatement , Contracts ,
Sec. 59 and Kentucky Annotations . Under the Code the inclusion of
additional or different terms does not serve to destroy the efficacy of a
definite and reasonable expression of acceptance . Subsection ( 2 ) provides
that the additional terms are proposals for additions to the contract . See ,
Restatement, Contracts , Secs . 60 and 62 and Kentucky Annotations . Such
additional or different terms become part of the agreement if they are not
expressly excepted, or do not materially vary the terms of the offer or
reasonable obections are not seasonably made .
(2) The intent of UCC 2-207 is to meet the situation where a part
of the negotiations of the parties consisted of an exchange of business
forms . The old rule that acceptance of an offer had to conform to the
terms of the offer before it would be effective meant that almost any
variance between the terms of the offer and purported acceptance would
render the latter ineffective to form a contract . This rule never worked
too satisfactorily . Quite often a form intended as an acceptance or con-
firmation of a previously established agreement contains certain printed
provisions which are at variance with the terms of a printed form offer .
Everyday experience tells us that often the parties proceed with the agree-
ment notwithstanding these differences and the performance produces an
