Section 2-208. Course of Performance or Practical Construction .
Summary
(1) Where the contract involves repeated occasions for performance
by either party and the other party has knowledge and opportunity to
object , any course of performance accepted or acquiesced in without objec-
tion shall be relevant to determine meaning of the agreement or to show
waiver or modification of any term inconsistent with such course .
(2) The express terms of the agreement and any such course of
performance , as well as any course of dealing and usage of trade , shall be
construed whenever reasonable as consistent with each other ; but when
such construction is unreasonable , express terms shall control course of
performance and course of performance shall control both course of
dealing and usage of trade (UCC 1-205 ) .
(3) Subject to the provisions of the next section on modification
and waiver , such course of performance shall be relevant to show a waiver
or modification of any term inconsistent with such course of performance .
Kentucky Statutes
No such general provision but concept of this section recognized
by terms such as " course of dealing," " the circumstances of the case,"
"the conduct of the parties ," etc. , in Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361 .
Comment
On rendering the failure to object to a course of performance
"relevant ," but not controlling , to determine the meaning of the agree-
ment , see , accord , KRS 361.030 ( contract "may be inferred from the con-
duct of the parties " ) ; KRS 361.710 (implied terms may be negatived or
varied "by the course of dealing " ) ; Myers v . Land , 314 Ky . 514, 235 S. W.
2d 988 ( 1950 ) ; compare Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 72 , 235 ( e) and Ken-
tucky Annotations . As to "waiver " by failure to object , see , accord, KRS
361.490 ( failure to give notice of breach of warranty ) ; Restatement ,
Contracts , Sec. 298, and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-209. Modification , Recission and Waiver .
Summary
(1) An agreement modifying a contract within this article needs no
consideration .
(2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or recission
except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded ,

(3 ) The requirements of the Statute of Frauds ( UCC 2-201 ) must be
satisfied if the modified contract is within its provisions .
(4) An attempt at modification or recission can operate as a waiver
even though it does not satisfy subsections ( 2) or ( 3 ) .
(5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion
of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received
by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term
waived , unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change
of position in reliance on the waiver .

None.
Comment
The Code presents innovations on the subject of modification , re-
cission and waiver . An agreement to modify a contract is in itself a
contract , hence , under present law it needs a supporting consideration as
much as any other contract . Subsection ( 1) abolishes the requirement
of consideration in agreements modifying sale contracts and the mere
agreement to modify is sufficient , unless the original signed agreement
excludes modification or recission except by another signed writing ,
(subsection 2 ) . Whether a modification of a contract is effective under
the established doctrines of consideration is often a very difficult problem.
See Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 76, 88-90 , and Kentucky Annotations .
Subsection ( 2) also contains a special safeguard when applied between
merchant and nonmerchant . It is provided that when a nonmerchant
subscribes to a contract containing a clause requiring a signed writing
for an effective modification or recission on a form supplied by a merchant ,
a nonmerchant must separately sign the clause in order to make it enforce-
able .
It appears that subsection (2) would overrule Kentucky cases per-
mitting the modification of a written contract by an oral agreement , in
cases where a signed agreement excludes oral modification . Illinois Cent .
R. R. Co. v . Manion , 113 Ky. 7, 67 S. W. 40 ( 1902 ) ; Continental Ins . Co. of
New York v . Simpson , 220 Ky . 167, 294 S. W. 1048 ( 1927 ) ; see Restatement ,
Contracts , Sec. 407 , and Kentucky Annotations . Under subsections (4) and
(5) , an ineffective attempt at modification can operate as a waiver , but
the waiver can be withdrawn if it has not been acted on. Accord , Restate-
ment, Contracts , Secs . 88 (2) , 297, and Kentucky Annotations .
The rule of subsection (3) that the requirements of the Statute of
Frauds must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions
is substantially the same as that of Secs. 222-224 of the Restatement,
Contracts , Kentucky Annotations to the sections show that no cases were
found . Corbin , in his treatise on Contracts states that it is a fair general

Section 2-210 . Delegation of Performance ; Assignment of Rights .
Summary
(1) A party may perform through a delegate unless otherwise agreed
or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having the original
promisor perform or control the performance . No delegation relieves a
party of any duty to perform or any liability for breach .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed all rights of either seller or buyer can
be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the
duty of the other party , or increase materially the burden or risk imposed
on him by his contract , or impair materially his chance of obtaining return
performance . A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a
right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation
can be assigned despite agreement otherwise .
(3) Unless the circumstances indicate the contrary , a prohibition of
assignment of "the contract " bars only the delegation of the assignor's
performance .
(4) An assignment of "the contract" or of "all my rights under the
contract " or in similar general terms is an assignment of rights and unless
the language or the circumstances indicate the contrary , it is a delegation
of performance of the assignor's duties and its acceptance by the assignee
is his promise to perform . Either the assignor or other party to the contract
may enforce this promise .
(5) The other party may treat an assignment of performance as
creating grounds for insecurity and may without prejudice to his rights
against the assignor demand assurances from the assignee .

accord with Sec. 151 of the Restatement , Contracts . See Kentucky Annota-
tions , citing cases in accord .

classification.title: Section 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights.
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (1957) outlines rules regarding the delegation of contractual performance and the assignment of contractual rights. It states that a party can delegate performance unless the other party has a substantial interest in the original promisor performing. No delegation relieves the original party of their duties or liabilities. It also details conditions under which rights can be assigned, generally allowing assignment unless it materially changes the other party's duty, burden, risk, or chance of return performance. Specific clauses address prohibitions on assignment and general assignments of 'the contract'.

(3) Assignment of Contract as Delegation of Performance ; Inter-
pretation . The rule of subsection ( 4) that, unless the circumstances indicate
to the contrary , an assignment of the contract not only assigns rights but
delegates duties , is comparable to that of Sec. 164 (1 ) of the Restatement,
Contracts . See Kentucky Annotations . Subsection (4) is also comparable
to the rule of Sec . 164 (2) of the Restatement , Contracts , that acceptance
of the assignment is interpreted " as a promise to the assignor to assume the
performance of the assignor's duties ." The Code , however , goes beyond
the rule of the restatement by providing the promise is enforceable by
both the assignor and the other party . See 32 Ky. L. J. 216 .
(4) Right to Assurances from Assignee . Subsection ( 5) providing for

Part 3
GENERAL OBLIGATIONS AND CONSTRUCTION OF CONTRACT
Introductory Comment
This part states the general obligations of the parties to the sale
in terms of the provisions of the contract . Since the policy of the Code
is to emphasize the terms of the contract and de-emphasize the title
concept, the rules of construction of the contract are set out with particu-
larity . In addition to general rules , specific rules are given for many
commonly used provisions in order that uniformity may be achieved . The
provisions of this part permit the court to refuse to enforce all or any part
of an "unconscionable " contract . This appears to be a new theory in the
law of sales .
Section 2-301 . General Obligations of Parties .
Summary
The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the
buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.110 , 361.410 .
Comment
UCC 2-301 in substance reiterates Sec . 41 of the Uniform Sales Act ,
KRS 361.410, in stating that the primary obligation of both parties is per-
formance in accordance with the contract . The term "obligation " is used
so as to include the conditional aspects of delivery and payment . The Code
throughout increases the emphasis placed on the contract in sales trans-
actions .

Section 2-302 . Unconscionable Contract or Clause .
Summary
(1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause
of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made , the
court may refuse to enforce the contract or it may enforce the remainder
of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the
application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable
result.
(2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or
any clause thereof may be unconscionable , the parties shall be afforded
a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting ,
purpose , and effect to aid the court in making the determination .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section states a theory new to sales law. The section appears
to be intended to carry equity practice into the sales field. See Restatement ,
Contracts , Sec . 367 , and Kentucky Annotations . The Commissioners Com-
ment , citing cases from other states , states that the results intended under
this section have been accomplished by indirection by such means as
interpretation of language . Compare Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 236 and
Kentucky Annotations . For general discussion see Corbin , Contracts , Secs .
128 and 1164 .
Subsection ( 2) is addressed to the court . It states clearly that parties
have a right to a reasonable opportunity to present evidence going to the
question of unconscionability . Under the section , the court , in its discretion ,
may refuse to enforce the contract as a whole if it is permeated by
unconscionability or it may strike any single clause or group of clauses
which are so tainted or which are contrary to the essential purpose of
the agreement .
Section 2-303 . Allocation or Division of Risks .
Summary
Where this article allocates a risk or a burden as between the parties
"unless otherwise agreed ," the agreement may not only shift the allocation
but may also divide the risk or burden .

None.

Section 2-304 . Price Payable in Money , Goods , Realty , or
Otherwise .
Summary
( 1 ) The price may be payable in money or otherwise , and if payable
in goods , the party obligated to deliver goods is a seller with respect to
those goods .
(2 ) When the price is paid by an interest in realty , the seller's obli-
gations are covered by this act, but the transfer of the interest in realty
and the transferor's obligations in connection therewith are not .
Kentucky Statutes
Subsections (2) and (3) of KRS 361.090 .
Comment
(1) The provision that the price can be paid "in money or other-
wise " broadens the coverage of this article beyond that of Sec. 9 ( 2) of
the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.090 , under which the price "may be made
payable in any personal property ." The effect of this change is seen most
clearly under subsection ( 2 ) , below .
(2) Under subsection ( 2 ) , if realty is to be exchanged for goods , the
Code fixes the obligations of the seller with respect to the goods , although
not the realty . This constitutes an expansion beyond the scope of the
Uniform Sales Act ; under Sec. 9 (3 ) , KRS 361.090 (3) , the present act is
inapplicable if " any interest in real estate constitutes the whole or part
of the consideration " for a sale .
Section 2-305 . Open Price Term .
Summary
(1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a sale even though
the price is not settled . In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the
time for delivery if:
(a) nothing is said as to price ; or
(b) the price is left to be agreed on by the parties and they fail
to agree; or
(c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or
standard set by a third person or agency and it is not so set .
(2) A price to be fixed by one of the parties is a price to be fixed
by him in good faith .

(3) When a price which is to be fixed otherwise than by agreement
of the parties fails to be so fixed through the fault of one party , the other
may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable
price.
(4) Where the parties do not intend to be bound unless the price
is a fixed or agreed price and the price is not fixed or agreed then there
is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods not used,
the reasonable value of any goods already used, and the seller must return
any portion of the price paid on account.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.090 , 361.100 .
Comment
The rule under this section that the parties can bind themselves to
a contract for sale without settling the price "if they so intend ," resolves
an ambiguity between Secs . 9 ( 1) and 9 (4) of the Uniform Sales Act,
KRS 361.090 . See Gaines & Sea v . Reynolds Tobacco Co., 163 Ky . 716, 174
S. W. 482 ( 1915 ) , (a price at original cost, plus cost of handling and a " nice"
or reasonable profit was held too indefinite for enforcement ) . Compare
Jenkins v . Richardson , 29 Kу . ( 6 J. J. Marsh ) 441 , 22 Am . Dec. 82 ( 1831 ) ,
wherein it was held that where no price is expressly agreed on by parties
or where seller has not put a specific price on goods when he sends them
to the buyer , it is well settled at common law that the price is to be
determined ordinarily by the market value or reasonable value of the
goods . Compare the cases supra under UCC 2-201 where memorandum
does not state the price . It thus appears that this section of the Code will
probably modify present law to render binding agreements where no price
is stated. See Restatement, Contracts , Sec. 32 and Kentucky Annotations .
(See particularly illustrations 9 and 10.) The buyer may not keep the
goods without compensating the seller for them . Such was the result
under Sec. 9 (4) of the Uniform Sales Act , although no price was stated .
Under this section the parties , if they so intend , may conclude a contract
without settling on the question of price . This would leave the price to be
determined ultimately by the court , by its findings as to what would con-
stitute a reasonable price in the event of disagreement or inability to apply
a standard .
For general discussion on points raised by this section see Corbin ,
Contracts , Secs . 97 through 99. Also 46 Am . Jur., Sales , Secs . 179 and 180 .

(2 ) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive
dealing in the kind of goods concerned, imposes unless otherwise agreed an
obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the
buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
The Code deals with contracts which are based on the "output " of the
seller or on the "requirements " of the buyer . The Uniform Sales Act em-
bodied in KRS Ch . 361 did not deal with this question .
Requirement and output contracts are enforceable in Kentucky. Ross-
Vaughan Tobacco Co. v . Johnson , 182 Ky . 325, 206 S.W. 487 ( 1918 ) . See
Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 32 , Illustration 12 , and Kentucky Annotations .
2 Williston , Sales , Sec. 464A et seq . , 1 Corbin , Contracts , Sec. 156 et seq; 77
C.J.S. , Sales, Sec. 171 and 172. See also Mitchell - Taylor Tie Co. v . Whitaker ,
158 Ky . 651 , 166 S.W. 193 ( 1914) (the number of ties a seller could make
within a year is not too indefinite ) ; Ayer & Lord Tie Co. v . Ο . Τ . Ο'Βαnnor
& Co. , 164 Ky. 34, 174 S.W. 783 ( 1915 ) (all ties vendor could deliver before
a certain date is not too uncertain ) . But compare Springton Coal Co. v .
Bowling , 228 Ky. 317, 14 S.W. 2d 1082 ( 1929 ) , where an output contract
covering all the coal mined was held invalid for lack of mutuality on the
basis that there was no obligation to deliver . ( This decision being against
the decided weight of authority ) .
The Code provision that "output " or " requirement " means "such actual
output or requirements as may occur in good faith " finds support in Ken-
tucky Tobacco Products Co. v. Lucas , 5 F. 2d 723 ( W.D. Ky .) 1925. See
also 1 Williston, Contracts , Sec. 104A .
Subsection ( 1) states that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to
either a stated estimate or otherwise comparable prior output and require-
ments may be tendered or demanded . It is probable that this part of the
subsection is simply a restatement of the good faith test and not an addi-
tional limitation upon quantitative variations . The comments point out that
variations , even to the point of cessation of requirements , are permitted so
long as in good faith and not for the purpose of avoiding the contract . Thus
in a fixed -price requirements contract , the requirements will perhaps
dwindle to nothing as the market price sinks below the contract price since ,
presumably , the buyer will be forced from the market by lower priced com-
petition . This is not a breach by the buyer , but he is not permitted to shut
down just to avoid the loss which will accrue because of his purchases at a
price above the market . The problem is one of determining what is "un-
reasonably disproportionate ." If this means that any variation which is not
attributable to bad faith is permissible , then the assertion that the section
is a restatement of the good faith test is correct . If it means that under no

Section 2-307 . Delivery in Single Lot or Several Lots .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed , all goods called for by a contract for sale must
be tendered in a single lot and payment is due only on such tender , but
where the circumstances give either party the right to make or demand de-
livery in several lots , the price may be apportioned and demanded for each
lot.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.450 ( 1) , 361.420 .
Comment
(1) Presumption of Single Delivery . The presumption that the goods
shall be tendered in a single delivery carries forward the same rule laid
down in Sec. 45 ( 1) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.450 ( 1) .
(2) Presumption of Separate Payment for Each Lot . The presumption
that where installment delivery is permitted , the buyer shall pay for each
lot separately , applies to this particular situation the general rule of con-
current payment and delivery set forth in Sec. 42 of the Uniform Sales Act ,
KRS 361.420 . Cf. UCC 2-511 ( 1) . See Gilmore & Co. v . W. B. Samuels & Co. ,
135 Ky . 706, 123 S.W. 271 ( 1909 ) ; Collins v . Swan -Day Lumber Co. , 158 Ky .
231 , 164 S.W. 813 ( 1914 ) . The qualification upon this rule that the price be
apportionable was not expressly stated under the Uniform Sales Act , but is
consistent with it, since inability to apportion the price would bear upon
the intent of the parties as to separate payment .

Subsections ( a) and (b) state the same presumption against placing on
the seller the obligation to deliver as was set forth in Sec . 43 ( 1) of the

Subsection ( c) provides that delivery of documents of title may be
made through customary banking channels . The Code comment indicates
that notification to the buyer by the bank is all that is necessary , thus
eliminating the necessity of a tender at the buyer's address . No cases have
been found applying such a rule in Kentucky , but it is unquestionably the
rule by business custom .

(1) The time for shipment or delivery if not specified by this article
or by agreement of the parties , shall be a reasonable time .
(2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is
indefinite in duration , the contract is valid for a reasonable time but unless
otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party .
(3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening
of an agreed event requires that reasonable notice shall be given the other
party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its opera-
tion would be unconscionable .

(2) This subsection would substitute a single rule of duration of “a

(3) Notice of Termination . Subsection ( 3) follows the rule that revo-
cation of an offer must be received by the offeree to be effective . In all
agreements indefinite as to duration , notice of election to terminate is re-
quired , at least after either party has begun performance . Peters Branch of
International Shoe Co. v. Jones , 247 Ky. 193 , 56 S.W. 2d 994 ( 1933 ) ; Elkhorn
Consol . Coal & Coke Co. v. Eaton , Rhodes & Co. 163 Ky . 306, 173 S.W. 798
(1915); Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 41 , 44 , and Kentucky Annotations .
But the provision invalidating "unconscionable " agreements dispensing with
notification changes the law . Compare UCC 1-102 , 1-204 , 2-302 .
Section 2-310 . Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit ;
Authority to Ship Under Reservation .
Unless otherwise agreed :
Summary
(a) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to
receive the goods even though the shipment is the place of
delivery ; and
(b) if the seller is authorized to send the goods , he may ship them
under reservation and may tender the documents , but the
buyer may inspect the goods before payment is due unless
such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract
(UCC 2-513 ) ; and
(c) if delivery is authorized by way of documents otherwise than
by subsection (b) , then payment is due at the place at which
buyer is to receive the documents regardless of where the
goods are received ; and
(d) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods
on credit , the credit period will run from the time of shipment
unless the invoice is post-dated or delayed , in which case the
commencement of the credit period will be correspondingly
delayed .

Summary
(1) An agreement for sale which is otherwise sufficiently definite is

Section 2-312 . Warranty of Title and Against Infringement ;

Summary
Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty
by the seller that there is :
(a) good title and rightful transfer ; and
(b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest
or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time
of contracting has no knowledge .
(2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified
only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason
to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is
purporting to sell only such rights or title as he or a third person may have .
(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed , a seller who is a merchant regularly deal-
ing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of
the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like ,
but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller
harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the
specifications .

able to convey title.

Section 2-313 . Express Warranties by Affirmation , Promise ,

(1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows :
( a) any affirmation of fact or promise in relation to the goods

(b) any description which is made part of the basis of the bargain

conform .
(2) It is not necessary that formal words of warranty be used to
create the warranty or that the seller have specific intention to create a
warranty but an affirmation merely of value or a statement purporting to
be merely the seller's opinion or commendation does not create a warranty .

Summary
(1) Unless excluded or modified , a warranty of merchantability will
be implied in every contract for sale where the seller is a merchant . Under
this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on
the premises or elsewhere is a sale .
(2 ) Merchantable goods are those which at least :
(a) pass without objection in the trade as described in the con-

(b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality ; and

used; and
(d) are of even run in quality and quantity ; and
(e) are adequately packaged and labeled ; and
(f) conform to the promises made on the label , if any .
(3) Unless excluded or modified , other warranties may arise from
course of dealing or usage of trade .

For decisions applying the warranty of merchantable quality see : Porter
v . Craddock , 84 F. Supp . 704 ( W.D. Ky . 1949 ) ; Finck v . Albers Super Markets ,
136 F. 2d 191 (6th Cir. 1943 ) ; Martin v . Great A. & P. Tea Co. , 301 Ky . 429,
192 S.W. 2d 201 ( 1945 ) ; Nehi Beverage Co. v . Hall , 295 Ky . 353, 174 S.W. 2d

(1933 ); Great A. & P. Tea Co. v . Eiseman , 259 Ky . 103 , 81 S.W. 2d 900 ( 1935 ) .
(b) Serving of Food or Drink . The Uniform Sales Act did not specify
whether serving of food or drink would be a "sale" within its scope . The
Code adopts the majority view by declaring the serving of food or drink as
a " sale ." See Anno ., 7 A.L.R. 2d 1027 ( 1949 ) .
(2) Definition of Merchantable Quality . The Uniform Sales Act , unlike
the Code , does not establish tests to be used in determining whether goods
are of "merchantable quality ." Neither have the Kentucky cases expressly
laid down a series of tests to be applied in determining whether goods are
merchantable . But the tests of the Code do not seem to make any change
in the conception of merchantability as that term is used in the cases cited
above. The tests of subsection (2) ( b ) and ( c) which are in terms of "fair
average quality " and " ordinary purposes " are consistent with the majority
view which denies liability under an implied warranty where injury is the
result of the buyer's individual peculiarity .
(3) Warranty by Usage of Trade . Subsection ( 3) is comparable to
Sec. 15 ( 5) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.150 ( 5 ) . See , North American
Fertilizer Co. v . Combs , 307 Ky. 869 , 212 S.W. 2d 526 ( 1948 ) .
Section 2-315 . Implied Warranty ; Fitness for Particular Purpose .
Summary
Where the seller has reason to know that the goods are required for
a particular purpose and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or
judgment , there is, unless excluded or modified , an implied warranty that
the goods are fit for such purpose .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.150 (1) , (4) and (5) .

This section roughly approximates Sec. 15 ( 2) of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.150 (2 ) . The Code is somewhat broader than the present
statute in making clear that a warranty may arise if the buyer relies on
the seller either "to select" or " to furnish " suitable goods . Under the Code a
warranty is also imposed where the seller has "reason to know " a buyer's

The warranty of fitness of purpose under the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.150 , has had broad application in the decisions . See, Great A. & P. Tea
Co. v . Eiseman , 259 Ку . 103, 81 S.W. 2d 869 ( 1935 ) ( grocer held to warranty
that chicken he selected was fit for human consumption ) ; Louisville Grind-
ing & Machine Co. v . Southern Oil and Tar Co. , 230 Ky . 39, 188 S.W. 2d 877
(1929 ) (where seller impliedly warranted oil he selected would be reason-
ably suitable for the purpose of hardening a race track ) ; Day Pulverizer Co.
v . Rutledge , 238 Ky . 817, 38 S.W. 2d 949 ( 1931 ) ( where dealer recommended
particular type rock crusher ) ; McBride v . Farmers Seed Ass'n , 248 Ky . 514,
58 S.W. 2d 909 (1933) (sale of chicken feed ) ; Snead v. Waite , 306 Ky . 587,
208 S.W. 2d 749 ( 1948 ) ; Martin v . Great A. & P. Tea Co., 301 Ky . 429 , 192
S.W. 2d 201 ( 1945) ( retailer liable for defect in commodity sold in sealed
container ) ; Frick Co. v . Wiley , 290 Ky . 665, 162 S.W. 2d 190 ( 1942 ) ; R. B.
Tyler Co. v . Hampton Cracker Co. , 265 Ky . 236, 96 S.W. 2d 593 ( 1936 ) ( no
warranty where buyer orders according to specifications ) .
Sales under Patent or Trade Name . The Code contains no provision
comparable to Sec . 15 (4) of the niform Sales Act, KRS 361.150 (4) , under
which the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose is made in-
applicable to sales " of a specified article under its patent or other trade
name ." The Kentucky cases have frequently held the implied warranty of
fitness for a particular purpose inapplicable to a sale under a patent or
trade name by virtue of this section. R. B. Tyler Co. v . Hampton Cracker
Co. , 265 Ky . 236 , 96 S.W. 2d 593 ( 1936 ) ; Halterman v . Louisville Bridge &
Iron Co. , Ky . 254 S.W. 2d 493 ( 1953 ) .
The omission of the trade name qualification from the Code makes it
clear that a sale under a patent or a trade name is merely one factor to be
considered on the question of whether the buyer actually relied on the
seller . This omission , however , seems to clarify the present law rather than
change it, since that factor has not been held to be decisive where other
factors show that the buyer relied on the seller , even though the seller
recommended an article with a trade name . Halterman v . Louisville Bridge
& Iron Co. , Ky . 254 S.W. 2d 493 ( 1953 ) .

visions of this artcle on parol or extrinsic evidence ( UCC 2-202 ) negation or
limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable .

(a) exact or technical specifications displace inconsistent sample

On cumulation of warranties see KRS 361.140 , 361.150 , and 361.160 .
Comment
This section incorporates the narrower rule of Sec. 15 (6) of the Uni-
form Sales Act , KRS 361.150 ( 6) , to the effect that an express warranty does
not negative an implied warranty unless inconsistent therewith . See anno-
tation to UCC 2-316 ( 2 ) and cases there cited, supra .
In addition , the Code establishes a set of presumptions for determining
the parties ' intention with respect to which warranty is dominant in the
situation where to construe the warranties as consistent and cumulative
would be unreasonable . These presumptions for reconciling different types
of warranties have no counterpart in the present statute.
However , the emphasis of the section upon construing warranties as
cumulative and of subsection ( a) on exact specifications displacing general
language of description is consistent with the general contract principles re-
ferred to in the annotation to UCC 2-316 ( 1 ) , supra .
Section 2-318 . Third Party Beneficiaries of Warranties Express
or Implied .
Summary
An express or implied warranty extends to any member of the buyer's
family or household or to his guest if it is reasonable to expect such person
may use or consume the goods and is injured in person by the breach . A
seller may not exclude or limit the operation of this section .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Uniform Sales Act did not specify whether the seller's warranty
obligations ran to persons other than the immediate buyer . In Snead v.
Waite , 306 Ky. 587, 208 S.W. 2d 749 ( 1948 ) ; a retailer of barbecued meat ,
who cut and wrapped meat in buyer's presence and delivered it to buyer for
home consumption , impliedly warranted that the meat was wholesome , and
was liable in damages for illness of buyer and his family arising from
tainted condition of meat. The prohibition against limiting by contract the
responsibility to third persons is new .

Section 2-320 . C.I.F. and C. & F. Terms .
Summary
(1) C.I.F. means the price includes costs of goods , insurance and
freight to named destination . C. & F. or C.F. means the price includes cost
and freight to named destination .
(2 ) Unless otherwise agreed and even though used in connection with
a price , the term C.I.F. destination requires the seller at his risk and ex-
pense to:
(a) put the goods in carrier's possession for shipment and obtain
negotiable bill of lading covering entire transportation to
destination ; and
(b) load the goods and obtain a receipt showing freight paid ; and
(c) obtain insurance , including war risk insurance , of a kind and
upon terms then current at port of shipment in the usual
amount , in the currency of the contract , shown to cover the
goods covered by the bill of lading , with payment to buyer's
order or to whom it may concern ; the seller may add the war
risk premium to the price ; and
(d) prepare invoice of goods and any other documents required
for shipment or to comply with the contract ; and
(e) forward and tender with commercial promptness all the
documents in due form and with any indorsement necessary to
perfect the buyer's rights .
(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed, the term C. & F. or equivalent has the
same effect and imposes the same obligations as C.I.F. except as to insur-
ance .
(4) Unless otherwise agreed, under both C.I.F. or C. & F. payment
must be made against tender of the documents and the seller cannot tender ,
nor the buyer demand delivery of the goods in lieu of documents .

Comment
(1) Definitions . The Uniform Sales Act does not specify the effect to
be given these shipping terms . The Code's definition of C.I.F. states com-
mercial understanding that the price quoted by the seller under this term
includes the cost of the goods , the insurance and the freight . See 2 Willis-
ton , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280c ; American Foreign Trade Definitions ( Chamber
of Commerce , 1941 ) V.
The Code also states commercial understanding in defining C. & F. as
meaning that the price quoted includes cost and freight to the named
desination . See 2 Williston, Sales (1948 ) Sec. 280c ; American Foreign Trade
Definitions ( Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) IV.
(2 ) Seller's Obligations and Risks under C.I.F. Contract . The detailed
obligations listed in subsection (2) reflect commercial understanding . See
2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280c ; American Foregin Trade Definitions
(Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) IV.
The Code , in placing the risk of loss in transit on the buyer , is also
consistent with Commercial understanding . See 2 Williston, Sales ( 1948 )
Sec. 280c ; American Foreign Trade Definitions ( Chamber of Commerce ,
1941) commercial understanding is sufficient to override the presump-
tion of Sec . 19 (Rule 5) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.190 ( 5 ) , arising
from the seller's responsibility for freight . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec .
280d . The Code is also consistent with KRS 361.220 which makes provision as
to parties otherwise agreeing as to the seller's obligations and risk .
(3 ) Seller's Obligations and Risks under C. & F. Contract . Here again
the Code is in line with mercantile understanding , American Foreign Trade
Definitions ( Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) IV , including the shifting of the
risk of loss in transit to the buyer in spite of the fact that the seller pays
the freight . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280h .
(4) Payments Against , and Tender of , Documents Rather Than Goods .
In requiring the buyer to make payment against documents, without await-
ing the arrival of the goods , and the seller to tender documents , and not the
goods, the Code again adopts business usage . See 2 Williston, Sales ( 1948 )
Sec. 280c .

(2) Under subsection ( 2) , an agreement whereby the price is based

Section 2-324 . “ No Arrival , No Sale ” Term .
Summary
Under a term " no arrival, no sale" or terms of like meaning unless
otherwise agreed :
(a) The seller must ship conforming goods and if they arrive he
must tender them but he assumes no obligation that they will
arrive unless he has caused the nonarrival ; and
(b) where without fault of the seller the goods are in part lost , or
deteriorated or arrive after the contract time , the buyer may
proceed as if there had been casualty to identified goods .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
The Uniform Sales Act did not deal with this term . When " no arrival,
no sale" or a similar term is used , the Code obligates the seller to ship the
goods and to tender them ; it gives the buyer the choice of accepting or re-
jecting the goods that arrive if there is a casualty in transit , but relieves
the seller of liability . These provisions seem in accord with the cases out-
side of Kentucky . See 1 Williston, Sales ( 1948 ) Secs . 188, 188a , 188b . In
giving the buyer the unlimited option to accept the goods that arrive , the
Code may be changing the present law . See 1 Williston, Sales ( 1948 ) Sec .
188b ( seller is not obligated to deliver the portion that arrives ) .

If goods are lost, deteriorated, or arrive late without the seller's fault, the buyer can treat it as a casualty to identified goods.

Section 2-325 . " Letter of Credit " Term ; " Confirmed Credit ."
Summary
(1) Failure of buyer to furnish an agreed letter of credit is a breach
of the sale contract .
(2) The delivery to seller of a proper letter of credit suspends the
buyer's obligation to pay . If the letter of credit is dishonored , the seller may
on seasonable notification to the buyer require payment directly from him .
(3) "Letter of credit " or "banker's credit " in a contract means an ir-
revocable credit issued by a financing agency of good repute , and , if an
overseas shipment is involved , of good international repute . "Confirmed
credit " means that the credit carries the direct obligation of such an agency
doing business in the seller's financial market .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Irrevocable Letter of Credit Required . Subsection (3) appears in accord
with the weight of authority outside Kentucky . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 )
Sec. 469 (e) . But cf. Customs and Practices for Commercial Documentary
Credits (International Chamber of Commerce 1938) Art. 3. (This incon-
sistent rule favoring revocability may however be construed as relating to
relationship between customer and bank .)
The definition of "confirmed credit " in subsection (3 ) is also in line
with banking practice . See 2 Williston , Sales (1948 ) Sec. 469d .
Section 2-326 . Sale on Approval and Sale or Return , Consignment
Sales and Rights of Creditors .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed, if delivered goods may be returned by
the buyer even though they conform to the contract , the transaction is :
(a) a"sale on approval " if the goods are delivered primarily for
use, and
(b) a "sale or return " if the goods are delivered primarily for
resale .
(2) Except as provided in subsection ( 3) , goods held on approval are
not subject to the claims of the buyer's creditors until acceptance ; goods
held on sale or return are subject to such claims while in the buyer's pos-
session .
(3) Where goods are delivered to a person for sale and such person
maintains a place of business at which he deals in goods of the kind involved ,
under a name other than the name of the person making delivery , then with

respect to claims of creditors of the person conducting the business the goods
are deemed to be on sale or return . The provisions of this subsection are ap-
plicable even though an agreement purports to reserve title in the person
making delivery until payment or resale or uses such words as " on consign-
ment" or " on memorandum . " However , this subsection is not applicable if
the person making delivery :
(a) complies with an applicable law providing for a consignor's
interest or the like to be evidenced by a sign , or
(b) establishes that the person conducting the business is gen-
erally known by his creditors to be substantially engaged in
selling the goods of others , or
(c) complies with the filing provisions of the article on Secured
Transactions (Article 9 ) .
(4) Any " or return " term of a contract for sale is to be treated as a

( UCC 2-20 ) and as contradicting the sale aspect of the contract within the
provisions of this article on parol or extrinsic evidence ( UCC 2-202 ) .
KRS 361.190 ( 3 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Transactions Distinguished . The distinction between a " sale or
return " and a " sale on approval " depends on whether goods are delivered for
use or for resale, rather than on the title test of the Uniform Sales Act (Sec .
19 , Rules 3 ( 1) and ( 2) , KRS 361.190 ( 3 ) ( 1) and ( 2 ) . For Kentucky deci-
sions see : Stratton & Terstegge Co. v . Criswell, 290 Ky . 64, 160 S. W. 2d
137 ( 1942 ) ; Porter v. Price , Ky . 128 S. W. 701 , ( 1910 ) ; Jameson v . Gregory's
Exec . 61 Ky . ( 4 Metc .) 363 ( 1863 ) .
(2) Rights of Creditors . Subsection (2) makes explicit the rights
of creditors against a buyer who holds goods on approval or on sale or
return .
(3 ) Subsection (3 ) provides that where the buyer has a place of
business at which he deals in goods of the kind received , words such as
"on consignment " purporting to reserve title to the seller until payment
or resale will not prevent a transaction from being a sale or return , thus
subjecting the goods to levy by the buyer's creditors . Thus , doubts as to
the nature of the transaction are resolved in favor of the general creditors
of the buyer , and the possibility of using the form of a consignment (bail-
ment ) to conceal what is essentially a sale is reduced . Under the present
law , difficult questions of fact as to the nature of the transaction have
arisen . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 338 .
The Code , therefore , gives general creditors of such buyers protection
comparable to that given bona fide purchasers from agents , consignees and
factors entrusted with merchandise and the authority to sell . See Annotation
to UCC 2-403 ( 2) , infra .

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957 - KRS 361.190 (3)
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code, specifically KRS 361.190 (3), addresses the treatment of goods delivered to a business that deals in similar goods. It clarifies that even if a transaction is labeled as 'on consignment' or 'on memorandum,' if the buyer has a place of business where they sell such goods, the goods are considered 'on sale or return' with respect to the buyer's creditors. This protects creditors by making the goods subject to their claims, preventing sellers from using consignment forms to shield goods from the buyer's debts. The section also outlines conditions under which this rule does not apply, such as compliance with specific laws for evidencing a consignor's interest or the buyer being generally known to sell others' goods.

Section 2-327 . Special Incidents of Sale on Approval and Sale or
Return .
Summary
(1 ) Under a sale on approval , unless otherwise agreed :
(a) although the goods are identified to the contract , risk of loss
and title do not pass until acceptance ; and
(b) use of goods for trial is not acceptance , but failure to season-
ably notify of election to return is acceptance , and if the
goods conform , acceptance of a part is acceptance of the
whole; and
(c) after notice of election to return , return is at seller's risk and
expense, but a merchant buyer must follow reasonable in-
struction .
(2) Under a sale or return , unless otherwise agreed :
(a) the option to return extends to the whole or any commercial
unit of the goods while in substantially their original condi-
tion, but must be exercised seasonably ;
(b) the return is at the buyer's risk and expense .

Section 2-328 . Sale by Auction .
Summary
(1) In a sale by auction if goods are put up in lots each lot is the
subject of a separate sale .
(2) A sale by auction is complete when the auctioneer so announces
by the fall of the hammer or other customary way . Where a bid is made
while the hammer is falling on a prior bid, the auctioneer may reopen the
bidding or declare the goods sold under the prior bid .
(3 ) Such a sale is with reserve unless the goods are in explicit terms
put up without reserve . In an auction with reserve , the auctioneer may
withdraw the goods at any time until he announces completion of the
sale. In an auction without reserve , after the auctioneer calls for bids
on an article or lot, that article or lot cannot be withdrawn unless no bid
is made within a reasonable time . In either case a bidder may retract his
bid until the auctioneer's announcement of completion of the sale, but a
bidder's retraction does not revive any previous bid .
(4) If the auctioneer knowingly receives a bid on the seller's behalf
or the seller makes or procures such a bid, and notice has not been given
that liberty for such bidding is reserved , the buyer may at his option avoid
the sale or take the goods at the price of the last good faith bid prior to
the completion of the sale. This subsection shall not apply to any bid
at a forced sale.

Introductory Comment
Although the Uniform Commercial Code abandons the "title " concept
in the law of sales, there are instances outside the law of sales where state
of title is material . This part presents the rules for determining title in
such cases .
Section 2-401 . Passing of Title : Reservation for Security ; Limited
Application of This Section .

Each provision of this article with regard to the rights , obligations
and remedies of the seller , buyer or other third parties applies irrespective
of title to the goods except where the provision refers to such title . Insofar
as situations are not covered by other provisions and matters concerning
title become material , the following rules apply :

identification to the contract ( UCC 2-501 ) , and unless otherwise explicitly

limited by this act. Any retention or reservation by the seller of the title

Sales Act . Therefore , in dealing with any problem under the Code , it is
important to ascertain whether any specific provision deals with the prob-
lem; if so , it will override any implication to be drawn from the location
of the title .

Section 2-402. Rights of Seller's Creditors against Sold Goods .
Summary
(1) Except as provided in subsections ( 2) and ( 3 ) , rights of unse-
cured creditors of the seller with respect to goods which have been identi-
fied to a contract for sale are subject to the buyer's rights to recover the
goods under this article ( UCC 2-502 and 2-716 ) .
(2) A creditor of the seller may treat a sale or an identification of
goods as void if as against him possession by the seller is fraudulent under
any law of the state where the goods are situated , but possession in good
faith and current course of trade by a merchant - seller for a commercially
reasonable time after sale or identification is not fraudulent .
(3) Nothing in this article shall be deemed to impair the rights of
creditors of the seller :
(a) under the provisions of the article on Secured Transactions
(Article 9 ) ; or
(b) where identification to the contract or delivery is made not
in current course of trade but in satisfaction of or as security
for a pre -existing claim for money, security or the like and
is made under circumstances which under any rule of law
of the state where the goods are situated would apart from
this article constitute the transaction a fraudulent transfer
or avoidable preference .

Subsection ( 1) KRS 378.040 ; subsection (2) KRS 361.260 .
Comment
( 1) Subsection ( 1) subordinates the rights of the seller's creditors
to the buyer's possessory remedies . The Code seems to extend the buyer's
rights from those provided in KRS 378.040 whereby the buyer may only
recover if he is a purchaser without notice .
(2) Sold Goods in Seller's Possession . This section of the Code con-
tains the general rule of Sec. 26 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.260 ,
whereby the local rules of law on transactions in fraud of creditors are
preserved . The exception in subsection ( 2 ) preserving the buyer's interest
in goods left with a merchant - seller for a " commercially reasonable time
in current course of trade" is not found in the Uniform Sales Act . This
provision would appear substantially to modify the Kentucky rule em-
bodied in KRS 378.040, that leaving goods in the seller's possession is deemed
fraudulent as against creditors . Kentucky courts have held , in an unbroken
line of decisions , that an absolute sale of personal property is in judgment
of law per se fraudulent as to purchasers from and creditors of the seller ,
unless the possession of the property accompanies the title. Vanmeter v .
Estill , 78 Ky . 456 ( 1880 ) ; Bourbon Bank v . Porter , 22 K. L. R. 429 , 57 S. W.
609 ( 1900 ) ; Frankfort Chair Co. v . Buchanan , 21 K. L. R. 269 , 51 S. W. 179 ;
(1899 ) ; Ky. Ref . Co. v . Globe Ref. Co., 104 Ky . 559 , 47 S. W. 602 ( 1898 ) ; but
this rule does not apply to the sale of growing crops , or of property not
in a condition to be removed without injury , Robbins v . Oldham , 62 Ky . ( 1
Duv .) 28 ( 1863 ) ; Kenton v . Ratcliffe , 105 Ky . 376 , 49 S. W. 14 ( 1898 ) ; nor to
property sold under execution , Greathouse v . Brown , 21 Ky . ( 5 T. B. Mon. )
280 ( 1827 ) ; or to property exempt from execution , Anthony v . Wade , 64 Ky .
(1 Bush ) 110 (1866 ) .
(3) Preferences . Subsection ( 3 ) is designed to give effect to the
rule of law of the state where the goods are situated governing preferential
identification or delivery not in the current course of trade .

or had power to transfer except that a purchaser of a limited interest
acquires rights only to the extent of the interest purchased . A person with
voidable title has power to transfer a good title to a good faith purchaser
for value . When goods have been delivered under a transaction of purchase
the purchaser has such power even though :
(a) the transferor was deceived as to the identity of the pur-
chaser; or

Comment
(1) Power to Transfer . Subsection ( 1) , in giving a purchaser all
title his transferor "has power to transfer ," appears consistent with general
agency principles , such as the doctrine of apparent authority . See Restate-
ment , Agency , Secs . 8, 12, and Kentucky Annotations .
The provision of subsection ( 1) giving good title to a good faith
purchaser for value who purchases from a person with a voidable title
restates with no apparent change Sec. 24 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS
361.240 . See Dudley v . Lovins , 310 Ky. 491, 220 S. W. 2d 978 ( 1949 ) . Under
this provision , a bona fide purchaser from one who obtained delivery of
goods by a bad check would receive good title . Moreover , by virtue of
UCC 2-401 ( 1 ) (b) , supra, even if the bona fide purchaser's transferor
acquired the goods by a bad check under a "cash sale" agreement, the bona
fide purchaser would apparently still be protected against the "cash sale"
seller . Compare 2 Williston, Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 346a .
(2) Entrusting to a Merchant Who Deals in Those Goods . The pro-
vision of subsection ( 2) is a departure from the present law . It protects one
who purchases in the ordinary course of business from a merchant , to whom
the goods have been entrusted , even though the merchant was given no
authority to deal with the goods by the entruster . No Kentucky case has
gone this far, and the provision is contra to general principles of agency .
See Restatement , Agency , Sec . 200 .
The protection of subsection ( 2) is limited to a "buyer in the ordinary
course of business ." This term as defined in UCC 1-201 ( 9 ) , supra , is more
restricted than a " good faith purchaser for value ," defined in UCC 1-201
(19 ) . The Code's definition excludes transfers in bulk or as security for

Comment
The buyer's remedy here is new . Compare KRS 361.660 basing buyer's
right to obtain the goods on title having passed to him . Although no Ken-
tucky cases have been found supporting the Code's new provision , it does
find strong support in 1 Williston , Sales Secs . 143 and 144 wherein it is
stated :
"Since an insolvent is not precluded by the existing bank-
ruptcy act, or any other, from making a transfer of his assets , or
any part of them, for a return honestly bargained for as an equiv-
alent , there is no objection to the performance by him of a fair
contract wholly executory on both sides, if made in good faith ; ...
but if the insolvent , prior to the performance on his part , has
already received the whole or part of the consideration for his
own promised performance , so that a debt or obligation is due from
him , the situation is different . In that event, insolvency can never
properly be a make -weight for the decision of a court . Ifunder
the facts of the case , apart from the defendant's insolvency , equity
regards the plaintiff as having an interest in the property in ques-
tion , specific enforcement of the obligation to transfer to him that
interest should be granted , and if the defendant becomes bankrupt ,
the court of bankruptcy should recognize the plaintiff's interest in
the property ." ( emphasis added)
..
This section would modify to some extent the rule of Vanmeter v . Estill ,
78 Ky . 456 ( 1880 ) , allowing seller's creditors to attach goods retained by
seller after conveyance to buyer . Compare UCC 2-402 .
As to insolvency after the ten day period , see Article 9 (Secured
Transactions ) .
Section 2-503 . Manner of Seller's Tender of Delivery .
Summary
(1) Tender of delivery requires the seller to put and hold conforming
goods at the buyer's disposition and give the buyer notice to take delivery .
The manner , time and place for tender are determined by the agreement
and this article , and in particular :
(a) tender must be at a reasonable hour and goods must be kept
available for the time necessary for the buyer to take pos-
session ; but
(b) unless otherwise agreed , the buyer must furnish suitable
facilities for the receipt of the goods .
(2) Where the case is within UCC 2-504 tender requires the seller
to comply with that section .
(3 ) Where seller is required to deliver at destination , tender requires
his compliance with subsection ( 1) and tender of documents under sub-
sections ( 4) and ( 5) where necessary .

Section 2-506 . Rights of Financing Agency .
Summary
(1) A financing agency by paying or purchasing for value a draft
which relates to a shipment of goods acquires to the extent of the payment
or purchase and in addition to its own rights under the draft and any
document of title securing it any rights of the shipper in the goods including
the right to stop delivery and the shipper's right to have the draft honored
by the buyer .
(2) The right to reimbursement of a financing agency which has in
good faith honored or purchased the draft under commitment or authority
from the buyer is not impaired by subsequent discovery of defects in
relevant documents which were apparently regular on their faces .

Comment

This section is new . Details of the relationship between seller or buyer
and financing banks are governed by Article 4 Bank Deposits and Col-
lections , and Article 5 Letters of Credit. The rights given the bank for
making payment against a draft which relates to a shipment appear to be
consistent with general principles of equitable assignment .
Section 2-507 . Effect of Seller's Tender ; Delivery on Condition .
Summary
(1 ) Tender of delivery is a condition to the buyer's duty to accept
and , absent agreement , to pay for the goods . Tender entitles the seller to
acceptance and payment according to the contract .
(2) Where payment is due and demanded on delivery to the buyer ,
his right as against the seller to retain or dispose of the goods is conditional
on his making the payment .

Comment
The section states general principles consistent with present law. In
making tender a condition of buyer's reciprocal duty the Code is consistent
with Sec. 11 (2) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.110 ( 2) . Making pay-
ment , if due, a concurrent condition of the duty to tender is consistent with
Sec. 42 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.420 .
Section 2-508 . Cure by Seller of Improper Tender or Delivery ;
Replacement .
Summary
( 1) Where tender or delivery is rejected as nonconforming and time
for performance has not expired , the seller may notify the buyer of his
intention to cure and then make conforming delivery within the contract
time.
( 2) Where a nonconforming tender is rejected and the seller thought
it would be accepted with or without allowance , if the seller notifies the
buyer he may have a reasonable time to substitute a conforming tender .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
( 1 ) Subsection ( 1 ) allowing seller to replace a defective tender with
a conforming tender "within the contract time" is new as a statutory pro-
vision , but it may not change prevailing law . A defective tender coupled
with notice of intention to perform fully within the contract time probably
is not an anticipatory breach or repudiation . Restatement , Contracts , Sec .
319 and Kentucky Annotations thereto ( cases cited to the effect that an
anticipatory breach may be withdrawn before "accepted and acted upon " ) .
However , no Kentucky decisions have been found where the repudiation
was later nullified.
(2) Subsection ( 2) which covers replacement of a nonconforming
tender appears to be consistent with the cases cited in the Kentucky
Annotations to the Restatement , Contracts , Sec . 276 .

destination , the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the
goods are duly delivered to the carrier even though the ship-
ment is under reservation (UCC 2-505 ) ; but

KRS 361.190 (4) (b) and (5) , 361.220 .
Comment
The rule of subsection (a) in transferring risk to buyer on delivery to
carrier conforms with the result under Sec. 19 , Rules 4 ( 2) and ( 5 ) and
Sec . 22 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 , 361.220, under which risk is
derived from " title ." See accord , Troy Refining Corp. v . Slagter Oil and
Grease Co. , 61 F. Supp . 369 ( W.D. Ky. 1945 ) .
The requirement that the goods be "duly " delivered to transfer risk car-
ries forward the requirement of Sec. 19 , Rule 4 (b) of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.190 ( 4) (b) , that the shipment be "in pursuance" of the con-
tract . See accord , UCC 2-510 of the Code .
A problem of construction and of comparison with existing law is pre-
sented by subsection ( 1) (b) of this section of the Code. Risk is held on
seller during shipment if the contract "requires him to deliver at a particular
destination ." Under UCC 2-319 ( 1 ) ( b ) risk is on seller if the quotation
is "F.O.B. the place of destination ." But it is not clear whether the same
result follows if the seller quotes a delivered price without use of the term
"F.O.B." If under a delivered price risk is placed on the buyer , the Code
thereby would change existing law under Sec. 19, Rule 5, and Sec. 46 of
the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 (5 ) , 361.460. (It would also appear
that the Code would thereby make results turn on an insubstantial differ-
ence in the form of stating the price .)

Section 2-510. Effect of Breach on Risk of Loss .
Summary
( 1 ) Where a tender or delivery so fails to conform to the contract as
to give a right of rejection , the risk of loss remains on the seller until cure or
acceptance.
(2) Where the buyer rightfully revokes acceptance he may to the
extent of any deficiency in his effective insurance coverage, treat the risk
of loss as having rested on the seller from the beginning .
(3) Where the buyer as to conforming goods identified to the contract
repudiates or otherwise is in breach before risk of loss has passed , the seller
may to the extent of any deficiency in his insurance coverage, treat the risk
of loss as resting on the buyer for a commercially reasonable time .

Comment
(1) Loss on Non -Conforming Seller . Holding risk during delivery on
a seller who is not conforming to the contract to the extent that the breach
is such as to give a right of rejection carries forward the rule of Sec. 19 ,
Rule 4 (2) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 (4) ( b ) , that the seller
appropriates by delivering goods "in pursuance " to the contract .
(2) Risk After Rightful Revocation of Acceptance . The general rule
of subsection (2 ) placing risk of loss on seller after the buyer revokes ac-
ceptance is in accord with Secs . 22 and 69 ( 1) (d) of the Uniform Sales Act
which gives the buyer power to rescind and thereby transfer risk to seller ,
KRS 361.220, 361.690 ( 1) ( d) . It is however , subject to an insurance pro-
vision discussed below .
(3) Insurance . Subsection (2) and ( 3) as to insurance are new . Sub-
section (3) limits the buyer's risk under that subsection to a "commercially
reasonable time ," thereby preventing the aggrieved seller from holding the
goods indefinitely at the buyer's risk. Compare KRS 361.220 (2) ( risk of
loss for delay in delivery ) , 361.640 ( 1) and ( 2 ) ( damages for breach by
buyer ) and 361.670 (2) and (3) (damages for seller's breach ) .
Section 2-511 . Tender of Payment by Buyer ; Payment by Check .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed, tender of payment is a condition to the
seller's duty to tender and complete delivery .
(2) Tender of payment is sufficient when made by any means or in
any manner current in the ordinary course of business unless the seller de-
mands legal tender and gives a reasonable extension of time to procure it .
(3) Subject to the provisions of this act on the effect of an instrument
on an obligation ( UCC 3-802 ) payment by check is conditional and is de-
feated as between the parties by dishonor .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.420 .
Comment
Subsection ( 1) , stating that tender of payment and tender of delivery
are presumed to be concurrent conditions follows KRS 361.420 .
Subsection (2 ) is new as a statutory provision , but probably does not
change existing law . See Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 305 and Kentucky
Annotations .
Subsection (3 ) , making payment by check conditional as between the
parties is likewise new , in that KRS Ch. 361 contains no express coverage of
the situation . But the provision seems to be in conformity with the view in
most jurisdictions . See 3 Williston, Contracts , Sec. 732 ; Vold, Sales, 174 .

are consistent with this provision . ( Buyer "acquires no added right" by
wrongfully retaining bill of lading without honoring draft.) Apparently the
Code intends to allow seller to recover the goods from the buyer on dishonor
of a check given in payment . This is consistent with prevailing rules allow-
ing rescission for fraud . But it is to be noted that in making payment by
check conditional the Code does not affect the rights of third parties , such
as bona fide purchasers .
Section 2-512 . Payment by Buyer Before Inspection .
Summary
( 1) Where payment is required before inspection nonconformity does
not excuse the buyer from making payment unless :
(a) the nonconformity appears without inspection ; or
(b) despite tender of documents the circumstances would justify
injunction against honor under UCC 5-111 .
(2) Payment under subsection ( 1 ) does not constitute acceptance or
impair the buyer's right to inspect or any of his remedies .
KRS 361.470 , 361.490 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The provision of subsection ( 1 ) requiring payment of price by the
buyer before inspection where the contract requires it has no express coun-
terpart in KRS Ch . 361 ( Unifor mSales Act ) . But compare , KRS 361.470
(3 ) . The requirement seems to be in accord with general principles of con-
tract law.
Preservation of the buyer's rights and remedies in spite of payment
before inspection , in accordance with the contract appears to follow the
policy of KRS 361.490 .
Section 2-513 . Buyer's Right to Inspection of Goods .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed, and subject to subsection ( 3 ) , where
goods are tendered or delivered or identified to the contract for sale, the
buyer has a right before payment or acceptance to inspect them at any
reasonable place and time and in any reasonable manner . When the seller is
required or authorized to send the goods to the buyer , the inspection may
be after their arrival .
(2) The buyer must bear the inspection expenses , but if the goods are
nonconforming and are rejected he may recover from the seller .

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) addresses payment by a buyer before inspection of goods. It outlines conditions under which nonconformity of goods does not excuse the buyer from payment, such as when the nonconformity is apparent without inspection or when circumstances justify an injunction against honoring documents. It also clarifies that payment before inspection does not constitute acceptance or impair the buyer's right to inspect or their remedies. The text also references analogous rules in the Uniform Sales Act and general contract law principles.

Comment
( 1) General Right of Inspection . The general rule of subsection ( 1)

vision but seems in accord with the general view . See , 3 Williston , Sales,
Sec . 477 .
(3) Instances Where no Inspection Presumed , C.O.D. In accord with
subsection 3 (a) , Uniform Sales Act Sec. 47 (3) , KRS 361.470 (3) . See also,
3 Williston , Sales, Sec. 479a .
(4) Place or Method of Inspection . Subsection ( 4 ) is new , there being
no corresponding provision appearing in KRS Ch . 361 , the Uniform Sales
Act , treating place or method of inspection . There are no analogous Ken-
tucky cases on the point . Compare 3 Williston , Sales, Sec. 480 .

When on Payment .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed documents against which a draft is drawn are
to be delivered to the drawee on acceptance of the draft if it is payable more
than three days after presentment ; otherwise only on payment .
None.

properly surrender the bill of lading on acceptance (without payment ) of
the bill of exchange . See 2 Williston , Sales , Sec. 290. National Bank of Com-
merce v . Merchants ' National Bank, 91 U.S. 92 ( 1875 ) ; Moore v. Louisiana
Nat. Bank , 44 La. 99 , 10 So. 407 (1892) . If the bill of exchange which goes
forward with the bill of lading is payable on demand , it is equally clear
that the bill of lading should not be surrendered until the bill of exchange
is paid , not merely accepted ; acceptance of a demand bill is not customary .
2 Williston , Sales , Sec. 290 ; Tampico Banking Co. , S.A. , v . Barber , 3 F. 2d
136 ( 5th Cir. 1925 ) ( involved in a sight draft) . Also it is well established
that if a bank , receiving for collection a draft with a bill of lading attached,
under instructions to deliver the latter only upon payment of the draft, sur-
renders the bill of lading without collecting the draft, it is liable for the loss
sustained by reason of such unauthorized action . Shippee v. Pallotti , An-
dretta & Co. , 117 Conn . 472 , 168 Atl . 880 ( 1933 ) .
Attention is directed to the fact that while the cases cited are limited to
bills of lading this section is broader , being designed to cover any document
against which a draft may be drawn whatever may be its form . See also
UCC 3-703 and 5-112 .
Section 2-515 . Preserving Evidence of Goods in Dispute .
Summary
In furtherance of the adjustment of any claim or dispute :
(a) either party on notice to the other and for the purpose of
ascertaining facts and preserving evidence may inspect , test
and sample the goods including those in the possession or
control of the other ; and
(b) the parties may agree to a third party inspection or survey
to determine the conformity or condition of the goods and
may agree that the findings shall be binding upon them in any
subsequent litigation or adjustment .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
This section is new to the sales law , no corresponding provision appear-
ing in the Uniform Sales Act , KRS Ch. 361. It represents another instance
of integrating the rules of evidence and practice and procedure into the Code
as is pointed up in the provision which entitles either party , upon notice , to
an inspection or testing of the goods in the possession of the other party to
the dispute , for the purpose of establishing evidence in the dispute .
Subsection (b ) conforms to the existing favorable attitude toward the
settlement of disputes by arbitrators . See KRS Ch. 417 .

Part 6
BREACH , REPUDIATION AND EXCUSE
Introductory Comment
This part of the Code defines the buyer's rights in the case of improper
delivery and his duties in regard to the exercise of those rights . The ques-
tions of what constitutes an acceptance of goods and the incidents that arise
therefrom are also covered in this part . The part provides for methods of
rightful rejection of the goods , rather than "recission" of the sale , and the
right after acceptance of the goods to "revoke acceptance ." It appears that
the distinction between rescission and rejection as to rights has been abol-
ished . Of very practical significance is the right in the buyer to accept any
commercial unit and reject the rest. The effect of repudiation of the con-
tract by either part , as well as their respective rights in case of casualty to
unique goods or failure of presupposed conditions , are provided in this part .
Section 2-601 . Buyer's Rights on Improper Delivery .
Summary
Subject to the provisions of UCC 2-612 on breach of installment con-
tracts and unless otherwise agreed , under UCC 2-718 and 2-719 on contractual
limitation of remedies , if the goods or tender of delivery fail to conform , the
buyer may :
(a) reject the whole ; or
(b) accept the whole ; or
(c) accept any commercial unit or units and reject the rest .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.110 , 361.440 , 361.690 ( 1) .
Comment
(a) Performance by Seller; Rejection of Whole . The general rule of
subsection ( a) allowing rejection is in accord with Sec. 69 ( 1) (c) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.690 ( 1) ( c) . The right of rejection is, however ,
limited in other Sections of the Code . See , e.g. , UCC 2-508 ( cure ) ; UCC
2-612 ( installment contracts ) . The Code abandons the distinction between
" rejection " and " recission " under Secs . 69 ( 1 ) ( c) and 69 ( 1 ) (d) of the
Uniform Sales Act . However , roughly comparable to "rescission " is the
power to revoke acceptance under Sec. 2-608 , infra .
(b) Acceptance . Accord, Secs . 49 , 69 ( 1) ( a ) and (b) of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.490 , 361.690 ( 1 ) ( a) and (b) .
(c) Partial Acceptance . The Uniform Sales Act did not specifically
provide for partial acceptance where seller tenders defective goods , but an
analogy favoring partial acceptance may be drawn from Sec. 44 (3) of the

The Sales Act uses and defines the phrase "divisible contract " for a
different purpose , and makes divisibility turn on whether the price can be
divided . KRS 361.070 (2 ) ( b ) , 361.080 ( 2 ) ( b ) , 361.760 ( 1) . This section of

provision on partial revocation of acceptance in UCC 2-608 , infra .
Section 2-602 . Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection .
Summary
(1) Rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their
delivery or tender . It is ineffective without notice to the seller .
(2) Subject to UCC 2-603 and 2-604 on rejected goods :
(a) after rejection any exercise of ownership by the buyer is
wrongful as against the seller ; and
(b) if the buyer before rejection has taken possession of the goods
in which he does not have a security interest under subsection
(3 ) of UCC 2-711 , after rejection he shall hold them with rea-
sonable care at seller's disposition long enough for the seller
to remove them; but
(c) the buyer has no further obligations with regard to goods
rightfully rejected .
(3 ) With respect to goods wrongfully rejected , the seller's rights are
governed by UCC 2-703 on seller's remedies .
KRS 361.480 , 361.500 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1 ) Rejection Within Reasonable Time ; Notice . Subsection ( 1 ) is in
accord with Sec. 48 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.480 , under which the
buyer is deemed to have "accepted " the goods if he keeps the goods an un-
reasonable time without notice of rejection . Cf. Uniform Sales Act Sec .
69 ( 3 ) , KRS 361.690 (3 ) , cutting off a buyer's right to "rescission " under
such circumstances . See cases under UCC 2-606 and 2-607 , infra .
(2 ) Exercise of Ownership After Rejection ; Duty of Buyer to Hold
With Reasonable Care . The rule of subsection (2) (b ) may impose a broader
responsibility upon a buyer than that under Sec. 50 of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.500, which provides that "it is sufficient if (buyer ) notifies
seller that he refuses to 'accept' the goods ."

entries.citation: Uniform Commercial Code: Analysis of Effect on Existing Kentucky Law, 1957, p. 123

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957 - Section 2-602. Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) outlines the procedures and consequences for rejecting goods purchased under a sales contract. It specifies that rejection must occur within a reasonable time after delivery or tender and requires notice to the seller. After rejection, any exercise of ownership by the buyer is wrongful. If the buyer has taken possession of the goods before rejection, they must hold them with reasonable care at the seller's disposition for a period allowing the seller to remove them. The buyer has no further obligations for rightfully rejected goods. Wrongfully rejected goods are subject to the seller's remedies.

Section 2-603 . Merchant Buyer's Duties as to Rightfully Rejected
Goods .
Summary
(1 ) Subject to any security interest in the buyer , when the seller has
no agent or place of busines sat the market of rejection , a merchant buyer's
duty after rejection of goods in his possession or control , is to follow the
reasonable instructions of the seller and absent instructions to make rea-
sonable efforts to sell them for the seller if they are perishable or threaten
to decline in value . Instructions are not reasonable if on demand indemnity
for expenses is not forthcoming .
(2) When the buyer sells goods under subsection ( 1) he is entitled to
reimbursement from the seller or the proceeds of the sale for reasonable ex-
penses, and if no selling commission is included in the expenses , to the usual
commission or to a reasonable sum not to exceed ten percent of the gross
proceeds .
(3) The buyer is held only to good faith and good faith conduct is
neither acceptance nor conversion nor the basis of an action for damages .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Duty to Follow Instructions and Make Reasonable Efforts to Sell.
The Sales Act does not specifically impose any duty upon a buyer who
rightfully rejects to mitigate the seller's loss by selling . By Sec . 50 , KRS
361.500 , where B "refuses to accept " he is "not bound to return them"-and
"it is sufficient if he notifies the seller that he refused to accept them ."
Where "rescission " is involved , Sec. 69 (3) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.690 ( 3) , requires buyer to "return or offer to return " the goods .
Insofar as this subsection in stated circumstances places a duty on the
buyer to resell , it probably changes existing law . It is comparable , however ,
to the obligation imposed by the Perishable Agricultural Commodities Act,
7 U.S.C. , Sec. 499 (b) ( 3 ) , as to goods shipped in interstate commerce,
which makes it unlawful for any commission merchant to discard , dump or
destroy without reasonable cause .
Also the obligation is probably not much greater than that imposed
upon the buyer in case of rescission where the seller has refused to accept
the buyer's offer to return the goods . By KRS 361.690 ( 5 ) the buyer in such
cases is deemed to hold the goods as bailee for the seller . Automatic Equip-
ment Co. v . Mohney , 295 Ky . 451 , 174 S.W. 2d 716 ( 1943 ) .
(2) Buyer's Reimbursement for Expenses . This specific provision is
new . However , aside from the allowance of a selling commission of ten
percent , it appears to be in accord with general rules . See 29 A.L.R. 61 ,
(1924) . Compare KRS 361.690 ( 5) giving the rescinding buyer the rights
given an unpaid seller by KRS 361.530; Sears, Roebuck & Co. v . Lea , 198 F.
2d 1012 (6th Cir . 1952 ) . Subsection ( 3 ) immunizes the buyer who exercises
in good faith the power given by this section .

classification.title: Section 2-603. Merchant Buyer's Duties as to Rightfully Rejected Goods
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties of a merchant buyer after rightfully rejecting goods. If the seller has no agent or place of business at the market of rejection, the buyer must follow the seller's reasonable instructions. If no instructions are given, the buyer must make reasonable efforts to sell the goods if they are perishable or likely to decline in value. The buyer is entitled to reimbursement for reasonable expenses and a selling commission. The buyer is only held to good faith conduct.

The statute's purpose is to regulate commercial transactions, not to enforce racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 2-604 . Buyer's Options as to Salvage of Rightfully Rejected
Goods .
Summary
Subject to UCC 2-603 on perishables , if the seller gives no instructions
within a reasonable time after notification the buyer may store the goods
for seller's account or reship them to him or resell for seller's account with
reimbursement . Such action is not acceptance or conversion .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Sales Act contains no comparable provision , but see , accord 29
A.L.R. 61 ( 1924 ) . Compare KRS 361.690 (5 ) as to rescinding buyer . Under
some circumstances a change in present law may be effected since under
KRS 361.480 a buyer is deemed to have accepted goods by any act in rela-
tion to the goods which is inconsistent with the ownership of the seller .
The Code provision encourages salvage by buyers who have rightfully re-
jected without the danger of incurring liability for the price by acceptance .
Section 2-605 . Waiver of Buyer's Objections by Failure to
Particularize .
Summary
( 1) The buyer's failure to state a defect ascertainable by reasonable
inspection precludes his reliance on the unstated defect to justify rejection
or establish breach :
(2 )
(a) where the seller could have cured it if seasonably stated ; or
(b) between merchants when the seller made a written request for
a full and final written statement of defects on which the
buyer proposes to rely .
Payment against documents without reservation precludes re-
covery of the payment for defects apparent on the face of the documents .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
( 1) Failure to State Particular Defect in Connection with Rejection .
The Uniform Sales Act has no such provision , but a similar rule is recog-
nized as a matter of contract law . See Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 304 , and
Kentucky Annotations . 46 Am. Jur ., Sales, Sec . 210 ; 77 C.J.S. , Sales , Sec .
202. Compare KRS 361.490 under which notice must "fairly advise the
seller of the alleged defects " if a claim for breach is to be made after ac
