classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) from 1957 discusses the legal concept of a 'holder' in relation to negotiable instruments, particularly when the instrument has been obtained unlawfully. It clarifies that a party obtaining an instrument by unlawful means is considered a holder, but can be divested of it by the rightful owner. This presumption does not apply to lost or stolen instruments in the hands of a finder. The UCC also allows for various remedies to recover an instrument, which are only cut off by a holder in due course. Section 3-208 addresses 'Reacquisition,' where a prior party can cancel indorsements and reissue or further negotiate an instrument upon its return, discharging intervening parties.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) of 1957, specifically discussing negotiable instruments and the rights of holders. The content focuses on legal definitions and procedures related to financial instruments, such as how an instrument obtained unlawfully is treated, the concept of a 'holder in due course,' and the process of reacquiring an instrument. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it refers to compiled statutes like the Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) and the UCC itself is a codified body of law.

(4) New Topics .
(a) A payee may be a holder in due course .
(b) One is not a holder in due course who purchases at judicial
sale or acquires under legal process, or in taking over an
estate or who purchases or otherwise takes in bulk the assets
of a prior holder not in ordinary course of such holder's
business .
(c) Value includes security for an antecedent claim whether or
not due , and the giving up of a negotiable instrument or an
irrevocable commitment to a third person .
(d) A considerable listing of matters which constitute or do not
constitute notice to a purchaser in UCC 3-304 .
(e) Defenses of infancy and any other incapacity rendering the
obligation a nullity are good against a holder in due course .
(f) Defense of a fraud in the factum is good against a holder
in due course .
(g) Discharge in bankruptcy or other insolvency proceedings is
good against a holder in due course, but knowledge of dis-
charge of one party does not preclude a holder from being a
holder in due course as to others .
(h) Signatures are admitted unless denied in the pleadings ; bur-
den is on party claiming under a disputed signature , but he is
aided by a presumption of validity except where action is to
enforce obligation of a purported signer who has died or be-
come incompetent before proof is required .
Section 3-301 . Rights of a Holder .
Summary
The holder of an instrument whether or not he is the owner may trans-
fer or negotiate it and, except as provided in UCC 3-603 , discharge it or en-
force payment in his own name .
KRS 356.051 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord , KRS 356.051 . The provision of KRS 356.051 as to discharge by
payment is now covered by UCC 3-603 ( 1 ) . The purpose of this section is to
state in one place all the rights of a holder and to make it clear that all
holders have such rights . The one exception is found in UCC 3-603 on pay-
ment and satisfaction .
There is no question but that the holder of a negotiable instrument prop-
erly indorsed may sue thereon in his own name . Doherty v . Louisville First

Section 3-302 . Holder in Due Course .
Summary
(1) A holder in due course is a holder who takes the instrument :
(a) for value ; and
(b) in good faith ; and
(c) without notice that it is overdue or has been dishonored or
of any defense against or claim to it on the part of any person .
(2) A payee may be a holder in due course .
(3) A holder does not become a holder in due course :
(a) by purchase at juidicial sale or by taking under legal process ;
or
(b) by acquiring it in taking over an estate ; or
(c) by purchasing it as part of a bulk transaction not in regular
course of business of the transferor .
(4) A purchaser of a limited interest can be a holder in due course
only to the extent of the interest purchased .
KRS 356.052 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) (a) Accord , KRS 356.052 ( 3 ) .
(b) Accord, KRS 356.052 ( 3 ) .
(c) Compare KRS 356.052, conditions 1, 2 and 4. Under present
law, the instrument must be in fact not overdue for a person
taking it to be a holder in due course . Kentucky cases do not
discuss the point , but the language of the cases speaks strongly
of taking before maturity . See Hughes v . West, 217 Ky . 40 ,
288 S. W. 1011 , ( 1926 ) ; Paintsville National Bank v . Robinson ,
220 Ky . 418, 295 S. W. 412, ( 1927 ) . Under the proposed law
he will be a holder in due course if he took without notice
that it was overdue even if it was so in fact .
The provisions as to previous dishonor and infirmity are in accord
with KRS 356.052 ( 2 ) and (4) . As to "claim against it " see UCC
3-304 and annotations .
(2) A long line of Kentucky decisions have held that a payee is not
a holder in due course . Southern National Life Realty Corp. v . Peoples '

classification.title: Section 3-302. Holder in Due Course.
classification.summary: This section defines a 'holder in due course' as a holder who takes a negotiable instrument for value, in good faith, and without notice of it being overdue, dishonored, or of any defense or claim against it. It clarifies that a payee can be a holder in due course, but certain acquisitions like judicial sales, legal processes, estate takings, or bulk transactions not in the regular course of business do not qualify a holder as a holder in due course. Purchasers of limited interests are only holders in due course to the extent of their interest.

Section 3-303 . Taking for Value .
Summary
A holder takes the instrument for value :
(a) To the extent that the agreed consideration has been performed
or that he acquires a security interest or lien otherwise than by legal
process ; or
(b) When he takes in payment of or as security for an antecedent
claim against any person whether due or not ; or
(c) When he gives a negotiable instrument or makes an irrevocable
commitment to a third person .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.025 . 356.026 , 356.027 , 356.054 .
Comment
(a) As to security interests , Accord , KRS 356.027 . As to payment of
consideration : Contra , KRS 356.025 , which makes any consideration suf-
ficient to support a simple contract "value " . This should include an execu-
tory promise . But compare KRS 356.054 whereby a holder who has not
fully paid the agreed amount is given the rights of a holder in due course
only to the extent of the amount actually paid .

Section 3-304 . Notice to Purchaser .
Summary
(1) The purchaser has notice of a claim or defense if:
(a) the instrument is so incomplete , bears visible evidence of
forgery or alteration or is otherwise so irregular as to call
into question its validity, terms or ownership or create an
ambiguity as to the party to pay ; or
(b) he has notice that it is voidable in whole or part as to any
party or that all parties have been discharged .
(2) The purchaser has notice of a claim when he has knowledge that
a fiduciary has negotiated it in payment of or as security for his own debt
or in any transaction for his own benefit or otherwise in breach of duty .
(3) The purchaser has notice that an instrument is overdue if he has
reason to know that :
(a) any of the principal is overdue or that there is an uncured
default in payment of another instrument of the same series ;
(b)
(c)
or
acceleration has been made; or
he is taking a demand instrument after demand or more than
a reasonable time after its issue . For a check drawn and pay-
able within the United States and its territories a reasonable
time is presumed to be thirty days .
(4) Knowledge of the following is not notice :
(a) that the instrument is antedated or postdated ;
(b) that is was issued or negotiated for an executory promise or
accompanied by a separate agreement unless he has notice of
a defense or claim arising therefrom ;
(c) that any party has signed for accommodation ;
(d) that an incomplete instrument has been completed unless he
has notice of improper completion ;

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code, specifically Section 3-304, outlines what constitutes 'notice to a purchaser' regarding claims or defenses against a negotiable instrument. It details conditions under which a purchaser would be considered to have notice, such as an instrument being incomplete, forged, altered, or irregular. It also covers notice of voidable instruments, discharge of parties, overdue instruments, and acceleration of payment. Importantly, it specifies what knowledge does *not* constitute notice, including antedating, postdating, executory promises, accommodation signatures, or incomplete instruments that have been properly completed.

(e) except as otherwise provided with respect to restrictive in-
dorsements that any person negotiating the instrument is or
was a fiduciary ;
(f) that there has been a default in interest payment or payment
of any other instrument except one in the same series.
(5 ) The filing or recording of a document is not notice to any one
who would otherwise be a holder in due course .
(6) Notice must be received so as to give a reasonable opportunity to
act upon it .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.045 , 356.052 , 356.053 , 356.055 , 356.056 .
Comment
(1) ( a) Accord, KRS 356.052 ( 1) . The present wording is in terms
stricter than the new , but the cases appear to be in accord
with the Code in requiring that the irregularity be one which
indicates that something is wrong . Karsner v . Cooper , 195
Ky . 8 , 241 S. W. 346 ( 1922 ) ; Citizens ' State Bank v . Johnson
County , 182 Ky. 531 , 207 S. W. 8 ( 1918 ) ; Pensacola State Bank
v . Melton , 210 F. 57 ( W.D. Ky . 1913 ) . See also , 10 C.J.S. ,
Bills and Notes , Sec . 328 ( a ) .
(b) This subsection appears to include notice of such defenses de-
nominated "defective title " under KRS 356.055 , which are
now called " infirmities ." Since defenses which render the
instrument a nullity are good even against a holder in due
course , UCC 3-305 , there was no need to include defenses
other than those rendering the obligation voidable .
(2 ) Compare KRS 356.056. Accord , Kenyon Realty Co. v . National De-
posit Bank , 140 Ky . 133 , 130 S. W. 965 ( 1910 ) ; Dowell v . Bauman , 189 Ky.
136 , 224 S. W .. 641 ( 1920 ) ; Armour v . Lynch Bank , 207 Ky . 203, 268 S. W.
1091 ( 1925 ) .
(3 ) This subsection setting for the criteria for being overdue is
stated in terms of notice , whereas under present law a holder evidently
cannot be in due course if the instrument is in fact overdue . KRS 356.052
(2) . But insofar as these situations constitute dishonor rather than being
overdue , there must be notice under present law to prevent holder from
being in due course . No Kentucky cases have been found on the point of
acceleration constituting notice that an instrument is overdue . Under present
law, if the instrument is negotiated an unreasonable length of time after
issue , the holder is not in due course . KRS 356.053. The only Kentucky
decisions passing on the question of length of time which would be con-
sidered as "reasonable" were Asbury v . Taube , 151 Ку . 142, 151 S. W. 372
(1912) which held that two days was not an unreasonable time for negotia-
tion of a check after its issue , and Fayette Nat. Bank v . Meyers , 211 Ky . 185 ,
277 S. W. 292, ( 1925 ) holding that a nine months ' delay was unreasonable .
There appears to be no conflict insofar as the 30- day presumption set forth
in the Code .

Summary
A holder in due course takes free from :
(1) All claims by any person ; and
(2) All defenses of any party with whom he has not dealt except :
(a) infancy , to the extent it is a defense to a simple contract , and
(b) such other incapacity , duress or illegality as renders the obli-
gation a nullity ; and
(c) such misrepresentation as has induced the party to sign with-
out knowledge or opportunity to learn of its character or
essential terms ; and
(d) discharge in insolvency ; and
( e) any other discharge of which he has notice when he takes .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.015 , 356.016 , 356.057 .
Comment
(1) Preamble and subsection ( 1) are in accord with KRS 356.057 , ex-
cept that "takes" is substituted for "holds " to make clear that defenses may
arise against the holder after transfer , and "claims to it on the part of any
person" is substituted for " defect of title of prior parties ," to make explicit
that a holder in due course is protected against set - offs , garnishments , etc.
This includes any claim for rescission of a prior negotiation , in accordance
with the provisions of the section on reacquisition . ( UCC 3-208 ) .
(2 ) Accord , KRS 356.057. As to undelivered instruments , compare
KRS 356.015 and 356.016 . The rule of KRS 356.015 is reversed by this sec-
tion . KRS 356.016 achieves the same result as this section in a different
fashion . Compare UCC 3-306 and 3-307 .
(a) This paragraph is new . It follows the decisions under KRS
356.057 in providing that the defense of infancy may be as-
serted against a holder in due course , even though its effect is
to render the instrument voidable but not void . Universal
Credit Co. v . Hibbard , 273 Ky . 597, 117 S. W. 2d 583 ( 1928 ) ;
Harris v . Shaffer , 197 Ky . 54, 246 S. W. 26 ( 1922 ) ; 10 C.J.S. ,
Bills and Notes , Sec. 506 ( c ) . This applies only to defenses .
The right of rescission of a transfer of an instrument is cut
off . See UCC 3-207 .
(b) Paragraph (b) is also new . It covers mental incompetence,
guardianship , ultra vires acts or lack of corporate capacity to
do business , any remaining incapacity of married women , or
any other incapacity apart from infancy . In accord , Clark
County National Bank v . Allen , 262 Ky . 236 90 S. W. 2d 17
(1935) ( married woman as co - surety ) ; Alexander & Co. v .
Hazelrigg , 123 Ky . 677, 97 S. W. 353 , ( 1906 ) (note given to
satisfy gambling debt is void even in the hands of a holder in
due course ) ; Whitaker et al. v . Smith , 255 Ky . 339, 72 S. W.
1105 ( 1934 ) (Usury : the instrument is valid but the usurious
interest is not due) . Compare , Rath's Committee v . Smith , 180
154

Section 3-306. Rights of One Not Holder in Due Course .
Summary
A holder not in due course takes subject to :
(1) All valid claims of any person ; and
(2) All defenses available in an action on a simple contract ; and
(3) The defenses of want or failure of consideration , non -performance
of a condition precedent , non -delivery or delivery for a special purpose
( UCC 3-408 ) ; and
(4) the defense that he or a person through whom he holds the in-
strument acquired it by theft , or that payment or satisfaction to such holder
would be inconsistent with the terms of a restrictive indorsement . The claim
of any third person to the instrument is not otherwise available as a defense

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) discusses the rights of a holder in due course regarding negotiable instruments. It outlines defenses that are not available against a holder in due course, such as the lunacy of the maker (if the holder was unaware), violations of the Blue Sky Law, failure to comply with foreign corporation laws, and notes given for corrupt elections. It also touches upon the UCC's provisions regarding notice of discharge and renunciation. Section 3-306 details the rights of a holder who is not a holder in due course, stating they take subject to all valid claims, defenses available in a simple contract action, and specific defenses like want or failure of consideration, non-performance of a condition precedent, non-delivery, or delivery for a special purpose. It also mentions defenses related to theft or inconsistent payment with restrictive indorsements.

Section 3-307 . Burden of Establishing Signatures , Defenses and
Due Course .
Summary
( 1) Unless specifically denied in the pleadings each signature is ad-
mitted . When a signature is put in issue :
(a) the burden of establishing it is on the party claiming under
it; but
(b) the signature is presumed genuine or authorized except where
the signer is dead or incompetent .
(2) When signatures are admitted or established , the holder may
recover upon production of the instrument unless a defense is established.
(3) After it is shown that a defense exists , one claiming rights as
holder in due course has burden of establishing it .
KRS 356.059 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1) is new but is in accord with Kentucky Civil Rules 8.02
and 8.04 . Its purpose is to require a specific denial of the signature in order
to give plaintiff notice of the defense interposed . The form of the denial will
depend upon the local rule , probably in Kentucky it would be in the answer .
While the burden of establishing any signature is on the person claiming
under it, he is aided by the presumption of genuineness except in the cases
stated . See Menton v . Pensacola Bank and Trust Co., 190 F. 126 (6th Cir .
1911) .
Subsection (2) is substituted for the first clause of KRS 356.059 . Once
a signature is proved or admitted , the holder makes his case by production
of the instrument unless other evidence is in .

Kentucky Statutes

Section 3-402 . Signature in Ambiguous Capacity .
Summary
Unless the instrument clearly indicates that a signature is made in
some other capacity it is an indorsement .

Section 3-403 . Signature by Authorized Representative .
Summary
(1) A signature may be made by a representative whose authority
may be established as in other cases . No particular form of appointment
is necessary .
(2) An authorized representative who signs his own name to an
instrument :
(a) is personally obligated if the instrument neither names the

in a representative capacity ;
(b ) except as otherwise established between the immediate par-

Comment
(1) The first sentence of subsection ( 1) is in accord with KRS 356.019
which states that the signature of any party may be made by an agent.
However , Kentucky in adopting the NIL modified Sec. 19 and required
that the authority of the representative must be expressed in a written
instrument . Therefore the second sentence of subsection ( 1) would be in
conflict with KRS 356.019 insofar as the requirement for written authori-
zation before an agent may sign on behalf of his principal .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.020 . However , the NIL makes no provision for
cases where the capacity in which a party signed is ambiguous . It has
been held that where the litigation is between the original parties parol
evidence is admissible to resolve it. See Riordan & Co. v . Thornsbury , 178
Ky . 324 , 198 S. W. 920 ( 1917 ) .
KRS 356.021 on signature by " procuration " is omitted . It is doubtful
whether the omission of KRS 356.021 will change the law . However , as
indicated in Comment 4 to this section of the Code , "per procuration " is
often understood to be the equivalent of "by ." See Clinton v . Hibbs , 202 Ky.
304 , 259 S. W. 356 ( 1924 ) .
Section 3-404 . Unauthorized Signatures .
Summary
( 1) An unauthorized signature is wholly inoperative as that of the
person whose name is signed unless he ratifies it or is precluded from
denying it; but it operates as the signature of the signer in favor of any
person who in good faith pays or takes for value .
(2) An unauthorized signature may be ratified but this does not
affect any rights of the person ratifying against the signer .

The second phrase " or is otherwise precluded " continues the present

signature is forged from recovering . The same word "precluded " is used

Section 3-405 . Impostors ; Signature in Name of Payee .
Summary
(1) An indorsement in the payee's name is effective if:
(a) an impostor by use of the mails or otherwise has induced the
issuance to him or his confederate in the name of the payee ;
(b)
or
a person signing as or for a maker or drawer intends the
payee to have no interest ; or
(c) the drawer's or maker's agent has given him the payee's
name intending the payee to have no such interest .
(2) Nothing in this section shall affect the criminal or civil liability
of the person so indorsing .
KRS 356.009 (3 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1 ) ( a) is new . It rejects the attempted distinction between
imposture by mail and face to face imposture . The distinction resulted in
the loss falling often on a later holder whereas it should fall on the drawer
who thought the impostor and the named payee were one and the same .
Subsection ( 1) ( b ) restates the substance of KRS 356.009 (3) but
limits it to drafts . The test is not the "fictitiousness " of the payee but
whether it is intended that he have any part of the proceeds.
Subsection ( 1) ( c ) is new and extends the rule of KRS 356.009 (3 ) to
cover the "padded payroll " cases .
See generally Mueller & Martin v . Liberty Insurance Bank, 187 Ку . 44,
218 S. W. 465 ( 1920 ) ; Com . v . Farmers ' Deposit Bank , 264 Ky . 839, 95 S. W.
2d 793 ( 1936 ) . The entire section has enlarged KRS 356.009 ( 3 ) . The words
"fictitious or nonexisting person " have been eliminated as misleading , since
the existence or nonexistence of the named payee is not decisive and is
important only as it may bear on the intent that he shall have no interest
in the instrument . The instrument is not made payable to bearer and
indorsements are still necessary to negotiation . The section however recog-
nizes as effective indorsement of the types of paper covered no matter by
whom made .

Section 3-406 . Negligence Contributing to Alteration or
Unauthorized Signature .
Summary
Any person whose negligence substantially contributes to a material
alteration or to the making of an unauthorized signature is precluded from
asserting these defenses against a holder in due course or any person who
pays the instrument in good faith .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord, Citizens' Union National Bank v . Terrell, 244 Ky . 16, 50 S. W.
2d 60 (1932 ) ; Baskett v . Ohio Valley Banking & Trust Co., 214 Ky . 41 , 281
S. W. 1022 ( 1925) ; United States v. Citizens ' National Bank , 40 F. S. 609
(W. D. Ky . 1941 ) .
Section 3-407 . Alteration .
Summary
(1) A material alteration is one which changes the contract of any
party in any respect including any change in:
(a) the number or relations of the parties ; or
(b) an incomplete instrument by unauthorized completion ; or
(c) the writing as signed by addition or removal .
(2) As against any person other than a subsequent holder in due
course:
(a) a fraudulent and material alteration by the holder discharges
any party whose contract is thereby changed unless he assents
or is estopped ;
(b) no other alteration discharges any party and the instrument
is enforceable according to its original tenor , or to the
authority given to complete it.
(3) A subsequent holder in due course may enforce an instrument
according to its original tenor or as completed .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.014 , 356.015 , 356.124 , 356.125 .

one not having the rights of a holder in due course is in accord with
Berryman v . Harris , 196 Ky . 21 , 244 S. W. 68 ( 1922 ) ; Owsley County De-
posit Bank v . Burns , 196 Ky . 359 , 244 S. W. 755 ( 1922 ) ; City of Erlanger v .

S. W. 37 , ( 1906 ) ; Lovelace v . Lovelace , 136 Ky . 452, 124 S. W. 400 ( 1910 ) .
Section 3-409 . Draft Not an Assignment .
Summary
(1) A check or draft is not an assignment of any funds in the hands
of the drawer , and the drawee is not liable until he accepts .
(2) Nothing in this section shall affect any liability arising from
any letter of credit or other obligation which is not an acceptance .
KRS 356.127 , 356.189 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
KRS 356.127 and 356.189 are combined and reworded in this section,
and the rule of the sections retained without change . Accord , Boswell v .
Citizens' Savings Bank , 123 Ky . 485 , 96 S. W. 797 ( 1906 ) ; Ewing v . Citizens '
National Bank, 162 Ky . 551 , 172 S. W. 955 ( 1915 ) ; Kentucky Title Savings
Bank & Trust Co. v . Dunavan , 205 Ky. 801 , 266 S. W. 667 ( 1924 ) .
The language of KRS 356.189 that " the bank is not liable to the holder "
is eliminated as untrue . The drawee is not liable on the instrument until
he accepts but he remains liable to the holder for any other liability . See
UCC 4-302 .
Subsection ( 2) is new and is intended to make it clear that this section
does not affect any other liability that may arise apart from the instrument .
Section 3-410 . Definition and Operation of Acceptance .
Summary
(1) Acceptance is the drawee's signed engagement to honor the draft
as presented . It must be written on the draft , and may be his signature only .
It is operative when completed by delivery or notification .
(2 ) A draft may be accepted although it is unsigned or incomplete ,
or is overdue or dishonored .
(3 ) Where an acceptance of a draft payable at a fixed period after
sight is undated , the holder may supply a date in good faith .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.132 to 356.138 , 356.161 to 356.170 , 356.190 .

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (1957) clarifies that a check or draft is not an assignment of funds and the drawee is not liable until acceptance. It also states that this rule does not affect other liabilities arising from letters of credit or other non-acceptance obligations. It further defines acceptance as the drawee's signed engagement to honor a draft, which must be written on the draft and is operative upon delivery or notification. Drafts can be accepted even if unsigned, incomplete, overdue, or dishonored. If a draft payable at a fixed period after sight is undated, the holder may supply a date in good faith.

Subsection ( 1) by requiring the acceptance to be written upon the
draft eliminates KRS 356.134 and 356.135 providing for collateral accept-
ance by separate writing and virtual acceptance by written promise . The
effect of this requirement is to overturn cases holding that an acceptance
can be made by a separate paper . See Selma Savings Bank v . Webster
County Bank, 182 Ky . 604, 206 S. W. 870 ( 1918 ) ; Roberts v . Drovers' Na-
tional Bank , 199 Ky . 439, 251 S. W. 198 ( 1923 ) .
Even under the Negotiable Instruments Law , the holder could require
that the acceptance be written on the bill , KRS 356.133 . As to the time an
acceptance becomes operative this subsection accords with the definition
of acceptance in KRS 356.190. Most of the value of the concept of "extrinsic "
acceptance is preserved in the article on Documentary Letters of Credit ,
Article 5.
(2) Accord , KRS 356.138 .
(3) This is in accord with UCC 3-116 and KRS 356.014 on incomplete
instruments .
KRS 356.161 to 356.170 providing for acceptance for honor are omitted
as obsolete. The practice arose because of slow communications and has
not been used for a long time . No Kentucky decisions were found on the
subject .
Section 3-411 . Certification of a Check .
Summary
( 1) Certification of a check is acceptance . Certification procured by
a holder discharges the drawer and all prior indorsers .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed, a bank has no obligation to certify a
check .
(3) A bank may certify a check before returning it for lack of proper
indorsement , in which case the drawer is discharged .
KRS 356.187 , 356.188 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1) continues the rule of KRS 356.187 and 356.188 . Accord ,
First National Bank v . Bank of Ravenswood , 141 Ky . 671, 133 S. W. 581
(1911 ) ; Ewing v . Citizens ' National Bank , 162 Ky . 551, 172 S. W. 955 ( 1915 ) .
Subsection (2) is new but it adopts the generally accepted view . Since
a check is a demand instrument calling for payment rather than acceptance ,
the bank is under no duty to certify . It may be liable for breach of its
agreement to certify but it is not liable on the instrument .
Subsection (3 ) is new . It recognizes the practice of certification of a
check returned for indorsement in order to discharge the drawer . It is
consistent with the policy of acceptance of an incomplete instrument .
Section 3-412 . Acceptance Varying Draft.

Summary
( 1 ) When the proffered acceptance varies the draft as presented the
holder may refuse it and treat the draft as dishonored , in which case the
acceptance shall be cancelled .
(2) The terms of the draft are not varied by an acceptance to pay
at any particular bank or place in the continental United States, unless
the acceptance states that the draft is to be paid only at such bank or place .
(3) Where the holder assents to an acceptance varying the terms of
the draft each drawer and indorser who does not affirmatively assent is
discharged .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.139 to 356.142 .
Comment
(1 ) Accord, KRS 356.142. The distinctions between various kinds of
qualified acceptances in KRS 356.141 are eliminated and the rule is made
to apply to any " acceptance that in any manner varies the draft as pre-
sented . " A provision is added that where the holder refuses the conditional
acceptance , the drawee is entitled to have his acceptance cancelled.
(2) Accord, KRS 356.140 .
(3 ) Accord , KRS 356.142 insofar as drawers and indorsers are dis-
charged where the holder assents to an acceptance varying the terms of
the instrument . This section changes KRS 356.142 by requiring affirmative
assent to the acceptance.
Section 3-413 . Contract of Maker , Drawer and Acceptor .
Summary
(1 ) The maker or acceptor engages that he will pay the instrument
according to its tenor at the time of his engagement or as completed pur-
suant to UCC 3-115 on incomplete instruments .
(2 ) The drawer , unless he draws without recourse , engages that he
will pay the draft to the holder or indorser who takes it up .
(3) By making , drawing or accepting , the party admits as against
all subsequent parties the existence of the payee and his then capacity
to indorse .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.060 , 356.061 , 356.062 , 356.013 , 356.014 , 356.015 .
Comment
( 1) Accord, KRS 356.060 and 356.062 . See UCC 3-115 and KRS 356.013 ,
356.014 and 356.015 on incomplete instruments .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.061 .
(3) Accord, KRS 356.060, 356.061 , 356.062 ( 2 ) .

Section 3-414 . Contract of Indorser ; Order of Liability.
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise specified (such as "without recourse " ) the
indorser engages that upon dishonor and any necessary notice and protest
he will pay the instrument according to its tenor at the time of his indorse-
ment to the holder or any subsequent indorser who takes it up even though
the one who takes it up was not obligated to do so .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed, indorsers are liable to one another in
the order in which they indorse , which is presumed to be the order in
which their signatures appear on the instrument .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.038 , 356.044 , 356.066 , 356.067 and 356.068 .
Comment
(1) Accord, KRS 356.038, 356.044 , 356.066 , 356.067 .
(2 ) Accord , KRS 356.068 except that a presumption is added that the
order in which indorsers indorsed is the order in which their signatures
appear on the instrument .
Section 3-415 . Contract of Accommodation Party .
Summary
(1) An accommodation party is one who signs the instrument in any
capacity for the purpose of lending his name to another party to it.
(2) If the instrument is taken for value before it is due, the accom-
modation party is liable in the capacity in which he signed even though
the taker knows of the accommodation .
(3) Oral proof of accommodation is not admissible against a holder
in due course without notice , but it may be admitted in other cases .
(4) An indorsement not in chain of title is notice of accommodation .
(5) An accommodation party is not liable to the party accommodated ,
and has a right of recourse on the instrument against such party .
KRS 356.029 , 356.064 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment

Katterjohn , 137 Ky . 427, 125 S. W. 1071 ( 1910 ) ; Howard v . Southern Na-

Section 3-416 . Contract of Guarantor .
Summary
(1 ) "Payment guaranteed " or equivalent means that the signer en-
gages to pay the instrument if not paid when due without resort to any
other party.
(2) " Collection guaranteed" or equivalent means that the signer will
pay the instrument if the holder cannot collect it from the maker or ac-
ceptor .
(3) Words of guaranty which do not otherwise specify guarantee
payment .
(4) Words of guaranty added to the signature of a sole maker or
acceptor do not affect his liability , but such words added to signature of
one or two or more makers or acceptors show a presumption of accommo-
dation .
(5) When words of guaranty are used, presentment , notice and pro-
test are not necessary to charge the user .
(6 ) Any guaranty written on the instrument is enforceable notwith-
standing any statute of frauds .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This is a new section which is intended to state the commercial under-
standing of words of guaranty . An indorser guaranteeing payment waives
not only presentment , notice and protest , but also demand upon the maker
or drawer ; while his indorsement is still effective as such, his liability is
that of a co-maker . A guaranty of collection also waives presentment , notice
and protest , but requires the holder to proceed against the maker or ac-
ceptor first .
The Kentucky Statute of Frauds (KRS 371.010) does not require the
memorandum to state a consideration ; therefore , subsection (6 ) would not
change it.

Comment
( 1 ) Warranties on Transfer . (Compare UCC 3-417 (2) with KRS
356.065 .) Under the UCC warranties are given by any person who transfers
an instrument and receives consideration , unless it is otherwise agreed. If
the transfer is by delivery only the warranty runs to the transferee ; if the
transfer is by indorsement then the warranty runs to any subsequent holder
who takes the instrument in good faith. Under KRS 356.066 the warranties
of a general indorser ran only to "subsequent holders in due course ."
Under the UCC the warranties run in favor of holders in due course
who by acquisition of instruments have cut off prior defenses ; such a
holder in due course may go against the defrauding payee instead of the
defrauded maker . See UCC 3-417 , Commissioners Comment , Point 9. The
transferor under the UCC does not warrant against difficulties of collection
or insolvency that are not known to him . See UCC 3-417 , Commissioners
Comment , Point 10. By a transfer without recourse the transferor makes
the same warranties , except that he only warrants that he has no knowledge
of any defense of any party that is good against him . UCC 3-417 ( 3 ) .
A selling agent or broker who does not disclose that he is acting as
such gives the warranties prescribed , but if he makes disclosure he war-
rants only his good faith and authority . Under KRS 356.069 it is necessary ,
to avoid giving warranties , to disclose the name of the principal ; under
UCC 3-417 (4) , disclosure that he is acting as selling agent or broker is
sufficient .
(2) Warranties on Presentment . The UCC introduces a new concept
in order to bring within its compass the doctrine of Price v . Neal, 3 Burr .
354 (1762 ) . The case relates to the avoidance of acceptances given and the
recovery of money paid under a mistake of fact. This doctrine has previ-
ously rested on principles of restitution , and is only partially covered in
KRS 356.062 . The new concept is that the person presenting an instrument
for acceptance and payment makes warranties to the party who accepts
or pays .
A complicating factor in interpreting the UCC provisions is that UCC
3-418 , which according to the Commissioners Comment codifies Price v .
Neal, is subordinated to UCC 3-417 on warranties , and both in turn are
made subject to UCC Article 4 on Bank Deposits and Collections , the rules
of which are subject to variation by agreement. The language of UCC 4-207
in the article on Bank Deposits and Collections is similar to UCC 3-418 and
3-417 , but covers all bank deposits. The warranties of UCC 3-417 and 4-207
operate to permit recovery of money only after "final" payment has been
made for other purposes , although this is not made explicit in the UCC .
Thus , when a person receives cash , as in payment over the counter , UCC
3-417 , 3-418 and 4-207 will be determinative of whether the payor can
recover back the money . When a depositor receives credit in his bank
account , however , this may be revoked without reference to these sections ,
within the time limits and under the conditions prescribed in the sections
on deferred posting and the right of chargeback , UCC 4-301 , 4-213 and
4-212 . Only after these time limits have expired will UCC 3-417 , 3-418 , and
4-207 become effective to determine whether the credit can be revoked

that payment would be final and there can be no recovery back unless it is

(a) Forged Indorsements . UCC 3-417 ( 1 ) ( a) continues existing law
to the effect that money paid under a forged indorsement can be recovered
back . See Restatement, Restitution , Sec. 35 ; Farmers National Bank of
Augusta v . Farmers & Traders Bank of Maysville , 159 Ky . 141 , 166 S. W.
986 , ( 1914 ) . UCC 3-417 , Commissioners Comment , Point 3 states :
"3. Subsection ( 1) (a) retains the generally accepted rule that
the party who accepts or pays does not 'admit' the genuineness of
indorsements , and may recover from the person presenting the in-
strument when they turn out to be forged ."
A similar provision in UCC 4-207 ( a) is intended to have a similar result
and to make unnecessary the practice in bank collections of indorsing "prior
indorsements guaranteed ."
...,
The UCC accomplishes this result by imposing on the presenter and
any prior transferor a warranty " that he has a good title to the instrument ."
A payment or acceptance is final unless this warranty is broken . The
question then arises as to whether the UCC clearly provides that the person
who obtains payment of an instrument bearing a forged indorsement does
not have good title . There is no explicit provision on this point , but UCC
3-404 states that " any unauthorized signature is wholly inoperative as that
of the person whose name is signed but it operates as the signature of
the unauthorized signer in favor of any person who in good faith pays the
instrument or takes it for value ." Under this section it would seem that
the person in possession of an instrument bearing a forged indorsement has
good title only to a piece of paper bearing the signature of the forger . It
may be expected that in interpreting UCC 3-417 ( 1) (a) the intent of the
draftsman will be carried out, but a conclusion that this subsection clearly
states existing law cannot be borne out when UCC 3-404 is taken into con-
sideration .
(b) Forged Signatures of Makers and Drawers .
It is assumed in the following discussion that a person who takes an
instrument with knowledge of a forged or unauthorized signature of the
maker or drawer is not a holder in due course . This assumption , however ,
is the subject of some doubt since UCC 3-404 ( 1) provides that "Any un-
authorized signature is wholly inoperative as that of the person whose
name is signed but it operates as the signature of the unauthorized
signer in favor of any person who in good faith pays the instrument or takes
it for value ." If a person "in good faith " takes such an instrument intending
to hold the unauthorized signer and not the person whose name appears on
the instrument , perhaps he is a holder in due course , since there is no
"defense " against the instrument .
"
The results under the UCC in varying factual situations will be :
(1) Holder in due course who , when he presents and receives accept-
ance or payment , is :
(a) without knowledge of the forged signature . Payment

(b) with knowledge of the forged signature . Result is doubt-
ful . A holder in due course acting in good faith does not warrant
that he has no knowledge of forged signatures , except that the
holder in due course of a draft apparently does warrant to an
acceptor that at the same time he obtains the acceptance he has
no knowledge of the drawer's signature being unauthorized .
(2) Holder not in due course, who , when he presents and receives
acceptance or payment , is :
(a) without knowledge of the forged signature . Under
UCC 3-417 ( 1 ) ( b) no warranty has been broken since the warranty
is only against "knowledge of unauthorized signatures ." Yet under
UCC 3-418 the acceptance or payment is not final since the holder is
not a holder in due course . The result , therefore , is in doubt .
(b) with knowledge of the forged signature . Acceptance or
payment not final since the warranty against knowledge of a forged
signature is broken .
The UCC seems to speak of a holder in due course acting in good faith
when he obtains payment of an instrument , although he has knowledge
that the signature of the maker or drawer is forged . If he acts in good faith
he makes no warranty that he has no knowledge of the forgery ; but if he
acts otherwise than in good faith he warrants that has has no knowledge of
the forgery . The section on presentment warranties seems very confused
and to be susceptible to very different interpretations . This is especially
evident when an effort is made to relate the section to UCC 3-418 , which
purports to deal with finality of payment .
(c) Equitable Exceptions to Price v. Neal . As generally applied in
the United States the rule of Price v . Neal carries with it certain equitable
exceptions . If the person receiving payment knows of the mistake of fact
of the payor , that is, that the signature of the drawer or maker is forged
he cannot invoke the rule of Price v . Neal and the payor may recover back
the money . The same is true if the person receiving payment has " reason
to know" ( See Restatement , Restitution , Sec. 34) or if he was negligent .
With reference to these equitable exceptions the Commissioners Com-
ment , UCC 3-418 states :
"4. The section rejects decisions under the original act per-
mitting recovery on the basis of mere negligence of the holder in
taking the instrument . If such negligence amounts to a lack of
good faith .. or to a failure to observe the reasonable commercial
standards applicable to the holder's business or to notice under
the rules relating to notice to a purchaser of an instrument , the
holder is not a holder in due course and is not protected ; but
otherwise the holder's negligence does not affect the finality of the
payment or acceptance ."
The UCC has thus abolished the " equitable exceptions " as such, but has
subsumed some of them under the requirement that the person receiving
payment be a holder in due course . If the person receiving payment should
have purchased with knowledge , or with reason to know , of the forgery ,

or if he purchased with such negligence as to constitute a lack of good faith,
the payor can recover back the money . But , if his purchase is only negli-
gent, but does not constitute lack of good faith, the payment cannot be
recovered back .
Under the UCC the status of the person receiving payment becomes
fixed at the time he takes the instrument . Subsequent events cannot
change that status. If, after taking the instrument but before receiving pay-
ment , the holder in due course learns of the forgery , the situation is quite
anomalous . If he reveals his knowledge to the payor , the payor will of
course not pay, and he will have no recourse by right of action against the
payor . But if he conceals his knowledge from the payor and secures pay-
ment , then, it would seem , he can keep it.
Under another equitable exception to Price v . Neal under present law
a person who has received payment , learning facts from which he has
reason to know of the payor's mistake , is under a duty to notify the payor .
If he fails to do so he will be liable to the payor to the extent that the
payor has been prevented from obtaining restitution from a third person .
See Restatement , Restitution , Sec. 34 (c) . The UCC does not explicitly
cover this equitable exception , but if it should still be effective under the
UCC , we would have an anomalous situation . The holder in due course
would conceal his knowledge of the forgery until he secured payment in
hand , and then he would be under a duty to tell him the truth, so that the
payor could endeavor to recover from the forger .
The question arises as to whether the handling of the Price v . Neal
situation is in accord with the basic principle of "good faith" running
throughout the UCC .
(d) Material Alteration . The warranty under UCC 3-417 ( 1 ) ( c) and
4-207 ( 1) (c) is that the instrument "has not been materially altered ." This
is intended to continue existing law on material alteration , with the possible
exception of situations in which an instrument has been certified after alter-
ation , with respect to which there have been conflicting decisions . The UCC
takes the position that a bank is liable on a certified check according to its
tenor at the time of its certification , and not the tenor at the time the
instrument was originally drawn . This responsibility cannot be avoided
by certifying " payable as originally drawn ." See UCC 3-417 , Commissioners
Comment , Point 5.
The term "material alteration " used in these presentment warranties
seems to be used more in the sense in which it was used in the NIL than
as now used in the UCC itself in UCC 3-407 covering "Alteration " . A
material alteration is of itself ineffective to discharge any party under UCC
3-407 . Only a material alteration by a holder , which is also fraudulent ,
operates as a discharge and then only as respects a party whose contract
is changed and who has not assented or is not precluded from assserting
the defense.
Under the literal language of UCC 3-417 ( 1) ( c) if an instrument has
been materially altered , although not fraudulently , the presentment war-
ranty nevertheless has been broken and payment can be recovered back

party to any extent .
Under UCC 3-407 the completion of an incomplete instrument other-
wise than as authorized is a material alteration , but a holder in due course
may enforce the instrument as completed and other holders may enforce
it according to the authority given . Under the language of UCC 3-417 ( 1 )
(c) and 4-207 ( 1) (c) , however , the presenter and prior transferors of
such instruments completed before certification , break the presentment
warranty . Payment can be recovered back or acceptance avoided .
Disregarding this lack of integration between the sections on present-
ment warranties and the section on alteration the following results would
seem to obtain under UCC 3-417 and 3-407 :
(1) Holder in due course of a draft that was materially
altered at the time the holder in due course received acceptance :
payment is not final, since there is a warranty against the fact of
material alteration , unless transferee is the maker , drawer or ac-
ceptor of the draft, then no warranty is given by a holder in due
course .
(2) Other holders in due course acting in good faith : pay-
ment is final . There is no warranty against material alteration .
(3 ) Other holders in due course not acting in good faith :
payment is not final. There is a warranty against the fact of ma-
terial alteration .
(4) Holder not in due course of a materially altered instru-
ment : acceptance or payment is not final . There is a warranty
against the fact of material alteration .
(e) Insufficient funds . Although not made explicit in the text , final
payment according to the Comment extends to situations in which a drawee
pays because of a mistake as to the status of the drawer's account, there
being in fact insufficient funds .
3. By transferring "without recourse ." Accord , KRS 356.039 and KRS
356.065 . See Larue v . Barbee , 184 Ky . 354, 212 S. W. 142 ( 1919 ) ; Compare
Federal Fidelity Co. v . Royal Mortgage & Finance Co., 252 Ky . 716, 68 S. W.
2d 25 ( 1934 ) .
4. Selling agent or broker . Accord , KRS 356.069 except that it is
limited to selling agent.
Section 3-418 . Finality of Payment or Acceptance .
Summary
Payment or acceptance is final in favor of a holder in due course , or a
person who has in good faith changed his position in reliance on the pay-
ment , except for recovery of bank payments under Article 4 and except
for liability for breach of warranty on presentment .
KRS 356.062 .

Comment
Accord , KRS 356.062 . Price v . Neal, 3 Burr . 1354, 97 Eng . Reprint 871
(1762) ; Deposit Bank of Georgetown v . Fayette National Bank, 90 Ky . 10,
13 S. W. 339 ( 1890 ) ; Farmers National Bank of Augusta v. Farmers &
Traders Bank of Maysville , 159 Ky . 141 , 166 S. W. 986 ( 1914 ) ; Louisa Na-
tional Bank v . Kentucky National Bank , 239 Ky . 302 , 39 S. W. 2d 497 ( 1931 ) .
The rule as to finality is not limited to drawers but applies to a maker
or any other person who pays the instrument . It also applies to the payment
of overdrafts .
See also Comment to UCC 3-417 , supra .
Section 3-419 . Conversion of Instrument ; Innocent Representative .
Summary
(1) An instrument is converted when :
(a) a drawee to whom it is delivered for acceptance refuses to
return it ; or
(b) a person to whom it is delivered for payment refuses to pay
or return ; or
(c) it is paid on a forged indorsement .
(2) In an action against the drawee the measure of liability is the
face amount of the instrument , while in other actions the measure is pre-
sumed to be the face amount .
(3) Subject to provisions of this act concerning restrictive indorse-
ments , a representative who in good faith and in observance of reasonable
commercial standards had dealt with an instrument or its proceeds on
behalf of one not the true owner is not liable for conversion or otherwise
beyond the amount of any proceeds remaining in his hands .
(4) An intermediary bank or payor bank which is not a depository
bank is not liable in conversion solely by reason of the fact that proceeds
of an item indorsed restrictively ( UCC 3-205 and 3-206 ) are not applied
consistently with the restrictive indorsement of an indorser other than its
immediate transferor .
KRS 356.137 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The rule of KRS 356.137, that failure to return an instrument is deemed
an acceptance , has not bee nsatisfactory and is abrogated by this section
in favor of a rule giving the holder an action for conversion . See Kentucky
Title Savings Bank & Trust Co. v . Dunavan , 205 Ky . 801, 266 S. W. 667
(1924) .
Subsection (3) is new and adopts the rule of the better decisions that
a representative dealing in good faith and reasonable commercial standards
is not liable except to the extent of the proceeds remaining in his hands .

if the draft so provides , to waive a dishonor and present again .
(This is inserted as a move to allow uniformity with the law
of South America and Continental Europe by appropriate
drafting ) ;

protest , stamps or writings of drawee and books and records
of banks are admissible as evidence and, when showing the

Section 3-502 . Unexcused Delay ; Discharge .
Summary
(1) Where , without excuse, presentment or notice is delayed beyond
the time it is due :
(a) any indorser is discharged ; and
(b) any drawer or the acceptor of a draft payable at a bank or
the maker of a note payable at a bank who suffers loss
because the drawee or payor bank became insolvent during
the delay may discharge his liability by assigning his rights
against the drawee or bank to the holder ; otherwise he is
not discharged .
(2) An unexcused delay in protest will discharge any drawer or
indorser .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.007 , 356.070 , 356.089 , 356.144 , 356.150 , 356.152 and 356.186 .
Comment
(1) (a) Accord , KRS 356.070 , 356.089, 356.144.
(b) This section extends the rule of KRS 356.186 to any drawer
and to acceptors of draft and makers of notes payable at a
bank and further limits the discharge of such latter persons
to cases where the drawee or payor bank has become insol-
vent and only where the drawer , acceptor or maker gives a
written assignment of this right against the drawee or payor
bank to the holder .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.152 .
Section 3-503 . Time of Presentment .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise provided , time for presentment is determined
as follows :
(a) for an instrument payable at or a fixed period after a stated
date, presentment for acceptance must be made on or before
the date it is payable ;
(b) an instrument payable after sight must be presented for
acceptance or negotiated after date or issue whichever is
later;

Section 3-506. Time Allowed for Acceptance or Payment .
Summary
( 1) Acceptance may be deferred without dishonor until the close of
the next business day following presentment . The holder may also in a good
faith effort to obtain acceptance and without either dishonor of the instru-
ment or discharge of secondary parties allow postponement of acceptance
for an additional business day .
(2) Except in the case of documentary drafts drawn under a letter
of credit allowing a longer time or unless an earlier time is agreed to by
the party to pay , payment may be deferred without dishonor pending rea-
sonable examination but it must be made before the close of business on
the day of presentment .

Comment

ing presentment " and good faith postponement for an additional day for the
24 -hour period in KRS 356.136 . See also KRS 356.137.
(2) The section allows the party to take until the close of buisness on
the day of presentment for a reasonable examination to determine whether
the instrument is properly payable . As to drafts drawn under a letter of
credit , see UCC 5-112 .

It details what the presentor must do, such as exhibiting the instrument, providing reasonable identification and authority, and presenting at the specified or a reasonable place. It also specifies that a signed receipt is required upon full payment and surrender of the instrument. Failure to comply invalidates the presentment, but the presentor is given a reasonable time to comply, with the time for acceptance or payment running from the time of compliance. The subsequent section, Section 3-506, details the time allowed for acceptance or payment, allowing deferral until the close of the next business day for acceptance, and pending reasonable examination for payment, which must be made before the close of business on the day of presentment, unless otherwise agreed.

Section 3-507 . Dishonor ; Holder's Right of Recourse ; Term
Allowing Re -Presentment .
(1) Dishonor occurs when :
Summary
(a) upon presentment , due acceptance or payment is refused or
cannot be made within the prescribed time or in the case of
bank collections the instrument is returned by the midnight
deadline ( UCC 4-301 ) ; or
(b) presentment is excused and the instrument is not accepted or
paid.
(2) Subject to notice and protest , upon dishonor the holder has an
immediate right of recourse against the drawers and indorsers .
(3) Return for lack of indorsement is not dishonor .
(4) A term allowing a stated time for re-presentment gives the holder
an option to waive the dishonor without affecting the liability of secondary
parties and he may re-present up to the end of the stated time .
KRS 356.083 , 356.149 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) (a) Accord , KRS 356.083 ( 1 ) , 356.149 ( 1) .
(b) Accord , KRS 356.083 ( 2 ) , 356.149 (2 ) .
(2) Accord , KRS 356.084 , 356.151 .
(3) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . However , see Con-
tinental National Bank & Trust Company v . Olney National Bank, 33 F. 2d
437 ( C.C.A. 7th Cir ., Ill . 1929) and Opinions in 2 Paton's Digest , p. 2059 .
(4) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . This section leaves
open the question of who are secondary parties "bound by the term." In
this connection note that KRS 356.110 has been omitted from this article .
Section 3-508 . Notice of Dishonor .
Summary
(1) Notice of dishonor may be given to any person who may be liable
on the instrument by the holder or any person who has received notice or
any other person liable on the instrument . In addition an agent or bank
may give notice to its principal or customer or to another agent or bank .
(2 ) Notice must be given by a bank before its midnight deadline and
by any other person before midnight of the third business day after dishonor
or receipt of notice .
(3 ) Notice may be given in any reasonable manner . It may be oral or
written and in any terms which identify the instrument and state the dis-
honor . Misdescription which does not mislead does not vitiate the notice .
Sending the instrument bearing a stamp , ticket or writing stating acceptance
or payment refused or sending a notice of debit is sufficient .

Part 6
DISCHARGE
Introductory Comment
(1) NIL Topics Covered : Discharge , NIL Title 1 , Article 8 (KRS
356.119 through 356.123 ) ; payment in due course, NIL Sec. 88 (KRS 356.088 ) ;
effect of tender NIL Sec . 70 (KRS 356.070) ; striking out endorsements, NIL
Sec . 48 (KRS 356.048 ) .
(2) NIL Topics Covered Elsewhere : material alteration , (KRS 356.124
and 356.125 ) covered in Part 4, UCC 3-406 under liability of parties-effect
of material alteration .
(3) NIL Topics Omitted :
(a) "Payment in due course," NIL Sec. 88 (KRS 356.088 ) . The en-
tire concept that payment must be "at or after maturity , " and
"in due course" is eliminated . Payment before maturity will
be a personal defense , and as there can be no holder in due
course at or after maturity a payment then creates a defense
which cannot be cut off ;
(b) "Payment for honor ", NIL Secs . 171 through 177 (KRS 356.171
through 356.177) , as obsolete ;
(c) Burden of proof as to unintentional cancellation , NIL Sec. 123
(KRS 356.123 ) .
(4) New Topics :
(a) A provision ( UCC 3-606 ) making it clear that suretyship de-
fenses are available to indorsers and that impairment of re-
course or security will discharge secondary parties ;
(b) A provision (UCC 3-602 ) making all discharges personal de-
fenses to the party discharged and so not available against a
holder in due course .

(e) reacquisition of the instrument by prior party (UCC 3-208 ) ; or
(f) fraudulent and material alteration ( UCC 3-407 ) ; or

Section 3-602 . Effect of Discharge Against Holder in Due Course .
Summary
No discharge provided by this article is effective against a subsequent
holder in due course unless he has notice thereof when he takes the in-
dorsement .

Comment
This is a new provision making clear that all discharges are only per-
sonal defenses , effective against a subsequent holder in due course only if

Section 3-603 . Payment or Satisfaction .
Summary
(1) The liability of any party is discharged to the extent of his pay-
ment or satisfaction to the holder even though it is made with knowledge of
a claim of another person to the instrument unless prior to such payment or
satisfaction the person making the claim either supplies indemnity deemed
adequate by the party seeking the discharge or enjoins payment or satisfac-
tion by order of a court of competent jurisdiction in an action in which the
adverse claimant and the holder are parties . This subsection does not, how-
ever , result in the discharge of the liability :

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) outlines conditions under which a party's liability on a negotiable instrument is discharged. It covers unexcused delay in presentment, notice, or protest, as well as other acts or agreements that would discharge a simple contract for payment. It also addresses situations where parties are discharged due to reacquiring the instrument or being discharged from liability except for impairment of recourse or security. A new provision clarifies that discharges are personal defenses and only effective against a subsequent holder in due course if they have notice of the discharge. The section on payment or satisfaction states that a party's liability is discharged to the extent of their payment or satisfaction to the holder, even if they are aware of another person's claim, unless the claimant provides adequate indemnity or obtains a court order enjoining payment.

KRS 356.051 , 356.088 , 356.119 , 356.121 and 356.171 through 356.177 .
Comment
(1) Amends KRS 356.088, 356.051 and 356.119 . Formerly , discharge by
"payment in due course" took place only where the payor had no notice that
the holder's title was "defective " . The UCC provides for discharge by pay-
ment even with notice " of a claim of another person to the instrument " ,
unless the adverse claimant supplies the payor indemnity or secures an in-
junction. But , ( 1) ( a ) provides that liability is not discharged where the
party pays in bad faith one who acquired the instrument by theft or who
holds from one who so acquired it. ( 1 ) ( b ) states a party is not discharged
if he pays a holder of an instrument restrictively endorsed and the restric-
tive indorsement is not consistent with the terms of the restrictive indorse-
ment .
(2) Amends KRS 356.121 and repeals KRS 356.171 through 356.177 .
Under the UCC , anyone who pays with the consent of the holder can recover
on the instrument . See Roberts v . Byars , 210 Ky . 788, 276 S. W. 839. ( 1925 ) .
Section 3-604 . Tender of Payment .
Summary
(1) Any party making tender of full payment at or after maturity is
discharged as to subsequent interest , costs and attorney's fees .
(2) The holder's refusal of tender wholly discharges any party who
has a right of recourse against the party making the tender .
(3) Where the maker or acceptor of an instrument other than a de-
mand instrument is able and ready to pay as specified in the instrument it
is equivalent to tender .
KRS 356.070 , 356.120 .
Kentucky Statutes

( 1) This is a new provision but it states the generally accepted rule .

Comment
The words "any party to the instrument " are used to make it clear that
the suretyship defenses here provided are available to any party who is in
the position of a surety such as an accommodation maker known to the
holder and not just the parties "secondarily liable " . Partial release, dis-
charge or impairment operates pro tanto for the party having right of re-
course . The assent of the party may be given in advance and is commonly
incorporated in the instrument , or it may be given afterward . It requires
no consideration . The latent surety cannot take advantage of this section
since the holder must have knowledge of the party's capacity and he does
not discharge when he is ignorant of the relation . An extension of time and
covenant not to sue are both covered by the language "agrees to suspend the
right to enforce" .
Paragraph (b) of subsection ( 1) is new . It states a suretyship defense
which is generally recognized as being available to indorsers and accom-
modation parties .
Subsection ( 2) spells out the fact that where there is such reservation
the indorser's rights are saved as well as the holders right against him .
Part 7
COLLECTION OF DOCUMENTARY DRAFTS
Introductory Comment
The common practice where one international bank draws a draft on
an account it carries in another international bank , is for the drawer bank
to send to the drawer bank a letter of advice that a draft has been drawn
and will be forthcoming . This helps protect the drawer bank from the
European rule that a bank paying a check in good faith and in ordinary
course can charge its depositor's account notwithstanding forgery . The UCC
clarifies such practices .

This section is a statutory enactment of established international bank-

yet the drawee may protect the delinquent drawer's credit .
Subsection (2) clarifies the meaning of the charging of drawer's ac-
count upon receipt of the letter . No trust or assignment is intended . The
debit relates only to interest .

Section 3-801 . Drafts in a Set.
Summary
(1) The whole of the parts of a draft in a set , each part of which is
numbered and expressed to be an order only if no other part is honored , is
one draft, but a taker of a part may become a holder in due course of the
draft.
(2 ) A person negotiating , indorsing or accepting any part becomes
liable to a holder in due course of that part as if it were a set but as be-
tween holders of the parts , the holder whose title first accrues has all rights
to the draft and its proceeds .
(3) The first presented part of a draft in a set is entitled to payment
or acceptance . Acceptance of later parts renders drawer liable thereon
under subsection ( 2) . As to the holder and the drawee , payment of later
part of a sight draft has same effect as payment of a check notwithstanding
a stop order .
(4) Except as provided in this section, discharge of any part of a draft
in a set discharges the draft (UCC 4-407 ) .

Section 3-802 . Effect of Instrument on Obligation for Which It Is
Given .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed , where an instrument is taken for an
obligation :
(a) the obligation is discharged pro tanto if a bank is the drawer ,
maker or acceptor of the instrument and there is no recourse
against the obligor ; and
(b) otherwise the obligation is suspended pro tanto until the in-
strument is due or , if a demand one , is presented . In case of
dishonor , action may be had on either the instrument or the
obligation . Discharge of the obligor on the instrument also
discharges him on the obligation .

Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is intended to settle conflicts as to the effect of instruments
given in settlement of obligations (payment by check ) . Unless otherwise
agreed , if a bank is drawer , maker or acceptor on an instrument and there
is no recourse on the instrument against the underlying obligor the obliga-
tion is discharged pro tanto . Thus the certification of a check procured by
the holder discharges the drawer both on the check and on the underlying
obligation .
In other cases the taking of an instrument "suspends " the obligation
pro tanto until the instrument is due , or until presentment in the case of
demand instruments . According to the Commissioners Comment , the sus-
pension also suspends the running of the Statute of Limitations . If the in-
strument is dishonored action may be brought either on the instrument or
underlying obligation . Discharge of the underlying obligor on the instru-
ment also discharges him on the obligation . The taking of an uncertified
check in good faith , if presented in the time prescribed in the UCC , is not
an extension of time on the original obligation so as to discharge a surety.
Section 3-803 . Notice to Third Party .
Summary
Where a defendant is sued for breach of an obligation for which a
third person is answerable over under this article he may give the third
person written notice of the litigation , and the person notified may then give
similar notice to any other person who is answerable over to him under this
article . If the notice states that the person notified may come in and de-
fend and that if the person notified does not do so he will in any action
against him by the person giving the notice be bound by any determination
of fact common to the two litigations , then unless after seasonable receipt
of the notice the person notified does come in and defend he is so bound .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Compare the Kentucky Rules of Civil Procedure as to Interpleader and
Third Party practice .

Section 3-805 . Instruments Not Payable to Order or to Bearer .
Summary
This article applies to any instrument whose terms do not preclude
transfer and which is otherwise negotiable within this article but which is
not payable to order or to bearer , except that there can be no holder in due
course of such instrument .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comments
This section covers the "non -negotiable instrument " as distinguished
from the instrument which by its terms is not negotiable . A " non -negotiable
instrument " is one which meets all the requirements as to form of a nego-
tiable instrument except that it is not payable to order or to bearer . The
typical example is a check made "Pay to A" . It is not a negotiable instru-
ment but it is still a mercantile speciality which differs from a simple con-
tract , and is governed by the law merchant . This has been the rule before
the NIL and was continued under it . Since it contains no words of negotia-
bility there can be no holder in due course so that provisions specially
applicable to a holder in due course do not apply . Otherwise such an in-
strument is treated as a negotiable one.

ARTICLE FOUR
BANK DEPOSITS AND COLLECTIONS
Part 1
General Provisions and Definitions
Introductory Comment
Part 1 of Article 4 contains general provisions and definitions applicable
to bank deposits and collections . Although these provisions are for the
most part innovations insofar as Kentucky statutory law is concerned , they
actually present no great change but are merely amplications on the usage
of terms.
Enactment of Article 4 would necessitate the repeal of KRS Chapter
357 , entitled, "Bank Collection Code ."
Section 4-101 . Short Title .
Summary
This Article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial
Code Bank Deposits and Collections .
Section 4-102 . Applicability .
Summary
(1) Items within Article 4 which are within the scope of Article 3 and
8 are also subject to the latter articles . In the event of conflict , the pro-
visions or Article 4 govern those of Article 3, but the provisions of Article
8 govern those of Article 4.
(2) The liability of a bank for action or non -action with respect to
any item handled by it for purposes of presentment , payment or collection
is governed by the law of the place where the bank is located. In the case
of action or non -action by or at a branch or separate office of a bank , its
liability is governed by the law of the place where the branch or separate
office is located .
Comment
(1 ) Subsection ( 1) restates the usual rule of interpretation that the
particular governs the general . It should be noted that various provisions
of Article 3 should be consulted in connection with any legal questions
arising with respect to items being collected through banks in the form
of checks , drafts , notes , or certificates of deposit, since such items will also
be within the scope of Article 3. Subsection ( 1) expressly provides that

However , in some instances, items in the course of collection will also be
investment securities within the scope of Article 8 and in that case the
provisions of Article 8 will govern those of Article 4.
(2) Subsection (2) is in substantial accord with the effect of the
Restatement , Conflicts of Laws , Secs. 349 and 336 which are themselves in
conformity with the general rules of conflicts of law as to contracts . The
subsection , although criticized on the ground that it applies a different
conflict of laws rule especially for the benefit of banks (see Beutel , The
Proposed Uniform Commercial Code Should Not Be Adopted , 61 Yale L. J.
334 ) , appears to be sound, for the reason that persons using an ambulatory
instrument must know that action will be taken with respect to it in other
jurisdictions , especially in the jurisdiction where it is to be paid . The
section seems to be also in accord with prior case law in other jurisdictions .
Blackfeet Livestock Co. v . Northwestern National Bank, 138 Or . 530 , 5 Pac
2d 702 ( 1931 ) ; New York N. H. N H. R. Co. v . First National Bank of
Bridgeport , 105 Conn . 33, 134 Atl . 223 ( 1926 ) .
For a discussion of the conflicts of laws problems connected with Article
4 see Clarke & Bailey , Bank Deposits and Collections (A. L. I. Committee
on Continuing Legal Education ) .
Section 4-103 . Variation by Agreement ; Measure of Damage ;
Certain Action Constituting Ordinary Care .
Summary
(1) The provisions of Article 4 may be varied by agreement , except
to disclaim liability or limit damage for a bank's lack of good faith or
failure to exercise ordinary care ; but the parties may by agreement deter-
mine the standards by which such responsibility is to be measured if such
standards are not manifestly unreasonable .
(2) Federal reserve regulations and operating letters , clearing house
rules , etc. , have the effect of agreements , whether specifically assented to
by all the parties or not .
(3 ) Action or non -action approved by Article 4 or pursuant to Federal
Reserve regulations or operating letters constitutes the exercise of ordinary
care and , in the absence of special instructions , action or non -action con-
sistent with clearing house rules , etc. , or with general banking usage not
disapproved by Article 4, constitutes prima facie the exercise of ordinary
care .
(4) Approval of some procedures by Article 4 does not amount to
disapproval of other procedures .
(5) Measure of damages for failure to exercise ordinary care in
handling an item is the amount of the item reduced by amount which could
not have been realized by the use of ordinary care, and where there is bad
faith it includes other damages , suffered by the party as a proximate
consequence .

KRS 357.050 , 357.060 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) is in substantial accord with the rule as stated
9 C.J.S. , Banks & Banking , Sec. 216 :
"The parties are ordinarily free to contract as to the conditions
under which a bank will collect and credit paper , and their agree-
ment made will in general determine their rights and duties . This
is subject to the rule that a bank cannot exempt itself from liability
or its own negligence ...”
The present Kentucky banking code also provides that certain specified
actions are subject to variation by agreement of the parties (KRS 357.020 ,
357.040 ) , whereas subsection ( 1) confers a blanket power to vary all pro-
visions of the article by agreements of the ordinary kind, except that no
agreement can disclaim a bank's responsibility or limit the measure of
damages for its own lack of good faith or failure to exercise ordinary care.
In connection with the aforementioned exceptions, it should be noted that
nowhere is the term "ordinary care" defined , but the Commissioners Com-
ment points out that it is used in its normal tort meaning and not in any
special sense relating to bank collections . The term "good faith " is defined
in UCC 1-201 ( 19 ) as "honesty in fact in the conduct or transaction con-
cerned . " UCC 1-203 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance
and enforcement of every contract within the terms of the Code . That
section is clearly applicable to Article 4 and may not be varied by agree-
ment.
Under this subsection there would be two types of agreements capable
of varying the provisions of Article 4. The first kind of agreement per-
mitted would be one resulting from the direct relation of the parties , that
is, an agreement of the ordinary kind. Such an agreement would affect
only the parties thereto but would be binding as between them, taking
into account the effect of adoption , ratification , estoppel, etc. It might relate
to specific items or to items of a particular class. Some common examples
of such permitted agreements would be direct agreements between banks
and depositors with respect to the collection of items drawn on the bank
of deposit or on other banks or with respect to the payment of checks drawn
by the depositor .
The subsection also permits "indirect " agreements , as where the de-
positor authorizes a particular type of collection procedure on the part of
any bank in the collection chain .
The effectiveness of such an agreement of the ordinary kind , as between
the parties thereto , would be tested by the general rules of law governing
the existence and validity of a simple contract , taking into account such
factors as capacity to contract , fraud , duress and consideration .
Besides agreements on deposit tickets , other examples of agreements
of the ordinary kind might include legends on collection letters , on items
sent for collection , on signature cards or on pass books . For illustrative
cases under prior law in other jurisdictions see , Commissioners Comment

instructions by a customer to his bank as to the honoring or dishonoring of

It should be noted that subsection (1 ) permits broad variation of the
provisions of Article 4 by ordinary agreements between the affected parties,
subject to requirements of good faith and ordinary care . The good faith
limitation , would probably exist , even in the absence of specific statutory
requirement . The limitations would seem to give adequate protection to a
customer in any controversy with a bank involving an agreement contained
on a form drawn up by the bank , particularly since a court would also

the party who drafted it.
(2) Subsection (2) provides that certain types of agreements , of what
might be considered an "official " or "quasi - official" nature , may vary the
provisions of Article 4 and may affect the rights of other interested parties
who have not specifically assented thereto , subject to good faith and ordi-
nary care limitations .
The meaning of the term "Federal Reserve regulations and operating
letters , clearing house rules , and the like" may be summarized as follows
(See also Commissioners Comment 3 to UCC 4-103 ) :
(a) Federal Reserve regulations : Certain Federal Reserve
regulations issued pursuant to statutory authority by the Board
of Governors of the Federal Reserve System covering the handling
through Federal Reserve banks of items in the process of collection .
The current Federal Reserve regulations respecting bank collections
are denominated as Regulation G. Collection of Non -cash Items ; and
Regulation J, Check Clearing and Collection . It is these regulations
or others devised in the future respecting collection of items which
will have the effect of agreements , even though not specifically
assented to by all the parties concerned with the collection of a
particular item .
(b) Federal Reserve operating letters : Letters issued by in-
dividual Federal Reserve banks pursuant to Federal Reserve regu-
lations . Those relating to bank collections consist of statements
of the terms under which the issuing Federal Reserve banks will
handle items for collection . Also, the letters contain instructions
to other banks relating to the collection of items handled by Federal
Reserve banks in their course of collection . The current practice of
the Federal Reserve banks is to issue one or more of such letters
relating to bank collections . At present they are largely uniform
in scope.
(c) Clearing House Rules : Rules adopted for the convenience
of members of the clearing house primarily to expedite the collec-
tion of items through clearing house facilities .
(d) "And the like " : This phrase is meant to cover agreements
between associations or agencies exercising functions similar to
those of clearing houses , or organizations which may be devised

It outlines that ordinary agreements between parties, as well as official or quasi-official agreements like Federal Reserve regulations, operating letters, and clearing house rules, can alter the terms of bank collections. These variations are subject to limitations of good faith and ordinary care. The text explains the meaning of these various types of agreements.
