Section 5-109 . Issuer's Obligation to Its Customers .
Summary
(1) An issuer's obligation to its customer includes good faith and ob-
servance of any general banking usage but unless otherwise agreed does not
include liability or responsibility :
(a) for performance of the underlying contract for sale or other
transaction between the customer and the beneficiary ; or
(b) for any act or omission of any person other than itself or its
own branch or for loss or destruction of a draft , demand or
document in transit or in the possession of others ; or
(c) based on knowledge or lack of knowledge of any usage of any
particular trade .
(2) An issuer must examine documents with care so as to ascertain
that on their face they appear to comply with the terms of the credit but
unless otherwise agreed assumes no liability or responsibility for the genu-
ineness , falsification or effect of any document which appears on such ex-
amination to be regular on its face .
(3 ) A non -bank issuer is not bound by any banking usage of which it
has no knowledge .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) relieves the issuing bank of any responsibility for
acts of its correspondents . This release of liability is frequently effected by
the customer designating all parties involved , other than the issuer , as his
agents rather than as agents of the issuer . Letters issued subject to the
Uniform Customs might automatically incorporate similar releases found in
Articles 11 , 12, 13 and 14 .
(2 ) Subsection ( 2) restates the rule of Article 9 of the Uniform Cus-
toms : "Banks must examine all documents and papers with care so as to
ascertain that on their face they appear to be in order " ; and the rule of
Article 11 of the Uniform Customs :

Section 5-110 . Availability of Credit in Portions ; Presenter's
Reservation of Lien or Claim .
Summary
( 1) Unless otherwise specified , a beneficiary may use a credit in such
portions as he desires .
(2) Unless otherwise specified a person presenting a documentary
draft or demand for payment under a credit relinquishes all claim to the
documents , and a person transferring such draft or demand or causing such
presentment authorizes such relinquishment . An explicit reservation of
claim makes the draft or demand non-complying .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) codifies current commercial custom . Letter of
credit forms frequently allow use of a credit in portions , and the Uniform
Customs expressly permit honor upon partial shipment ( Uniform Customs ,
Art. 6) .
(2) Subsection ( 2) recognizes the basic purpose of a documentary
letter of credit . It is designed to permit a buyer to obtain goods , while at the
same time assuring a distant seller of prompt payment . If the drafts are
duly honored , its purpose is accomplished , and the buyer is entitled to docu-
ments without reservation of any claim or lien .
Section 5-111 . Warranties on Transfer and Presentment .
Summary
( 1) Unless otherwise agreed , the beneficiary by transferring or pre-
senting a documentary draft or demand for payment warrants that the
necessary conditions of the credit have been complied with in addition to
any warranties arising under Articles 3, 4, 7 and 8 .
( 2 ) Unless otherwise agreed , a negotiating , advising , confirming , col-
lecting or issuing bank presenting or transferring a draft or demand for
payment under a credit warrants only the matters warranted by a collecting
bank under Article 4 and any such bank transferring a document warrants
only the matters warranted by an intermediary under Articles 7 and 8.

Section 5-112 . Time Allowed for Honor or Rejection ;
Withholding Honor or Rejection by Consent ;
" Presenter " .
Summary
(1) A bank to which a documentary draft or demand for payment is
presented under a credit may without dishonor of the draft, demand or
credit:
(a) defer honor until the close of the third banking day following
receipt of the documents ; and
(b) further defer honor if the presenter consents thereto .
Failure to honor within the time specified constitutes dishonor of the draft
or demand and of the credit .
(2) Upon dishonor the bank may unless otherwise instructed fulfill its
duty to return the draft or demand and the documents by holding them at
the disposal of the presenter and sending him an advice to that effect .
(3 ) "Presenter " means any person presenting a draft or demand for
payment for honor under a credit even though that person is a confirming
bank or other correspondent which is acting under an issuer's authorization .
KRS 356.136 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) Subsection ( 1) extends the time now allowed under the Negotiable
Instruments Law within which the drawee is permitted to consider whether
to dishonor or to accept the draft . KRS 356.136 allows the drawee 24 hours
after presentment in which to decide whether he will accept a bill of ex-
change. This time extension is made only as to drafts under a letter of credit .
It takes time to examine the accompanying documents , additional time which
is not allowed in the case of ordinary drafts . UCC 3-506 requires acceptance
in the latter case by the close of the next business day following present-
ment .
Section 5-113 . Indemnities .
Summary
(1) A bank seeking to obtain (whether for itself or another ) honor ,
negotiation or reimbursement under a credit may give an indemnity to in-
duce such honor , negotiation or reimbursement .

Comment
Ordinarily , a bank is not permitted to act as surety or guarantor of a
paper in which it has no beneficial interest . Nowell v . Equitable Trust Co. ,
249 Mass . 585, 144 Ν.Ε. 749 ( 1924) ; 8 Zollman , Banks and Banking , Sec. 5122 .

that a bank seeking payment or negotiation may give indemnity as a proper
part of banking business . Without this provision there might be some ques-
tion as to whether such indemnities were incident to the bank's transaction
of business .
Subsection (2) sets out some of the legal effects of an indemnity agree-
ment .
Section 5-114 . Issuer's Duty and Privilege to Honor ;

conform to the underlying contract for sale or other contract between the
customer and the beneficiary . The issuer is not excused from honor of such
a draft or demand by reason of an additional general term that all docuи-

specified documents must be satisfactory to it .

ments to the underlying sales contract is not ground for dishonor . See ,
O'Meara v . National Park Bank , 239 N.Y. 386, 146 Ν.Ε. 636 ( 1925 ) ; Uniform
Customs , Arts . 1, 9 and UCC 5-102 ( 1 ) .
(2) This subsection is in accord with the law as stated in 9 C.J.S. ,
Banks & Banking , Sec. 178b :
"The bank deals in documents , not merchandise ; it undertakes that
it will pay any draft drawn and negotiated pursuant to the terms of
the letter of credit ; its liability on its letter of credit does not de-
pend on the terms of the sales contract which gave rise to the letter
of credit , but it may pay on the required documents , and , in the
absence of an express provision in the letter of credit to that effect ,
it is not required to look beyond the accompanying documents to
ascertain whether the sales contract has actually been complied
with ."
There is some question as to whether this subsection may not incor-
porate a change from present law. Again, turning to C.J.S. , ( supra , p . 390)
in the absence of Kentucky cases in point it is found that if a bank knows
that a document is false or illegal , even though correct in form , it cannot be
called upon to recognize such document as complying with the terms of the
letter of credit . Under the subsection , even if notified , of an alleged forgery
or fraud , the bank is not obligated to endanger its own credit on the basis of
a question of fact which is in any case disputed and difficult of proof. So ,
the bank may honor the claim against itself , in spite of such a notice , and is
entitled to reimbursement for such honor . However , this is subject to the
power of a court to enjoin honor in the circumstances described in the
section.

Summary
(1) When an issuer wrongfully dishonors a draft or demand for pay-

to any documents the rights of a person in the position of a seller ( UCC

resale or other utilization is made the documents , goods or other subject
matter involved in the transaction must be turned over to the issuer on pay-
ment of judgment .
(2) When an issuer wrongfully cancels or repudiates a credit before
presentment of a draft or demand for payment drawn under it, the benefici-
ary has the rights of a seller after anticipatory repudiation by the buyer
under UCC 2-610 if he learns of the repudiation in time reasonably to avoid
procurement of the required documents . Otherwise the beneficiary has an
immediate right of action for wrongful dishonor .

Sec . 183. Kentucky courts recognize an action for anticipatory repudiation
of a contract , therefore subsection ( 2) appears to be consistent with existing
law .
Section 5-116 . Transfer and Assignment .
Summary
(1) The right to draw under a credit can be transferred or assigned
only when the credit is expressly designated as transferable or assignable.

ARTICLE SIX
BULK TRANSFERS
Introductory Comment
The original Bulk Sales Law was enacted in Kentucky in 1904 and is
now KRS Chapter 377 ( enacted in 1948) . Almost all of the other states have
such a law although there is no uniformity of provisions . The purpose of
bulk statutes is to require the seller to give warning to his creditors that he
intends to dispose of the bulk of his property . The statutes are of three
types: the first, such as the Missouri Statute , requiring notice to creditors ;
the second , such as we have in Kentucky , requiring , in addition to notice,
that the buyer make certain that the proceeds of the sale be applied to the
debts ; and the third, such as the California statute, which uses a public
record as a means of notifying creditors of the impending sale.
The chief objection to a Bulk Sales Law is that it impedes the legitimate
sale of a business . Further, it sometimes serves as a trap for the inexperi-
enced buyer . This article attempts to minimize such objections by requiring
compliance with the Bulk Sales Act only in the case where fraud is more
likely to occur . Generally speaking , this article makes no major change in
the present Kentucky law, with the exception that there is no penalty pro-
vision in the article similar to that found in KRS 377.990 .
Section 6-101 . Short Title .
Summary
This article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial
Code-Bulk Transfers .
Section 6-102 . “ Bulk Transfer " ; Transfers of Equipment ;
Enterprises Subject to This Article ; Bulk
Transfers Subject to This Article .
Summary
(1) A "bulk transfer " is a transfer in bulk and not in the ordinary
course of business of a major part of the materials , supplies , merchandise or
other inventory of an enterprise subject to this article .
(2) A transfer of a substantial part of the equipment is a bulk transfer
only if made in connection with a bulk transfer of inventory .
(3 ) All enterprises where the principal business is the sale of mer-
chandise from stock , including manufacturers , are subject to this article .
(4) Except as otherwise limited all bulk transfers are subject to this
article .
KRS 377.010 .
Kentucky Statutes

KRS Chapter 377. Subsection ( 1) identifies the transfers covered by this
article which are further limited by UCC 6-103 , infra. KRS 377.010 uses the
language ". whole or large part of any stock of goods , wares or mer-
chandise of any kind or fixtures in bulk ... otherwise than in the ordinary
course of trade and in the regular and usual prosecution of the seller's
business ." This definition appears to be substantially in accord with the
Code definition . Subsection (2) limits the application of this article in cases
of bulk transfers of equipment only to cases where such transfer is in
connection with a bulk transfer of inventory . There is no similar limitation
in the present Kentucky statute .
Subsection ( 3) defines the business covered in the article . These are the
ones whose principal business is the sale of merchandise and not a service .
This exclusion of businesses selling services is in accord with present Ken-
tucky law. See St. Matthews Motor Co. v. Schnepp , 306 Ку . 823 , 209 S. W.
2d 481 ( 1948 ) holding that the bulk sales law is intended to apply only to
the ordinary merchant and his usual merchandise .
Section 6-103 . Transfers Excepted From This Article .
Summary
The following transfers are not subject to this article :
(1) Transfers for security ;
(2) Assignments for the benefit of creditors ;
(3 ) Transfers in settlement or realization of a lien or security interest ;
(4) Sales by executors , administrators , receivers , trustees in bank-
ruptcy or judicial sales ;
( 5) Sales made in the course of judicial or administrative proceedings
for the dissolution or reorganization of a corporation and of which notice is
sent to the corporation creditors pursuant to order of the court or adminis-
trative agency ;
( 6 ) Transfers to existing business which is bound to pay all debts , give
notice to that effect and is solvent ;
(7 ) Transfers to successor business if notice is given and successor as-
sumes debts and receives nothing except an interest junior to the creditors ;
(8 ) Transfers of exempt property .
KRS 377.010 ( 3) .

Comment
(1) The purpose of this section is to state the transfers which are
excepted from the article even though they might be within the general
definition of UCC 6-102 . Subsection ( 1 ) excludes all security transactions
since under the Code they are covered by Article 9. The Kentucky stat-

include as a transfer the creation of a security interest . However , in other

the debts and is solvent after assuming them, there is no reason to subject
the transaction to the delay and bother of this article . However , compare
Riedling v . Ross & Zeitz , 213 Ky . 187 , 280 S. W. 923 ( 1926 ) . There A took
over B's stock of goods, keeping it and its proceeds separately from his own ,
furnished money wherewith to compromise the claims of certain creditors ,
and collected outstanding accounts . This was held to show a bulk sale under
the Kentucky statute .
(7) Subsection (7) is new and covers certain changes in ownership of
a business . Within the prescribed limits the exclusion is probably justified .
The creditor is protected , or at least no worse off, because both the original
debtor and the new enterprise are bound , the property is still subject to the
debt , and the original debtor has taken nothing except an interest junior to
the creditors . See , Gwin Bros. & Co. v . Peoples General Store , 269 Ky . 813 ,
108 S. W. 2d 1001 ( 1937) .

Section 6-104 . Schedule of Property , List of Creditors .
Summary
(1) Except as to auction sales , a bulk transfer is ineffective against a
creditor unless :
(a) a list of creditors is properly prepared ; and
(b) a schedule of property is prepared ; and
(c) the transferee preserves the list and schedule for six months
and permits inspection and copying by any creditor or files
them in (here designate a public office ) .
(2) The list must be signed and sworn to by the transferor . It must
contain the names and addresses of all creditors together with amount due
if known and also the names of all persons known by the transferee to be
asserting claims even though disputed .

Comment
The requirement that the transferee obtain from the transferor a sworn

Section 6-105 . Notice to Creditors .
Summary
A bulk transfer subject to this article , except one made by auction sale
( UCC 6-108 ) , is ineffective against any creditor of the transferor unless the
transferee gives notice of the transfer in the manner and to the persons
hereafter provided (UCC 6-107 ) at least 10 days before he takes possession of
the goods or pays for them .

Comment
This section requires notice to creditors in all bulk transfers subject to
the article except auction sales . The contents of the notice and other factors
are provided in UCC 6-107 . UCC 6-108 covers auction sales. The notice must
be given ten days in advance, which is in accord with KRS 377.030 .
The giving of notice is one of the main reasons for bulk sales statutes .
Once the creditor has notice he can take appropriate steps to protect himself .
Section 6-106 . Application of the Proceeds .
Summary
In addition to other requirements :
( 1) If a new consideration is paid , it is the duty of the transferee to
assure that such consideration is applied to pay the debts of the transferor
which are on the list (UCC 6-104 ) or filed in writing in the place mentioned
in the notice within thirty days of such notice. This duty runs to all the
holders of such debts and may be enforced by any holder for the benefit
of all.

(3) If the consideration is insufficient to pay all , distribution shall be

Comment
This section imposes a duty on the transferee similar to that imposed
by the present Kentucky Bulk Sales Law , KRS 377.040 . There is not una-
nimity of opinion as to the feasibility of such provision . Some states , such as
Missouri , and New York , feel that notice to creditors is sufficient . Other
states , including Kentucky and Pennsylvania , impose the greater duty similar
to this section on the transferee .
The purpose of this section is to give the creditors direct protection
against dissipation by the transferor in cases of transfer "for which a new
consideration becomes payable ." Many outright transfers are excluded from
the article by virtue of UCC 6-103 ( 6 ) and ( 7) . In other cases where the
debts are paid as they mature , the buyer is usually safe in disregarding this
section and is protected by UCC 6-109 (2) to the extent of sums honestly paid
to particular creditors .
The buyer may protect himself by any of several methods . He may
withhold payment of consideration or make an escrow or deposit it in court
and interplead the creditors . This obligation runs only to those creditors the
buyer may identify in one of the manners provided in subsection ( 1 ) of
UCC 6-104 .
Section 6-107 . The Notice .
Summary
(1) The notice to creditors shall state :
(a) that a bulk transfer is about to be made ; and
(b) the names and business addresses of the transferor and trans-
feree and all other business names and addresses used by the
tranferor within the last three years if known to the trans-
feree; and
(c) whether all of the debts are to be paid in full as they mature
and if so, the address to which the bills should be sent.
(2) If debts are not to be paid in full , or if the transferee is in doubt
on this, the notice shall state further :
(a) the location and description of the property and the estimated
total of the debts ;
(b) the place where the list of creditors and schedule may be in-
spected ;
(c) whether the transfer is to pay existing debts and if so the
amount and to whom owing ;

Comment
This section specifies the contents and manner of giving the notice in

Section 6-108 . Auction Sales ; " Auctioneer " .
Summary
(1) A bulk transfer by auction sale is subject to this article as provided
in this section.
(2) The transferor shall furnish a list of creditors and prepare a
schedule as provided in UCC 6-104 .
(3) The " auctioneer " is the person who directs , controls or is respon-
sible for the sale other than the transferor . The auctioneer shall :
(a) keep the list of creditors and prepare and keep the schedule
for the period stated in UCC 6-104 ;
(b) give notice of the auction as provided in UCC 6-107 ; and
(c) assure that the proceeds are applied as provided in UCC 6-106 .
(4) Failure of the auctioneer to perform does not invalidate the sale
or affect title of the purchaser , but if the auctioneer knows that the auction
constitutes a bulk transfer such failure makes him liable to the creditors to
the extent of the net proceeds of the auction . If the auctioneer consists of
several persons, their liability is joint and several .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 377.010 , 377.020 , 377.030 , 377.040 .
Comment
Auctioneers are held responsible for compliance with the Kentucky Bulk
Sales Law , KRS 377.010 through 377.990 , but the statute could not be literally
applied to them insofar as it requires advance notice to creditors of the
price to be paid for the goods .

Section 6-109 . What Creditors Protected ; Credit for Payment
to Particular Creditors .
Summary
(1) The creditors protected are those whose claims arose before the
bulk transfer , but creditors becoming such after notice is given are not en-
titled to notice .
(2) Against the aggregate obligation imposed by this article , the trans-
feree or auctioneer is entitled to credit for sums paid in good faith to par-
ticular creditors .
KRS 377.020 , 377.040 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection (1 ) identifies the creditors protected by this article . KRS
377.020 is not clear on this point but the decisions are in accord with sub-
section ( 1 ) . Dwiggins Wire Fence Co. v . Patterson , 166 Ky . 278, 179 S. W.
224. (1915) .
Subsection (2) gives the transferee or auctioneer credit for good faith
payments to particular creditors .
Section 6-110 . Subsequent Transfers .
Summary
When the transferee's title is defective because of noncompliance with
this article , then :
(1) a purchaser from him who pays no value or who takes with notice
takes subject to such defect, but
(2) a purchaser for value in good faith without such notice takes free
of the defect .
None.

Comment
There is no corresponding provision in the Kentucky Act, which pro-
vides merely that non -complying transactions are "fraudulent and void " .
(KRS 377.050) . No decisions have been found interpreting this phrase as to
subsequent transferees .
Section 6-111 . Limitation of Actions and Levies .
Summary
No action shall be brought nor levy made more than six months after
the date the transferee took possession unless the transfer was concealed in
which case actions may be brought or levies made within six months after
its discovery .
KRS 377.050 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Under KRS 377.050 the period of limitation was fixed at four months
from the consummation of the sale to the vendee instead of six months as
here proposed . KRS 377.050 had no provision for tolling the statute in the
case of concealed transfers .

entries.ocr_text:
ARTICLE SEVEN
WAREHOUSE RECEIPTS , BILLS OF LADING AND
OTHER DOCUMENTS OF TITLE
Introduction
Article 7 deals with documents of title. UCC 1-201 ( 15) defines docu-
ments of title as including "bills of lading , dock warrants , dock receipts ,
warehouse receipts or orders for the delivery of goods , and also any other
document which in the regular course of business or financing is treated
as adequately evidencing that the person in possession of it is entitled to
receive , hold and dispose of the document and the goods it covers . To be a
document of title a document must purport to be issued by or addressed to
a bailee and purport to cover goods in the bailee's possession which are
either identified or are fungible portions of an identified mass ."
Article 7 is designed by its sponsors, the National Conference of Com-
missioners on Uniform State Laws and the American Law Institute , to super-
sede two Uniform Acts prepared by the National Conference-the Uniform
Warehouse Receipts Act and the Uniform Bills of Lading Act . Also em-
braced by Article 7 are the provisions of the Uniform Sales Act concerning
negotiation of documents of title . The Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act ,
which was promulgated by the National Conference in 1906 , has been
adopted by every state , Kentucky enacting it in 1938. The Uniform Bills of
Lading Act which was approved by the National Conference in 1909 , has
been adopted in 31 states , but not in Kentucky . The Uniform Sales Act was
approved by the National Conference in 1906 and has been adopted in 34
states , Kentucky enacting it in 1928 .
Attention is directed to the fact that this article is concerned only with
the documents , and not with the business of warehouseman or carriers , which
subjects have been left to local regulatory law . The fact that a warehouse-
man violated some local statute, such as failing to file his annual report ,
should not vitiate the negotiability of an otherwise properly issued ware-
house receipt .
There is a significant omission in Article 7 of material contained in the
two prior uniform acts which it supersedes . Both acts contained provisions
establishing crimes and criminal penalties for certain misconduct in trans-
actions involving documents of title . The Code sponsors considered the
criminal penalty provisions inappropriate to a commercial law code and felt,
further , that they concerned matters upon which there was no commercial
or other necessity requiring uniformity and that , therefore , these questions
were more appropriately left to separate solution by the individual states .
Thus should Kentucky desire to preserve the special criminal provisions em-
bodied in the Warehouse Receipts Act, it will need to draft in the express

GENERAL
Introductory Comment
Part 1 of Article 7 is the definitive and delineable part of the article .
Because of the different subject matters of the several sections , no sum-

comments to the individual sections .
Section 7-101 . Short Title .
Summary
This article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial
Code-Documents of Title .
Section 7-102 . Definitions and Index of Definitions .
Summary
(1) In this article , unless the context otherwise requires :
( a) "Bailee " means the person who by a warehouse receipt , bill
of lading or other document of title acknowledges possession of
goods and contracts to deliver them ;
(b) "Consignee" means the person named in a bill to whom or to
whose order the bill promises delivery ;
(c) "Consignor " means the person named in a bill as the person
from whom the goods have been received for shipment ;

rected to a warehouseman , carrier or other person who in the
ordinary course of business issues warehouse receipts or bills
of lading ;

eral definitions in Article 1 ( UCC 1-201 ) ;

purposes of a contract of storage or transportation ;
(g) "Issuer " means a bailee who issues a document except that in
relation to an unaccepted delivery order , it means the person
who orders the possessor of goods to deliver . Issuer includes
any person for whom an agent or employee purports to act in
issuing a document if the agent or employee has real or ap-
parent authority to issue documents , notwithstanding that the

Section 7-104 . Negotiable and Non-Negotiable Warehouse Receipt ,
Bill of Lading or Other Document of Title .
Summary
(1) A negotiatble document of title is one which :
( a) provides by its terms for delivery of goods to bearer or to
the order of a named person ; or
(b) were recognized in overseas trade , if it runs to a named per-
son or assigns .
(2) All other documents are non -negotiable . A bill of lading in which
it is stated that the goods are consigned to a named person is not made
negotiable by a provision that the goods are to be delivered only against a
written order signed by the same or another named person .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 358.050 , 361.270 .
Comment
The present Warehouse Receipts and Uniform Sales Acts recognize
"bearer " documents (KRS 358.050 , 361.270) . The Code dispenses with the
existing requirements that warehousemen and carriers mark documents ex-
pressly "non - negotiable " when the documents do not contain words of
negotiability (KRS 358.070 ) . Also omitted , without apparent change in the
law, is the provision that such markings do not impair the negotiability
of a document which runs to order or to bearer (KRS 361.300 , 358.050 ) . The
result should be the same under the Code , however , since the section under
discussion clearly makes negotiability strictly dependent on the language
used to identify the person entitled to the goods .
The Code makes provision " where recognized in overseas trade ," for
documents running to "X or assigns ." Such documents are not now provided
for under Kentucky law. Such a document was held non -negotiable in a
New York case ; Gubelman v. Panama R.R. , 192 App. Div . 165, 182 N.Y. Supp .
403 ( 1st Dept. 1920 ) . The question may arise as to bills of lading issued
abroad ; Goldstein v . Societa Veneziana , 193 App . Div . 168, 183 N.Y. Supp . 460
(1st Dept. 1920 ) .
Section 7-105 . Construction Against Negative Implication .
Summary
The omission from either Part 2 or Part 3 of this article of a provision
corresponding to a provision made in the other part does not imply that a
corresponding rule of law is not applicable .
Kentucky Statutes
None.

This section is included to avoid any impairment , for example , of any
common -law right of indemnity a warehouseman may have corresponding

Part 2
WAREHOUSE RECEIPTS : SPECIAL PROVISIONS
Introductory Comment
Part 2 contains special provisions relating to warehouse receipts that
are not applicable to other documents of title. Essentially it is a re-enact-
ment of the Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act , KRS Ch. 358, with certain
clarifying changes .
Section 7-201 . Who May Issue a Warehouse Receipt ; Storage
Under Government Bond .
Summary
(1) A warehouse receipt may be issued by any warehouseman .
(2) Where receipts are issued for whiskey or other goods stored in
bonded warehouses , such receipts have the same effect as a warehouse
receipt even though issued by a person who is the owner of the goods and
not a warehouseman .
KRS 358.010 .
Kentucky Statutes
(1) Accord , KRS 358.010 .
Comment
(2) Subsection (2) covers receipts issued by the owner for whiskey or
other goods stored in bonded warehouses under such statutes as 26 U.S.C.
Ch. 26 .
Section 7-202 . Form of Warehouse Receipt ; Essential Terms ;
Optional Terms .
Summary
(1 ) A warehouse receipt need not be in any particular form .
(2 ) A warehouse is liable to any person for damages caused by the
omission from the receipt of any of the following :
(a) the location of warehouse ;
(b) the date of issuance of receipt ;
(c) the receipt's number ;
(d) to whom delivery will be made ;

Comment
Subsections ( 1) and ( 2) are generally in accord with KRS 358.020 . KRS

sidered an exception to subsection ( 1 ) . However , the Code makes it clear
that the only effect of omission of an essential term is to impose liability on
the issuer for damages caused by the omission . The existing statute leaves
room for the interpretation that a receipt which omits one of these terms
is entirely outside the scope of the act, regardless of the relevance of the
omission to the question being litigated . See Rapp v. Germantown Fireproof
Storage Co. , 44 D. & C. 169 ( 1942 ) .

Section 7-203 . Liability for Non - Receipt or Misdescription .
Summary
The holder of a document of title who relies upon the description therein
of the goods may recover from the issuer damages caused by non- receipt or
misdescription unless the document conspicuously indicates that the issuer
does not know the contents or the holder otherwise has notice .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 358.200 .
Comment
This section is generally in accord with KRS 358.200 . However , the
section contains a new requirement that the notation on the receipt of the
issuer's lack of knowledge be "conspicuous ." " Conspicuous " is defined in
UCC 1-201 ( 10 ) as "so written that a reasonable person against whom it is
to operate ought to have noticed it."

Section 7-204 . Duty of Care ; Contractual Limitations of
Warehouseman's Liability.
Summary
(1 ) A warehouseman is liable for damages for losses caused by failure
to exercise the degree of care that a reasonably careful man would exercise ,
but unless otherwise agreed he is not liable for damags which could not be
avoided by the exercise of such care .
(2) Damages may be limited by agreement fixing the liability at a
specified rate, except that the warehouseman's liability for gross negligence ,
willful wrong or fraud is not effected by such provision .
(3 ) Reasonable provisions as to the time and manner of presenting
claims and instituting actions based on the bailment may be included in the
warehouse receipt or tariff.
(4) This section does not impair or repeal
Note : Insert in subsection ( 4) a reference to any statute
which imposes a higher responsibility upon the warehouse-
man or invalidates contractual limitations which would be
permissible under this article .
KRS 358.030 , 358.210 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Kentucky Warehouse Receipts Act and decisions are silent on the
extent to which a warehouseman may enforce provisions limiting damages
or specifying when and how claims must be presented . The Code adopts
the generally accepted view that such provisions within prescribed limits
are proper . The standard of care in this section is the "reasonably careful
man under like circumstances ." It is unlikely that this new language
will be construed differently from the " reasonably careful owner " norm of
existing legislation .

Attention is directed to UCC 7-309 relating to bills of lading which is
virtually identical to this section.

The buyer of fungible goods from a warehouseman who is also a dealer
in such goods , takes clear of any claim under a warehouse receipt even

None .

Section 7-206 . Termination of Storage at Warehouseman's Option .
Summary
(1) Upon notice to proper persons, a warehouseman may require pay-
ment of charges and removal of goods . Failure to remove authorizes him to
sell them .
(2) If he believes goods will deterioriate or decline in value to less
than his lien sooner than the agreed period of storage, he may shorten time
in which to remove or may sell .
(3) If he unknowingly accepted hazardous goods, he can sell upon
notice and if no sale is made can dispose in any lawful manner .
(4) Prior to any sale, he must deliver upon proper demand .
(5) After deducting his charge , he must hold balance for person en-
titled thereto .
KRS 358.330 , 358.340 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1) Since existing statutes impose no duty of continuing storage on a
warehouseman , he has ( in the absence of express or implied agreement as
to minimum storage period ) the theoretical right to terminate storage at
any time. This subsection provides the warehouseman with a procedure to
enforce his right which hitherto could be enforced in many cases only by
risking substantial liability . The Code in effect imposes a period of re-
quired storage for thirty days after notification of intent to terminate , and
gives the warehouseman the same right thereafter to sell the goods as in
the case of unpaid charges .
(2) KRS 358.340 seems to require a deterioration in the intrinsic nature
or quality of the goods before the authorized procedure may be utilized.
The Code makes it clear that the same rights arise where the physical state
of the goods remains the same but market conditions cause a "decline in
value ."
The existing statute permits , after "reasonable notice ," either a public
or private sale. The Code requires a public sale after at least one week of
advertising or posting .

deterioration the same privilege of disposing of the goods "in any lawful

(3) Under the Code the hazards which will justify use of the author-

KRS 358.340 expressly enumerates the hazards covered: " odor , leakage ,
inflammability , or explosive nature ." The Code applies whenever the "goods
are a hazard ," thus clearly giving the warehouseman power to defend him-

(4) KRS 358.340 on its face seems to limit the right of redemption to
the person to whom notice was given , that is the owner or person in whose

person entitled to the goods ." It seems reasonable to assume , however , that
even under present law any transferee of a receipt would be held to have

The right of redemption under the Code provision is effective at any

(5) Accord , KRS 358.330 , 358.340 .

Summary
( 1) A warehouseman must keep goods covered by different receipts

(2) Commingled fungibles are owned in common by the various own-

Section 7-208 . Altered Warehouse Receipts .
Summary
A purchaser for value without notice may treat the unauthorized filling
of a blank in a negotiable receipt as authorized . Any other unauthorized
alteration leaves a receipt enforceable according to original tenor .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 358.130 .
Comment
The Code would hold a warehouseman to the terms of a negotiable re-
ceipt even though a blank had been filled in without authority . While there
have been no Kentucky decisions on the point , the language of KRS 358.130
would seem to classify unauthorized blank -filling as an "unauthorized "
alteration which does not bind the warehouseman , even though he is re-
sponsible for the receipt being issued with the blank in it.
This section would give a person who made a fraudulent alteration , or
a person who took with notice of such an alteration , the same right as any
other holder to enforce the receipt against the issuer according to the
original tenor of the receipt . Existing law, which has given rise to no cases
on the point in Kentucky , contains some ambiguous language which leaves
in doubt the exact duties of the issuer with respect to persons who either
make a fraudulent alteration or take with notice of such an alteration .
Section 7-209 . Lien of Warehouseman .
Summary
(1) A warehouseman has a lien against the bailor on the goods covered
by a warehouse receipt or on the proceeds thereof in his possession for
charges for storage or transportation ( including demurrage and terminal
charges ) , insurance , labor , or charges present or future in relation to the
goods , and for expenses necessary for preservation of the goods or reason-
ably incurred in their sale pursuant to law. If the person on whose account
the goods are held is liable for like charges or expenses in relation to other

for charges and expenses in relation to other goods , the warehouseman also
has a lien against him for such charges and expenses whether or not the
other goods have been delivered by the warehouseman . But against a person
to whom a negotiable warehouse receipt is duly negotiated , a warehouse-
man's lien is limited to charges in an amount or at a rate specified on the
receipt or if no charges are so specified then to a reasonable charge for
storage of the goods covered by the receipt subsequent to the date of the
receipt .
(2) A warehouseman may retain a security interest in the goods
against the bailor for a maximum amount fixed in the instrument for other
charges than those specified in subsection ( 1 ) such as for money advanced
and interest . This security interest is governed by Article 9.
(3 ) A warehouseman's lien is also effective against any person who
so entrusted the bailor with possession of the goods that a pledge of them
by him to a good faith purchaser for value would have been valid, but is not
effective against the true owner who does not acquiesce in or who is not
estopped to deny the bailment .
(4) The lien is lost upon voluntary delivery of the goods or unjusti-
fiable refusal to deliver .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 358.270 to 358.320 .
Comment
This section creating warehouseman's lien is derived from the same
source as KRS 358.270 to 358.320 , but has been rewritten to recognize two
types of liens . One, the lien for storage and charges, and the other a se-
curity interest based upon agreement. The former arises from the bailment ,
but the latter depends on agreement and must be noted on the receipt .
There exists a possibility of ambiguity between this section and UCC
7-202 . Subsection ( 2) requires that a security interest may be reserved "for
a maximum amount specified in the receipt " for charges such as money
advanced and interest . UCC 7-202 ( 2 ) ( i ) requires a statement of advances
but provides that in the event the exact amount is unknown , then a state-
ment of the fact that advances have been made is sufficient .
Taking the language of UCC 7-202 ( 2 ) ( i) literally , a bare statement
that advances had been made without any statement of amount would be
sufficient . However , if the " maximum amount " language of UCC 7-209 is
read in, then a general statement to be effective would have to contain a
"not to exceed " or "not more than" statement of amount .
Subsection ( 3) is in substantial accord with KRS 358.280 ( 1 ) .
Subsection ( 4) is in accord with KRS 358.290 . The basic right of the
bailee to withhold possession until his lien is satisfied is left by the Code
to negative implication . See UCC 7-403 which provides that a person
demanding delivery under a document must be prepared to satisfy the
bailee's lien . See KRS 358.310 .

Section 7-210 . Enforcement of Warehouseman's Lien .
Summary
The warehouseman may enforce his lien on goods stored in commercial
storage by notice to the proper person and sale on terms which are com-
mercially reasonable . As long as the sale is commercially reasonable the
warehouseman is protected .
On goods held in noncommercial storage, the warehouseman must
give notice in person or by registered mail to all persons of interest , ad-
vertise twice in a newspaper of general circulation , and sell at public
auction .
In either case , the owner may pay charges and halt the sale . The
warehouseman is liable for loss caused by his failure to comply with this
section and may buy at any public sale . A purchaser in good faith takes
clear of claims despite noncompliance by warehouseman . The warehouse-
man may satisfy lien from proceeds but must retain any balance for owner .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 358.330 .
Comment
The provisions of KRS 358.330 for satisfaction of the warehouseman's
lien are substantially continued in this section as respects "goods other
than goods stored by a merchant ," which would cover primarily household
goods of private owners . All persons claiming an interest must be notified
by a notification "delivered in person or sent by registered letter to the
last address ."
KRS 358.330 requires written notice . It is not clear whether this
requirement has been changed, since "delivered in person" might be con-
strued to mean a written notice delivered in person as distinguished from
a letter sent. The notification must include an itemized statement, a de-
scription of the goods , a demand for payment not less than ten days after
"receipt" and a "conspicuous" statement that unless paid , the goods will
be advertised for sale and sold at auction at a specified time and place . The
sale must be held at the nearest suitable place and must conform to the
terms of the notification .
The section apparently changes KRS 358.330 by providing that the
warehouseman is not liable for conversion for failure to comply with
the requirements for sale, unless the violation is willful . Although there
are no Kentucky decisions on the questions , cases decided in other juris-
dictions have held that a sale that does not comply with the statute con-
stitutes a conversion . See , Dawley v . Loria , 173 N. Y. Supp . 468 , (1919 ) ;
Heaton v . Hoerr, 66 N. D. 430, 266 N. W. 261 ( 1936 ) . In the absence of a
willful violation of the statutory requirements , the warehouseman is only
liable for the damages caused by failure to comply with the requirements
for sale. A purchaser in good faith at a noncomplying sale takes the goods
free of any rights of persons against whom the lien was valid .
274
entries.citation: Uniform Commercial Code: Analysis of Effect on Existing Kentucky Law, 1957, p. 309

classification.title: Section 7-210 . Enforcement of Warehouseman's Lien .
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for a warehouseman to enforce a lien on stored goods. It differentiates between commercial and non-commercial storage. For commercial storage, a commercially reasonable sale after notice is sufficient. For non-commercial storage (primarily household goods), stricter notice requirements (in person or registered mail to all interested parties) and public auction are mandated. In both cases, the owner can halt the sale by paying charges. The warehouseman is liable for losses due to non-compliance, but a good-faith purchaser is protected. The statute also references KRS 358.330, noting that the new provisions largely continue its requirements for non-merchant goods, with a change regarding liability for conversion unless the violation is willful.

entries.ocr_text:
Part 3
BILLS OF LADING ; SPECIAL PROVISIONS
Introductory Comment
Bills of lading are the second major type of document of title dealt
with in this article . Part 3 is concerned with provisions specially applicable
to bills of lading . Kentucky has not adopted the Uniform Bills of Lading
Act which forms the basis for this part of the Code .
The only statutes concerning bills of lading found in the Kentucky
Revised Statutes are KRS 276.470 which requires Transportation com-
panies to issue bills of lading , and the Uniform Sales Act , KRS Ch. 361 ,
Sec. 361.270 through 361.400 covering Documents of Title . Under the gen-
eral authority of KRS 281.680 and 281.695 the Department of Motor Trans-
portation is authorized to issue regulations governing Motor Carriers .
Regulation 6.03 (D) issued by that department requires common carriers to
issue bills of lading .
Case law concerning bills of lading in Kentucky is very limited . This
is because approximately 85 % of the freight traffic in Kentucky is inter-
state traffic. Therefore all questions arising based on the inter - state traffic
are brought in the Federal Courts . The law concerning bills of lading is
very well settled in the Federal Courts and rests on the Federal Bills of
Lading Act , 49 U. S. C. A. Secs . 81-124 . Since the Kentucky experience
comes under the Federal Bill of Lading Act for the purposes of this part ,
reference will be made to the pertinent sections of that act rather than
the Kentucky Statutes which are very limited .
Section 7-301 . Liability for Non-Receipt or Misdescription ; " Said
to Contain " ; " Shipper's Load and Count " ;
Improper Handling .
Summary
(1) A person relying upon description of the goods or upon the date
shown in the bill may recover damages from issuer caused by misdating ,
non- receipt or misdescription unless the document indicates shipper does
not know , such as "contents or condition of contents of packages unknown ,"
"said to contain ," "shipper's load and count" or similar , if such indication
be true.
(2) When goods are loaded by an issuer who is a common carrier , the
issuer must count the packages of goods if package freight and ascertain the
kind and quantity if bulk freight . In such cases "shipper's weight , load
and count " or other words indicating that the description was made by the
shipper are ineffective except as to freight concealed by packages .
(3 ) When bulk freight is loaded by a shipper who makes available
to the issuer adequate facilities for weighing such freight , an issuer who is
acommon carrier must ascertain the kind and quantity within a reasonable

Section 7-302 . Through Bills of Lading and Similar Documents .
Summary
(1) The issuer of a through bill or similar document is liable for
breach by its agents or any connecting carrier , except that on overseas
shipments this may be modified by agreement.
(2 ) The connecting carrier is subject to the same liability as the
issuer while the goods are in its possession . His obligation is discharged
by delivery of the goods to another person pursuant to the document and
he is not responsible for defaults of prior or subsequent carriers .
(3) The issuer may recover from the connecting carrier any amounts
it must pay for breach by such carrier .
Federal Bills of Lading Act
None .
Comment
Under the common law test , a carrier is liable for the faults of a
connecting carrier only if "contracted for." The Code places squarely such
liability on the issuer , giving him a right of recourse against the guilty
connecting carrier . Also , the Code makes it clear that such a connecting
carrier , while it holds the goods , holds them on terms defined by the bill
and is subject to the same rights and duties with respect to the goods for
that period as if it had issued the bill .
Section 7-303 . Diversion ; Reconsignment ; Change of Instructions .
Summary
(1) The carrier may deliver goods to a person or place other than
as designated in the bill upon instructions from :
(a) the holder of a negotiable bill ; or

Comment
Neither the Federal Bills of Lading nor the Uniform Bills of Lading
Act deals expressly with the matters covered by this section . The privilege
of the carrier under present law to permit a change of instructions depends
strictly on whether it results ultimately in a "justified delivery , " that is
a delivery to the holder of an order bill, the consignee on a straight bill or
"a person lawfully entitled to possession of the goods ." This requirement
of a "justified delivery " puts a great burden on the carrier to determine
facts for which he had little or no information . This section merely gives
the carrier immunity , but does not effect title to goods .
Section 7-304 . Bills of Lading in a Set .
Summary
(1) Except in overseas transportation , bills of lading are not to be
issued in sets , and any person so issuing is liable for any damages caused
thereby.
(2) Lawfully issued bills in set must be numbered and must state
that it is good only if other parts are still outstanding .
(3 ) Where parts of bills in set are negotiated to different persons ,
the holder of first part negotiated prevails even though a later holder
may have received the goods and discharged the carrier .
(4) One who negotiated a single part is liable to the holders of the
part as if it were the set .
(5) The bailee is obligated to deliver against the first presented
part and is discharged .

the issuance of domestic bills in set and attach liability for so doing . In
addition , the Code section contains provisions relative to improperly issued
bills in set in conformity to commercial practice and treaty .
Section 7-305 . Destination Bills .
Summary
Upon request , the carrier may have the bill issued at destination rather
than point of departure .
Upon request of proper person and surrender of outstanding bills , the
issuer may procure a substitute bill to be issued at any designated place .
None.
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Comment
This is a new provision designed to expedite fast shipments . Air and
truck transport move the goods faster than the documents move through
commercial channels , so that the goods arrive before the documents . This
is especially inconvenient where there are no storage or terminal facilities ,
as is often the case with air or truck shipments . The use of destination bills
would take advantage of bank collection and be preferable to C. O. D.'s .
Section 7-306 . Altered Bills of Lading .
Summary
An unauthorized alteration or filling in of a blank in a bill leaves it
enforceable according to its original tenor .
Sec. 13 .
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Comment
Unauthorized alteration does not relieve issuer from liability as
originally issued. Sec . 13 of the Federal Bills of Lading Act provides that
such alteration was "void " but apparently means that it did not change
issuer's liability . This same rule has been made to apply to unauthorized
filling of blanks about which Sec. 13 was silent .

Summary
(1) A carrier has a lien on the goods covered by a bill of lading
for charges subsequent to its receipt of the goods , but as against the pur-

which the carrier was required by law to receive for transportation is effec-
tive against the consignor or any person entitled to the goods unless the
carrier had notice that the consignor lacked authority to subject the goods

(3) The lien is lost upon voluntary delivery of the goods or unjusti-

Comment
KRS 358.270 to 358.320 are part of the Uniform Warehouse Receipts
Act. This section extends the lien of the warehouseman to carriers . How-
ever , since carriers , unlike warehouseman , do not commonly lend money
on the security of goods in their hands , provisions similar to those in UCC
7-209 ( 2 ) are omitted . Subsection (2) upholds the basic lien unless the
bailee had notice that the bailor lacked authority to pledge or dispose of
the goods . It should be noted that the section limits the bailee's lien on
stolen goods to goods which the carrier had a duty to receive . In the
absence of such a duty to receive the goods , the lien is made invalid if the
owner did not put the depositor in control of the goods .
For a detailed discussion of the lien see UCC 7-209 and comment .
Section 7-308 . Enforcement of Carrier's Lien .
Summary
The carrier may enforce its lien by public or private sale at any
time on commercially reasonable terms after notice to the proper parties .
If the carrier sells in conformity with commercially reasonable practices
of dealers in that type of goods , it is protected .
The owner may pay the charges and halt the sale up to the time of
actual sale. The carrier may buy at any public sale. A purchaser in
good faith takes clear of claims despite noncompliance by the carrier .
The carrier may satisfy lien from proceeds but must retain any balance
for owner .
A carrier's lien may be enforced in accordance with either subsection
(1) or the procedure set forth in subsection ( 2) of UCC 7-210 . The carrier

Section 7-309 . Duty of Care ; Contractual Limitation of Carrier's

Summary
(1 ) The issuing carrier must exercise the care a reasonably careful
owner would exercise under like circumstances . This subsection does not
repeal or change any law or rule of law which imposes liability upon a
common carrier for damages not caused by its negligence .
(2) Damages may be limited by a provision that liability shall not
exceed a value stated if the carrier's rates are dependent upon value and
consignor is afforded an opportunity to declare a higher value . No limita-
tion is effective in case of fraud or conversion to own use.
(3 ) The carrier may include reasonable provisions as to filing claims
or actions in the bill or tariff .

carriers and forbids contractual modification thereof . By virtue of the
second sentence of subsection ( 1) the Code leaves this statute in force , and

all.

warehouse receipts .

Part 4
WAREHOUSE RECEIPTS AND BILLS OF LADING :
GENERAL OBLIGATIONS
Introductory Comment
Part 4 is concerned with certain general obligations in connection with
the issuance of documents of title . Document , as used in Article 7, means
document of title which is defined in UCC 1-201 as follows :
"Document of title" includes bill of lading , dock warrant , dock
receipt , warehouse receipt or order for the delivery of goods , and
also any other document which in the regular course of business
or financing is treated as adequately evidencing that the person
in possession of it is entitled to receive , hold , and dispose of the
document and the goods it covers . To be a document of title , a
document must purport to be issued by or addressed to a bailee
and purport to cover goods in the bailee's possession which are
either identified or are fungible portions of an identified mass .
If a document falls within the foregoing definition , the obligations
of this article are imposed upon the issuer regardless of irregularities in
its issuance .
Section 7-401 . Irregularities in Issue of Receipt or Bill or Conduct
of Issuer .
Summary
The issuer's obligations under the article apply to any document
even though :
(a) the document does not comply with the article or other law
as to its issue, form or content ; or
(b) the issuer violated laws regulating his business ; or
(c) the bailee owned the goods covered by the document ; or
(d) the person issuing the document does not come within the
definition of warehouseman if it purports to be a warehouse
receipt .
KRS 358.200 .
Kentucky Statutes
Secs . 20 , 21 , 22 .
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Comment
KRS 358.200 and Federal Bills of Lading Act Secs . 20 , 21 and 22 deal
with bailee's liability for non -receipt or misdescription which are covered
by UCC 7-203 and 7-301 . The material in this section is mostly new , its
purpose being to make it clear that regardless of irregularities a document
which falls within the definition of a document of title imposes on the
issuer the obligations of the Code .

Section 7-402 . Duplicate Receipt or Bill ; Overissue .
Summary
No right is conferred by a duplicate or other document purporting
to cover goods already represented by an outstanding document of the
same issuer , except in case of bills in a set, overissue of documents cover-
ing fungibles , and substitutes . However , the issuer of such is liable for
damages caused by his overissue or failure to identify a duplicate docu-
ment as such by conspicuous notation on its face .
KRS 358.060 .
Kentucky Statutes
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Sec . 5 .
Comment
This section extends KRS 358.060 and Federal Bills of Lading Act Sec.
5 which apply only to duplicate negotiable documents to make the issuer
of unmarked , non -negotiable documents also liable for damages . The re-
quirement that the marking be conspicuous is made more definite in UCC
1-201 ( 10 ) . The provision that unmarked duplicates confer no rights in
the goods is new , but probably states existing law. See 2 Williston , Sales ,
Sec. 441 (1948 ) . The exception for fungible goods applies only to ware-
house receipts ( UCC 7-207 ( 2 ) ) . This section does not cover disputes
between holders of documents of title not "of the same issuer," such as the
holder of a warehouse receipt and the holder of a delivery order covering
the same goods ( see UCC 7-503 ( 2 ) ) .
Section 7-403 . Obligation of Warehouseman or Carrier to Deliver ;
Excuse .
Summary
(1) The bailee must deliver the goods to any person entitled under
the document who complies with subsections (2 ) and ( 3 ) , unless the bailee
is excused because :
(a) prior delivery to another whose receipt was rightful against
the claimant ;
(b) damage to or delay , loss or destruction of the goods for which
the bailee is not liable , but the burden of establishing negli-
gence in such cases is on the person entitled under the
document ;
(c) previous disposition to enforce lien or on warehouseman's
termination of storage;
(d) exercise by seller of right to stop delivery pursuant to UCC
2-705;
(e) diversion , reconsignment or other disposition pursuant to UCC
7-303 or tariff regulating such right;

Sec . 8 through 12 , 16 , 19.
Comment
This section consolidates and rewrites the above sections to simplify the
statement of the bailee's obligation on the document . Instead of separate
sections on obligation to deliver , justification in delivery , and liability for
misdelivery as in the present law, this section states what circumstances
excuse the bailee's obligation to deliver .
Subsection ( 1) ( b ) states the rule laid down for carriers in many
federal cases . The commissioners noted that state decisions are in con-
flict as to both carriers and warehousemen .
Section 7-404 . No Liability for Good Faith Delivery Pursuant to
Receipt or Bill .
Summary
A bailee , who in good faith including observance of reasonable com-
mercial standards has received goods and delivered or otherwise disposed
of them according to the terms of the document of title or pursuant to this
article , is not liable therefor . This rule applies even though the person from
whom he received the goods had no authority to procure the document or
to dispose of the goods and even though the person to whom he delivered
the goods had no authority to receive them .

Kentucky Statutes
KRS 358.100 .
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Section 10 .
Comment
This section substitutes the general test of good faith and observance
of reasonable commercial standards for the attempt to particularize what
is good faith in present statutees . The Code confers immunity on the
bailee even though the depositor was a thief. Restatement , Torts , Sec . 235
is in accord , but the present statutes do not clearly provide for such a situ-
ation . The Code immunity is limited to the bailee who follows the terms
of the document in good faith , and good faith here includes " observance
of reasonable commercial standards ." Contract UCC 1-201 (19 ) .
Part 5
WAREHOUSE RECEIPTS AND BILLS OF LADING :
NEGOTIATION AND TRANSFER
Introductory Comment
Part 5 is concerned with the procedure and consequences of negotiation
and transfer of documents of title . The form of negotiation and rights
acquired by due negotiation are substantially similar to present law .
Section 7-501 . Form of Negotiation and Requirements of " Due
Negotiation " .
Summary
(1) A negotiable document running to the order of a named person
is negotiated by his indorsement and delivery . It may be negotiated by
delivery alone after his indorsement in blank or to bearer .
(2) When a document is made to bearer it may be negotiated by
delivery alone . When a document to the order of a named person is de-
livered to him it has the same effect as a negotiation .
(3) After a negotiable document of title has been indorsed to a
specified person , the special indorsee must indorse as well as deliver to
negotiate .
(4) A document is "duly negotiated" when it is negotiated to a holder
who purchases it in good faith without notice of any defense against or
claim to it on the part of any person and for value , unless it is established
that the negotiation is not in the regular course of business or financing
or involves receiving the documuent in settlement or payment of a money
obligation .

Comment
This section is a consolidation of the above cited sections of the Uni-
form Sales Act , Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act and the Federal Bill of
Lading Act .
Subsection ( 2) extends to documents of title, the doctrine that
the payee of a negotiable instrument can be a holder in due course , entitled
to the rights created by negotiation . The result not expressly stated in the
Warehouse Receipts Act or the Federal Bills of Lading Act , is to enable
the person named in the document , whether he be depositor , buyer or
financing bank , to claim the rights created by negotiation . For examples
of such rights , see UCC 7-207 ( 2 ) , 7-304 ( 3 ) and ( 4 ) , 7-502 .
The bona fide purchaser of a negotiable document , other than the
bailee , gets the full protection given by the Code only if he takes by "due
negotiation . " Although the term "duly negotiated " is used in present
statutes (KRS 358.410, 361.330 ) , it was not expressly defined . Subsection
(4) sets out the requirements for a negotiable document to be "duly
negotiated ." These requirements are ( 1) negotiation , (2 ) good faith , (3 )
value, and (4) regular course of business or financing . For a detailed dis-
cussion of subsection ( 4) see Braucher , Documents of Title , ( A. L. I. Com-
mittee on Continuing Legal Education ) Sec. 5. 1, and the Commissioners
Comments to the section.
Section 7-502. Rights Acquired by Due Negotiation .
Summary
( 1) Subject to the following section and to the provisions of UCC
7-205 on fungible goods , the holder of a duly negotiated document acquires :
(a) title to the document ;
(b) title to the goods ;
(c) all rights accruing under the law of agency or estoppel ,
including rights to goods delivered to the bailee after the
document was issued ; and

Section 7-503 . Document of Title to Goods Defeated In Certain

( 1) A document of title confers no rights in goods against a person

(a) delivered or entrusted them or any document of title cover-

to the rights of anyone to whom a negotiable warehouse receipt or bill
of lading covering the goods has been duly negotiated . Such a title may be
defeated under the next section to the same extent as the rights of the
issuer or a transferee from the issuer .

KRS 361.330 , 358.410.
Sec . 31 .
Kentucky Statutes
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Comment
This section sets out three situations in which the purchaser's rights
may be defeated despite due negotiation : ( 1) unauthorized bailment , ( 2)
delivery orders in conflict with rights under other negotiable documents ,
(3) bills of lading issued by and to freight forwarders .
Subsection ( 1) provides for the case of goods stored or shipped with-
out authority . Generally , the title of a purchaser by due negotiation pre-
vails over almost any interest in the goods which existed prior to the
procurement of the document of title if it can be shown that the possession
of the goods by the person obtaining the document derived from any
action by the prior claimant which introduced the goods into the stream
of commerce or carried them along that stream. If the prior claimant was
responsible for placing the goods or documents in the hands of the person
whose subsequent actions were unauthorized , or has acquiesced in the pro-
curement by such person of a document of title , then his prior legal or
security interest would be barred . "Acquiescence " does not require active
consent. Knowledge of the possibility of storage or shipment will defeat
his rights as against the holder of a "duly negotiated" document .
Subsection ( 2) provides that title based on an unaccepted delivery
order may also be defeated by due negotiation of a warehouse receipt or
bill of lading covering the same goods .
Subsection ( 3 ) provides for cases of conflict between a bill of lading
issued by a freight forwarder and a bill issued to the freight forwarder
by the carrier . The theory is that a bill issued to a freight forwarder gives
notice on its face that the forwarder has probably issued a bill , but that a
carrier which receives only carload rates should not bear less- than - carload-
lot responsibility ( see Commissioners Comment ) . A person to whom the
forwarder bill is duly negotiated prevails over title based on the carrier
bill , even though the latter is duly negotiated. But the carrier is protected
in compliance with the bill issued by it.

Effect of Diversion ; Seller's Stoppage of Delivery .
Summary
(1) The transferee of a document , to whom the document has been
delivered but not duly negotiated , acquires the title and rights which the
transferor had or could convey .

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) from 1957 outlines conditions under which a purchaser's rights to goods, acquired through due negotiation of a document of title, might be defeated. It covers situations involving unauthorized bailments, conflicting delivery orders, and bills of lading issued by and to freight forwarders. The core principle is that a purchaser by due negotiation generally prevails, but exceptions exist if the goods were introduced into commerce without authority, or if there are conflicts with other negotiable documents or freight forwarder arrangements. The section also addresses the rights acquired by a transferee when a document is delivered but not duly negotiated.

(2) Until the bailee is notified of the transfer of a non -negotiable
document , the rights of the transferee may be defeated :
(a) by those creditors of the transferor who could treat the sale
as void under UCC 2-402 ; or
(b) by a buyer from the transferor in the ordinary course of
business if the bailee has delivered the goods to the buyer or
received notification of his rights ; or
(c) as against the bailee by good faith dealings of the bailee with
the transferor .
(3) A change in shipping instructions by a consignor of a non-
negotiable bill which causes a carrier not to deliver to the consignee defeats
the consignee's title to the goods , if they have been delivered to a buyer in
the ordinary course of business and in any event defeats the consignee's
rights against the bailee .
(4) The seller may stop delivery under a non -negotiable document
by complying with UCC 2-705 , and a bailee honoring such instructions
is entitled to indemnification from seller for any resulting loss or expense.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.310 , 361.340 , 358.390 , 358.410 , 358.420 .
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Secs . 31 , 32 .
Comment
This section corresponds to the above cited sections as consolidated
and rewritten with new material added. It deals with the rights of the
transferee of a document of title in the absence of due negotiation . A non-
negotiable document cannot be negotiated ; it can only be transferred under
this section . Negotiable documents are governed by this section when any
of the elements of due negotiation are missing from a transfer . Subsection
(1 ) provides that in all such cases a transferee "to whom the document
has been delivered ... acquires the title and rights which his transferor had
or had actual authority to convey ."
A transferee of a non - negotiable document , under subsection ( 1) , also
acquires only the rights which his transferor had , or had actual authority
to convey . Both in the case of a negotiable document and a non -negotiable
document , the Commissioners Comment points out that the transferee can-
not rely on principles of estoppel or agency to enable the transferor to
convey greater rights than he has , which is in contrast to situations in-
volving the goods themselves .
Subsection ( 2 ) provides for defeat of the transferee's rights in the
case of non - negotiable documents in ways not applicable to transfers of
negotiable documents . The theory of subsection ( 2) seems to be that , after
delivery of the document and before notification of the bailee , the trans-
feror's position is like that of a seller in possession of goods sold . The
rights of the transferee may be defeated during this interim "by those

Section 7-505 . Indorser Not a Guarantor for Other Parties .
Summary
Indorsement of a document does not make the indorser liable for any
default by the bailee or any previous indorser .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.370 , 358.450 .
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Sec . 35 .
Comment
This section is a consolidation of the above cited sections without any
substantial change .
The purpose of indorsement of a document of title is to strengthen
the transferee's title and not to impose additional obligations on the in-
dorser . The one exception is the case where the bailee has not yet become
liable on the instrument and the indorser guarantees that he will honor it .
Under such circumstances , the indorser engages that appropriate honor of
the document by the bailee will occur . However , once the bailee acknowl-
edges his responsibility to the transferee the indorser's obligation has been
fulfilled and he is no longer liable for the bailee's ultimate actual per-
formance .
Section 7-506 . Delivery Without Indorsement ; Right to Compel
Indorsement .
Summary
The transferee of a negotiable document has a specifically enforceable
right to have his transferor supply any necessary indorsement , but the
transfer becomes a negotiation only as of the time the indorsement is
supplied .

Section 7-601 . Lost and Missing Documents .
Summary
(1) Where a document has been lost , stolen or destroyed a court
may order delivery of the goods or issuance of a substitute document ; and
a bailee complying therewith shall not be liable to any person . The claimant
must post security if the document was negotiable and if non -negotiable
the court may require security . The court may allow the bailee reasonable
costs and attorney fees .
(2) A bailee delivering to a claimant under a missing negotiable
document without court order is liable to any person injured and if done in
bad faith is liable for conversion . Delivery in good faith is not conversion
if made in accordance with a filed classification or tariff or , where no
classification or tariff is filed, if the claimant posts security with the
bailee in an amount at least double the value of the goods at the time of
posting to indemnify any person injured by the delivery who files a notice
of claim within one year after the delivery .
KRS 358.140 .
Kentucky Statutes
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Sec . 14 .
Comment
The requirement that a negotiable document be surrendered when the
goods are delivered causes trouble when the document is lost , stolen or
destroyed . Present statutes authorize court orders for delivery in such
cases , to be made upon satisfactory proof of loss, theft or destruction and
the claimant's giving a bond approved by the court . Delivery without such
a court order is unauthorized and in the case of a warehouse receipt may
even be criminal in view of the section of the Uniform Warehouse Receipts
Act (KRS 358.540 ) making it a crime to deliver without taking up the
negotiable receipts except in cases covered by KRS 358.140, the lost receipt
section .
The Code revises generally the present statutes . This section authorizes
a court order for issuance of a substitute document as well as for delivery ,
and applies to both non - negotiable and negotiable documents . The section
covers "lost , stolen or destroyed " documents . Stolen documents were not
included in the coverage of KRS 358.140 but were in Sec. 14 of the Federal
Bills of Lading Act . The posting of security "to indemnify any person
who may suffer loss as a result of non - surrender of the document " is
mandatory where the court orders delivery under a lost document which
is negotiable , discretionary where the document is non -negotiable . As

Section 7-602 . Attachment of Goods Covered by a Negotiable
Document .
Summary
Except where the document was originally issued upon delivery of
the goods by one who had no authority to dispose of them, no lien attaches
by virtue of any judicial process to goods in the possession of a bailee for
which a negotiable document is outstanding unless it be first surrendered
to the bailee or its negotiation enjoined , and the bailee does not have to
deliver until the document is surrendered or impounded . A purchaser for
value without notice of the process or injunction takes free of the lien .
KRS 358.250 , 361.390 .
Sec . 23 .
Kentucky Statutes
Federal Bills of Lading Act
Comment
The Code does not authorize affirmatively the attachment of goods
subject to a document of title. But it is well understood that attachment is
not prevented by the fact that a non-negotiable document is oustanding .
This section assumed that rule and places limitations on seizure by judicial
process when a negotiable document is outstanding . The authorization
and procedure for seizure , however , whether under the name of attachment
or garnishment or trustee process or otherwise , must be found outside the
Code.
When a negotiable document is outstanding , title and rights acquired
by due negotiation are not defeated by surrender of the goods by the
bailee . To protect both the holder and the bailee , this section follows
present law in providing that no lien attaches by virtue of legal process
unless the document is first surrendered or its negotiation enjoined ; a
bona fide purchaser of the document takes free of the lien even after an
injunction has been granted . An exception to the foregoing is made where
the document was originally issued upon delivery of the goods by a person
who had no power to dispose of them .

If more than one person claims the goods, the bailee has a reasonable
time to ascertain the validity of claims or to bring an action to compel all
claimants to interplead and may compel such interpleader , either as defend-
ing an action for non -delivery of the goods , or by original action , whichever
is appropriate .

litigate with each other instead of with him .

entries.ocr_text:
ARTICLE EIGHT
INVESTMENT SECURITIES
Introduction
Article 8 deals with media of public investment in business enterprise .
The major media covered are corporate stocks and bonds, but the article
also applies to other types of commercial paper commonly recognized and
traded as media for investment such as beneficial interests in business
trusts , mortgage certificates and trust certificates . The primary objective
of the article is to extend elements of negotiability under uniform rules to
investment papers generally . As suggested by the Code Commissioners ,
Article 8 may be likened to "a negotiable instruments law dealing with
securities ."
The article is intended to supplant the Uniform Negotiable Instruments
Law insofar as that law applies to investment securities , principally bearer
bonds , and the Uniform Stock Transfer Act, which applies to corporate
stocks . Both of these uniform acts have been enacted in Kentucky , the Ne-
gotiable Instruments Law in 1904 as KRS Ch . 356, and the Uniform Stock
Transfer Act in 1944 as KRS Ch . 274.
In addition , the article is intended to bring within its uniform rules
registered corporate bonds and other investment papers not covered by
present uniform laws .
Neither the existing Uniform Acts , nor any act geared simply to com-
mercial paper on the one hand nor to share certificates on the other , can
deal neatly with the issue and transfer of securities of varied kinds . A law
special to securities is needed , and Article 8 of the Code is an attempt to
write one . It deals with all types of " securities ," as they are defined broadly
and functionally in UCC 8-102 . Anything that the market would recognize
as a security suitable for trading or investment is believed to be within the
definition .
Part 1
SHORT TITLE AND GENERAL MATTERS
Introductory Comment
The sections comprising the first part of Article 8 include definitions
and general material . The one major change is the adoption of the objective
test of the reasonable man in determining whether a party is charged with
notice, with the corresponding rejection of the test of actual knowledge or
bad faith prescribed by the Negotiable Instruments Law , KRS Ch . 356 .
297

A key change introduced is the adoption of an objective 'reasonable man' test for determining notice, replacing the subjective test of actual knowledge or bad faith found in the Negotiable Instruments Law.

Section 8-101 . Short Title .
Summary
This article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial
Code-Investment Securities .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section gives the article a short title and a means of citation .
Section 8-102 . Definitions and Index of Definitions .
Summary
(1) In this article unless the context otherwise requires :
( a) A "security " is an instrument which :
1. is issued in bearer or registered form ; and
2. is of a type comonly dealt in upon securities exchanges or
markets or commonly recognized in any area in which it is
issued or dealt in as a medium for investment ; and
3. is either one of a class or series or by its terms is divisible
into a class or series of instruments ; and
4. evidences a share , participation or other interest in prop-
erty or in an enterprise or evidences an obligation of the
issuer .
(b) A writing which is a security is governed by this article and
not by Article 3 on Commercial Paper even though it also
meets the requirements of that article . This article does not
apply to money .
(c) A security is in "registered form " when it specifies a person
entitled to the security or to the rights it evidences and when
its transfer may be registered upon books maintained for that
purpose by or on behalf of an issuer or the security so states .
(d) A security is in "bearer form " when it runs to bearer accord-
ing to its terms and not by reason of any indorsement .
(2) "Proper form" means regular on its face with regard to all formal
matters .
(3) A "subsequent purchaser " is a person who takes other than by
original issue.
(4) Other definitions applying to this article or to specified parts
thereof and the sections in which they appear are :
"Adverse claim"
"Bona fide purchaser "
"Broker"
