KRS 292.010 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This proposed section defines a "security " generally in an effort to
cover anything which securities markets are likely to regard as suitable for
trading. KRS 292.010 of the present Kentucky Blue Sky Law denominates
specifically the transactions which are included within the meaning of the
term "security " , as used in such law . Thus , a security included in the Blue
Sky Law would not be included in the proposed definition if it were not of
a type commonly dealt with in securities exchanges or commonly recognized
as a medium for investment .
In adopting the definition of notice , UCC 1-201 ( 25 ) , the objective test
of a reasonably prudent man is employed in determining whether a party
is charged with notice from the circumstances .
By KRS 356.052, to charge a person with notice, it is required that he
have actual knowledge of the infirmity or defect , or knowledge of such facts
that his action amounts to bad faith . This requires a great deal more knowl-
edge than that which would be sufficient to put a prudent man on inquiry .
Section 8-103 . Issuer's Lien .
Summary
A lien of the issuer upon a security is valid against a purchaser only if
the right to such lien is noted conspicuously on the security .
KRS 274.150 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The rule of KRS 274.150, of the Uniform Stock Transfer Act , providing
there shall be no lien or restriction upon the shares represented by a cer-
tificate unless indicated on the certificate , is made applicable to all se-
curities covered by this article . The word "conspicuously ," which is in-
cluded in this section is defined in proposed Article 1 , UCC 1-201 ( 10) as a
term so written that a reasonable person against whom it is to operate ought
to have noticed it.

Section 8-104 . Effect of Overissue ; " Overissue . ”
Summary
( 1) The provisions of this article which validate a security or compel
its issue or reissue do not apply to the extent that validation , issue or reissue
would result in overissue ; but :
(a) if an identical security which does not constitute an overissue
is reasonably available for purchase, the person entitled to
issue or validation may compel the issuer to purchase and
deliver such a security to him against surrender of the se-
curity, if any , which he holds ; or
(b) if a security is not so available for purchase , the person en-
titled to issue or validation may recover from the issuer the
price he or the last purchaser for value paid for it with in-
terest from the date of his demand .
(2) "Overissue " means the issue of securities in excess of the amount
which the issuer has corporate power to issue.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 287.085 (applicable only to banks and trust companies )
Const . Sec . 193 .
Comment
This section of the Code adopts the universally recognized prohibition
against overissue . Kentucky follows this view , Leffingwell v . Evans , 185 Ky.
351 , 216 S. W. 58 ( 1919 ) holding , an overissue of stock , after the whole au-
thorized capital stock has been issued, without complying with any of the
statutory requirements as to increase of stock , is wholly unauthorized and
void .
The right to recover damages for an overissue is also well settled . Some
question , however , has surrounded the measure of such damages ; some
courts allowing the value of the stock at the time registration is refused ;
some the value at the time of trial; and some the highest value between these
two periods . Wilson v . Joplin , 90 Ky . 117, 13 S. W. 362 ( 1889 ) held plaintiff
could require defendant to repay what was fraudulently obtained from him
due to the overissue . Leffingwell v . Evans , 185 Ky . 351 , 216 S. W. 58 ( 1919 )
held that one who in good faith purchases overissued stock may maintain an
action against the corporation or its officers to recover the loss sustained
by the wrongful issual of the stock .
No other Kentucky decisions have been found which state what the
measure of damages shall be. Thus the remedies found in Sec. ( 1 ) ( a) and
(b) are new in Kentucky . Attention is called to the fact that these remedies
are alternative .
Section 8-105 . Securities Negotiable ; Presumptions .
Summary
(1) Securities governed by this article are negotiable instruments .

Comment
Subsection ( 1) declares securities to be negotiable instruments . The
purpose of this section , which was recommended at the suggestion of the
section on Municipal Law of the American Bar Association , is to make se-
curities eligible for investments under certain state acts restricting "legal "
investments to negotiable instruments .
Subsection (2) sets out the applicable rules to proof of signatures and to
burden of proof after signatures are admitted or established . These provi-
sions are the same as those applicable to actions on commercial paper . See
UCC 3-307 , and comments thereunder .
Section 8-106 . Applicability .

The validity of a security and the rights and duties of the issuer with

conflict of laws rules ) of the jurisdiction of organization of the issuer .

Part 2
ISSUE- ISSUER
Introductory Comment
Part 2 of Article 8 deals with rights , responsibilities and defenses of
the issuer . As defined in UCC 8-201 " issuer" includes a maker , drawer , ac-
ceptor and accommodation party .
The changes which would become effective on the passage of these sec-
tions would include the extension of time after maturity within which a
purchaser of a security may still be considered a bona fide purchaser ; estop-
pel in favor of bona fide purchasers based on recitals in government se-
curities as to compliance with constitutional and statutory provisions where
defense is that issue is in excess of constitutional or legal limits, (see KRS
422.140 and comments to UCC 8-202 ( 2 ) (b ) ) ; the delineation of the specific
instances where a party will be estopped from alleging forgery or want of
authority ; the elimination of the fact of nondelivery of an incomplete in-
strument as a valid defense against a good faith purchaser for value ; and the
addition of an explicit provision denoting the obligations or warrants of an
authenticating trustee , registrar , or transfer agent .
Section 8-201 . “Issuer . ”
Summary
(1) An " issuer" includes a person who :
(a) places or authorizes the placing of his name on a security
(otherwise than as authenticating trustee , registrar , transfer
agent or the like) to evidence that it represents a share , par-
ticipation or other interest in his property or in an enterprise
or to evidence his duty to perform an obligation evidenced by
the security ; or
(b) creates fractional interests in his rights or property which
fractional interests are evidenced by securities ; or
(c) becomes responsible for any other issuer as herein described .
(2) A guarantor is an issuer to the extent of his guaranty whether or
not his obligation is noted on the security .
(3) With respect to registration of transfer (Part 4 of this article )
"issuer" means a person on whose behalf transfer books are maintained .

KRS 356.029 , 356.060 , 356.061 , 356.062 .
Comment
This definition of issuer includes a maker , a drawer , an acceptor and
an accommodation party . Such parties were formerly defined in the Nego-
tiable Instruments Law in the sections listed above. As pointed out in the
Commissioners Comment to this section, this article includes many types of
securities not covered by the NIL , and the term "issuer" as defined in the
section is a word of art , applicable to the various kinds of securities cov-
ered . Furthermore , the definition is for purposes of Article 8 only and has
no implications with respect to other statutes using the same term in a dif-
ferent sense .
Subsection (2 ) distinguishes the obligations of a guarantor as issuer
from those of the principal obligor . Subsection ( 3 ) narrows the definition of
"issuer" for purposes of Part 4 of this article (registration of transfer ) . It
is supplemented by UCC 8-406 .
Section 8-202 . Issuer's Responsibility and Defenses ; Notice of
Defect or Defense .
Summary
(1) Even against a purchaser for value and without notice, the terms
of a security include those stated on the security and those made part of
the security by reference to another instrument , indenture or document or
to a constitution , statute , ordinance , rule, regulation , order or the like to the
extent that the terms so referred to do not conflict with the stated terms .
Such a reference does not of itself charge a purchaser for value with notice
of a defect going to the validity of the security eve nthough the security
expressly states that a person accepting it admits such notice .
(2) ( a) A security other than one issued by a government is good
in the hands of a purchaser for value without notice of the
defect even though issued with a defect going to its validity
unless the defect involves a violation of the constitution , in
which case it is valid as to a subsequent purchaser for value
without notice of the defect .
(b) The rule of paragraph ( a) applies to a governmental agency
issuer if there has been a substantial compliance with the
legal requirements governing issue or the issuer has received
consideration and a stated purpose of the issue is one for
which the issuer has power to borrow money or issue the
security .
(3) Except as otherwise provided in the case of certain unauthorized
signatures on issue (UCC 8-205 ) , lack of genuineness of a security is a com-
plete defense even against a purchaser for value and without notice .

The above provisions of the negotiable instruments law have been en-

(1) At the presetn time under the NIL , KRS 356.003 , it is provided that
a promise or order is not so qualified as to make it conditional if there is
merely on the instrument a statement of the transaction which gives rise to
the instrument , or an indication of a particular fund , out of which reim-
bursement is to be made, or a particular account to be debited with the
amount . National Deposit Bank v . Ohio Oil Co. , 250 Ky . 288, 62 S. W. 2d
1048 , 1933 ) ; Pulaski County v . Eichstaedt , 110 F. 2d 79 (6th Cir . 1940 ) . In
Fidelity & Columbia Trust Co. v . Schmidt , 245 Ky . 432 , 53 S. W. 2d 713
(1932 ) the court stated :
"Appellants correctly and clearly classify the authorities in
point which fall into four groups . The first group contains the cases
which involved documents with the characteristics of a bond , but
showing on their face that the promise to pay was conditional . The
second group of cases involved bonds containing a reference to deeds
of trust " for a statement of the rights of the bondholders ." In such
cases the deeds must be examined to ascertain if anything is con-
tained to affect the negotiability of the bond . In the third class of
cases the bonds are made "subject to the terms " of some other in-
strument ; or the express terms of the bond state definitely the fact
that the bond is subordinate to another instrument . In the three
groups of cases just described , the negotiability of the instrument is
destroyed by the character of reference to collateral and connected
writings . The fourth class of cases in which the negotiability of
the securities is preserved embraces bonds that refer to other in-
struments merely as providing security , or as showing the origin of
the transaction , or as stating the consideration for the instrument ."
Subsection ( 1) provides that even as against purchasers for value and
without notice outside agreements even if referred to in the instrument are
not binding if they conflict with the stated terms . Furthermore such a
reference does not of itself charge a purchaser for value with notice of a
defect going to the validity of the security even though the security ex-
pressly states that a person accepting it admits such notice . In this respect
the NIL rule is considerably broadened .

entries.ocr_text:
(2) Subsection ( 2 ) ( a ) provides that in the hands of a purchaser for
value and without notice of the particular defect a security other than one
issued by a government or governmental subdivision is valid even though
issued with a defect going to its validity unless the defect involves violation
of constitutional provisions . In the latter case and in spite of the constitu-
tional provisions the security is valid in the hands of a subsequent purchaser
for value and without notice of the defect. It should be noted that subsection
(2) ( a) does not touch the relationship between the issuer and a purchaser
who takes an original issue when the defect in issue consists of the violation
of a constitutional provision . The Commissioners Comment to the section
states that that situation is not covered by this article but is left to the law
of the particular state .
Kentucky Constitution , Sec. 193 provides :
"No corporation shall issue stock or bonds, except for an equiv-
alent in money paid or labor done, or property actually received and
applied to the purposes for which such corporation was created , and
neither labor nor property shall be received in payment of stock or
bonds at a greater value than the market at the time such labor was
done or property delivered , and all fictitious increase of stock or in-
debtedness shall be void ."
KRS 271.175 is a similar statutory provision to Sec. 193 of the Kentucky
Constitution .
Kentucky cases appear to be in accord with this subsection of the Code
which would validate securities in the hands of bona fide purchasers where
the provisions are statutory and bona fide subsequent purchasers where the
provisions are constitutional . People's State Bank v . Jacksonian Hotel Co. ,
261 Ky . 166 , 87 S. W. 2d 111 ( 1935 ) ; Hess v . Trumbo , 27 K.L.R. 320 , 84 S. W.
1153 ( 1905 ) . See also, Taylor , et al . v . Citizens Oil Co. , 182 Ky . 350 , 206 S. W.
(1918 ) wherein the court at page 362 states :
"One who in good faith for value purchases stock from a third
person , without knowledge that the stock had been obtained from
the company without the payment of an equivalent in value to its
par , is not subject to the constitutional prohibition , which declares
"all fictitious increase of stock shall be void ," but one who takes
stock from a company in payment for property of less market
value than the par of the stock so issued does come within the pro-
hibition and the part which is in excess of the market value of the
thing given in payment therefor , is fictitious and therefore voidable
at the suit of the corporation ."
Also in accord with this subsection is KRS 356.057 of the negotiable in-
struments law which provides that a holder in due course holds an instru-
ment "free from any defect of title of prior parties and free from defenses
available to prior parties among themselves, ..." Kentucky cases reiterate
this provision . See Lynchburg Shoe Co. v . Hensley , 186 Ky . 769 , 218 S. W.
243 ( 1920 ) , holding that where a suit is brought on an instrument regular on
its face , the endorsee holds free of defenses available among prior parties , if
he took in good faith .

and governmental subdivisions . The proposed law provides that if there

distinguished from other issuers as a matter of public policy and additional
safeguards are imposed before governmental issues are validated . Govern-

substantial compliance with the legal requirements governing the issue or
if substantial consideration has been received and a stated purpose of the
issue is one for which the issuer has power to borrow money or issue the
security . The purpose of the substantial compliance requirement is to make
certain that a mere technicality as , e.g. , in the manner of publishing elec-
tion notices, shall not be a ground for depriving an innocent purchaser of
his rights in the security .
A long and well established line of Federal cases recognizes the principle
of estoppel in favor of bona fide purchasers where municipalities issue bonds
containing recitals of compliance with governing constitutional and statu-
tory provisions , made by the municipal authorities entrusted with deter-
mining such compliance . Knott Co. v . Aid Ass'n for Lutherans , 140 F. 2d 630
(6th Cir . 1944) ; Woodmen of the World v. Rowan County , Kentucky , 23 F.
Supp . 903 ( E.D. Ky . 1938 ) ; Dietrich v . Bath County , Kentucky , 292 Fed ., 279
(E.D. Ky . 1909 ) ; Woodmen of the World v. Clay County , Kentucky , 84 F.
Supp . 125 ( E.D. Ky . 1943 ) .
However , it appears that Kentucky decisions are contra to the Code pro-

notice of the defect , where the defect involves a violation of constitutional

"Municipal bonds issued in excess of constitutional and statu-

the holder is a bona fide holder or not , and where only part of the
issue is excessive, each bond is to be treated as only partly valid
and its due proportion to the whole debt attempted to be incurred
may be recovered ."

"No conduct , statement or representation of any officer of any

or serve to estop the county , city or taxing district from contesting

Section 8-203 . Staleness as Notice of Defects or Defenses .
Summary
(1 ) After an act or event which creates a right to immediate per-
formance of the obligation evidenced by the security or which sets a date
on or after which the security is to be presented or surrendered for re-
demption or exchange, a purchaser is charged with notice of any defect
in its issue or defense of the issuer :
(a) if the act or event is one requiring the payment of money or
the delivery of securities or both on surrender of the security
and such funds or securities are available on the date set for
payment or exchange and he takes the security more than one
year after that date ; and
(b) if the act or event is not covered by paragraph (a) and he
takes the security more than two years after the date set for
surrender or presentation or the date on which performance
became due .
(2) A call revoked is not within subsection ( 1) .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.052 ( 2) , 356.053 .

Comment
By KRS 356.052 of the Negotiable Instruments Law , one of the pre-
requisites for a holder in due course is the fact that such holder obtain such
instrument before it is overdue . Moreover , by KRS 356.053 :
"Where an instrument payable on demand is negotiated an
unreasonable length of time after its issue, the holder is not deemed
a holder in due course ."
Kentucky cases also follow this line of reasoning . In Croach v . United
Mercantile Agencies , 299 Ky . 208, 184 S. W. 2d 574 ( 1944) , it was held where
notes were assigned after maturity , any defense which might have been as-
serted against original holders of the notes could likewise be interposed
against the assignee .
The proposed section presents an extensive modification of the policy
that a holder in due course must take before maturity of the instrument , in
some cases extending the time limitation to two years from date of ma-
turity . However , it should be noted that different time limitations apply
when question is notice, not of the issuer's defenses , but of claims of owner-
ship . See UCC 8-305 and comments thereto .
Section 8-204 . Effect of Issuer's Restrictions on Transfer .
Summary
Unless noted conspicuously on the security a restriction on transfer im-
posed by the issuer even though otherwise lawful , is ineffective except
against a person with actual knowledge of it.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 274.150 .
Comment
KRS 274.150 presents a similar provision under present Kentucky law
which, however , is applicable only to stock transfers .
Section 8-205 . Effect of Unauthorized Signature on Issue .
Summary
An unauthorized signature placed on a security prior to or in the course
of issue is ineffective except that the signature is effective in favor of a
purchaser for value and without notice of the lack of authority if the sign-
ing has been done by :
(a) an authenticating trustee , registrar , transfer agent or other
person entrusted by the issuer with the signing of the security
or of similar securities or their immediate preparation for
signing ; or

Summary
(1 ) Where a security contains the necessary signature but is incom-

Comment

curity has a prima facie authority to fill in blanks . To be enforceable against
a person who became a party thereto prior to the completion , the instrument

a reasonable time . But in the hands of a holder in due course, it is valid and
effectual for all purposes in his hands , and he may enforce it as though it
had been filled in strictly in accordance with the authority given and within
a reasonable time .
KRS 356.015 of the NIL also provides that an incomplete instrument

person whose signature was placed thereon before delivery ." Under the

Section 8-207 . Rights of Issuer With Respect to Registered Owners .
Summary
(1) Prior to presentment for registration of transfer of a security , the
issuer or indenture trustee may treat the registered owner as person en-
titled to vote and exercise rights of owner .
(2) Nothing in this article shall be construed to affect the liability of
the registered owner of a security for calls , assessments or the like .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 274.030 , 271.005 ( 5) .
Comment
( 1) KRS 274.030 of the Uniform Stock Transfer Act provides that
nothing in the act shall be construed to forbid a corporation's recognizing
the registered owner as the person exclusively entitled to vote or to receive
dividends . But see Bogardus v. Kentucky State Bank , Ky . 281 S. W. 2d 904
(1955 ) .
( 2) KRS 274.030 also provides that nothing in the Uniform Stock
Transfer Act shall be construed to forbid a corporation's holding liable the
registered owner of shares for calls and assessments .
Section 8-208 . Effect of Signature of Authenticating Trustee ,
Registrar or Transfer Agent .
Summary
(1) A person placing his signature upon a security as authenticating
trustee , registrar or the like, warrants to a purchaser for value without no-
tice of the particular defect that :
(a) the security is genuine and in proper form ; and
(b) his actions are authorized ; and

Comment
At the present time , the agents dealt with by this section are selected
by the issuer and enter into a contract with the issuer outlining their duties
and responsibilities . This section spells out warranties to a purchaser for
value of the security consonant with the purpose of agency.
Part 3
PURCHASE
Introductory Comment
Part 3 of Article 8 covers the rights of successive holders of or claimants
to securities .
Included in these sections are many innovations in the field of statutory
law , although in many instances they represent a codification of judicial
decisions . Among the changes which require consideration are the fol-
lowing :
(a) protection to the purchaser regardless of the negotiability of
a security ;
(b) extension of the law to allow a bona fide purchaser of a ma-
tured instrument under given circumstances ;
(c) provision for partial indorsement of an instrument ;
(d) modification of the rule as to ineffectiveness of forged signa-
tures where a bona fide purchaser has received a new se-
curity;
(e) an innovation in the field of guarantees by making provision
for an " indorsement guarantee ; "
(f) extension of delivery to purchaser of security to include pos-
session by his broker of a security specially indorsed to or
issued in the name of the purchaser ;
(g) provision that a security actually must be seized for a valid
attachment ;
(h) enlargement of the Statute of Frauds to cover any contract
for the sale of securities regardless of the amount .

Section 8-301 . Rights Acquired by Purchaser ; "Adverse Claim ;"
Title Acquired by Bona Fide Purchaser .
Summary
(1) Upon delivery of a security , the purchaser acquires the rights
which his transferor had , except that a purchaser who himself was a party
to any fraud affecting the security or who as a prior holder had notice of
an adverse claim , cannot improve his position by taking from a later bona
fide purchaser . "Adverse claim " includes a claim that a transfer was or
would be wrongful or that a particular person is the owner of or has an
interest in the security .
(2) A bona fide purchaser in addition to acquiring the rights of a pur-
chaser also acquires the security free of any adverse claim .
(3) A purchaser of a limited interest only acquires rights to the extent
of the interest purchased .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.052 , 356.057 , 356.058 , 356.059 , 274.070 .
Comment
( 1) KRS 356.058 of the Negotiable Instruments Law provides that a
holder of a negotiable security who derives his title through a holder in
due course acquires all the rights of such holder in due course, provided he
is not himself a party to any fraud or illegality affecting the instrument .
Thomas v . Siddens , 230 Ky. 651 , 20 S. W. 2d 482 ( 1928) ; Coral Gables , Inc. v .
Barnes , 247 Ky . 292, 57 S. W. 2d 18 ( 1933 ) . Subsection ( 1) goes further
than KRS 356.058 in that it would deny protection also to a former holder
who had notice of the fraud or illegality even though he was not a partici-
pant therein . A definition of " adverse claim " is included to make it clear
that an " adverse claim " may include one asserted by a person not himself
entitled to possession of the security .
(2) Subsection ( 2) provides that a bona fide purchaser in addition to
acquiring the rights of a purchaser acquires also the security free of any
adverse claim . KRS 356.057 of the Negotiable Instruments Law provides
that a holder in due course holds the instrument free from any defect of
title of prior parties and free from defenses available to prior parties among
themselves and may enforce payment of the instrument for the full amount
thereof against all parties liable thereon . Lynchburg Shoe Co. v . Hensley ,
186 Ky . 769, 218 S. W. 243 ( 1920 ) . The Uniform Stock Transfer Act , at KRS
274.060 and 274.070, provides that if the indorsement or delivery of a cer-
tificate was obtained by fraud , duress , mistake , or without authority or after
the owner's death or legal incapacity , the possession of the certificate may
be reclaimed unless the certificate has been transferred to a purchaser for
value in good faith without notice of any facts making the transfer wrongful .
The new proposal appears to be a rephrasing of the present law, estab-
lishing a uniform policy for all investment securities . However , the pro-
tection accorded to a bona fide purchaser does not turn on the security's
negotiability or non -negotiability as it does under the Negotiable Instru-
ments Law .
312

Section 8-302 . “ Bona Fide Purchaser . "
Summary
A "bona fide purchaser " is a purchaser for value in good faith without
notice of any adverse claim who takes delivery of a security in bearer form
or in registered form issued to him or indorsed to him in blank.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.052 .
Comment
In KRS 356.052 of the Negotiable Instruments Law , various require-
ments are provided in order that a purchaser may be a holder in due course
which would be the counterpart of the bona fide purchaser under the pro-
posed law . These requirements of the NIL are to the effect that the instru-
ment must be complete and regular on its face, purchased before it is over-
due, without notice that it has been dishonored . The purchase must be in
good faith and for value and without notice of any infirmity or defect in
title.
Under the Code provision , even though a purchaser obtains the security
after its maturity date, he may nevertheless still be a bona fide purchaser .
Section 8-303 . “ Broker ."
Summary
"Broker " means a person engaged in the business of buying and selling
securities , who in the transaction concerned acts for, buys from or sells a
security to a customer . Nothing in this article determines the capacity in
which a person acts for purposes of any other statute or rule to which such
person is subject .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 434.320 ( 2) .
Comment
This section defines "broker " for the purposes of this article only .
Section 8-304 . Notice to Purchaser of Adverse Claims .
Summary
(1) A purchaser ( including a broker for the seller or buyer but ex-
cluding an intermediary bank ) of a security is charged with notice of ad-
verse claims if :
(a) the security has been indorsed "for collection " or "for sur-
render " or some purpose not involving transfer ; or
(b) the security is in bearer form and in unambiguous terms states
that it is the property of a person other than the transferor .
The mere writing of a name on the security is not enough .

(2) If the purchaser ( including a broker for the seller or buyer ) , has
notice that the security is held for a third person or is registered in the name
of or indorsed by a fiduciary , it does not create a duty of inquiry into the
rightfulness of the transfer or constitute notice of adverse claims . If, how-
ever , the purchaser ( excluding an intermediary bank ) has reason to know
the proceeds are for the individual benefit of the fiduciary , the purchaser
is charged with notice of adverse claims .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.037 , 356.056 , 386.100 , 386.110 .
Comment
This section presents situations in which a purchaser's reason to know
of adverse claims is declared as a matter of law . However , the listings are
not exhaustive , and do not exclude other sitautions giving similar notice .
(1 ) ( a ) This subsection in effect follows KRS 356.037 of the Negotia-
ble Instruments Law , which provides that a subsequent indorsee acquires
only the title of the first indorsee under a restrictive indorsement . There
have been no Kentucky cases which have considered the effect of a restric-
tive indorsement on a bearer instrument .
The Treasury Department has provided for restrictive indorsements of
United States bearer securities . Its regulation provides that a bank may
place restrictive indorsements on the face of United States bearer securities
owned by it or by its customers at the time of forwarding such securities to
a Federal Reserve Bank or Branch or to the Treasurer of the United States
for payment , redemption , or optional exchange for a new issue . Such in-
dorsements are authorized for no other purpose and at no other time . Treas-
ury Dept. Circ . No. 853 , Sec. 328 , 14 Fed. Reg . 6171 ( 1949 ) . This indorsement
makes the securities nonnegotiable and is intended to afford the banks or
their customers the same relief as is given in the case of registered securities
in the event of loss , theft or destruction . It also makes possible savings in
transportation and insurance charges .
(1 ) ( b) This subsection provides that a purchaser is charged with
notice of adverse claims if the security is in bearer form and has on it an
unambiguous statement that the security is the property of a person other
than the transferor . The Code provides that the mere writing of a name on
such a security is not such a statement. It does not appear that this pro-
vision contemplates any change from existing law . Such a statement should
certainly be construed , under present law , as a circumstance which would
impose upon the purchaser the duty to make inquiry. Or , under KRS
356.056 of the NIL , it would amount to "knowledge of such facts that his
action in taking the instrument amounted to bad faith ."
(2 ) KRS 386.100 provides that any person who in good faith pays or
transfers to a fiduciary any money or other property which the fiduciary as
such is authorized to receive shall not be responsible for the proper applica-
tion thereof by the fiduciary , and any right or title acquired from the fidu-
ciary in consideration of the payment or transfer shall not be invalid be-
cause of a misapplication by the fiduciary .

Section 8-305 . Staleness as Notice of Adverse Claims .
Summary
An act or event which creates a right to immediate performance of the
obligation evidenced by the security or which sets a date on or after which
the security is to be presented or surrendered for redemption or exchange
does not constitute notice of adverse claims except in case of a purchase :
(a) after one year from date set for such presentment or sur-
render ; or
(b) after 6 months from date set for payment of money against
presentation or surrender of the security if funds are avail-
able for payment on that date .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.052 ( 2) , 356.053 .
Comment
As noted in the comment to UCC 8-302 , the NIL requires the purchaser
to take the instrument prior to maturity in order to be entitled to the status
of a "holder in due course ." It should be noted that the definition of "bona
fide purchaser " in UCC 8-302 does not require purchase prior to maturity .
This section makes it clear that the mere fact of maturity will not operate
in itself to preclude the purchaser of securities from attaining the status of
a "bona fide purchaser " as against adverse claims of ownership .
While the maturity of the security does not automatically affect holders '
rights under UCC 8-305 , certain time limits are prescribed after which the
fact of staleness is regarded as notice of adverse claims of ownership . Re-
garding staleness of instruments payable on demand, KRS 356.053 of the
NIL provides :
"Where an instrument payable on demand is negotiated an unrea-
sonable length of time after its issue , the holder is not deemed a
holder in due course ."
An investment security payable on demand would not, under UCC 8-305
be regarded as " stale" regardless of the lapse of time after issue. The large
bulk of demand instruments covered by the NIL , such as drafts and checks ,
is ordinarily intended for immediate liquidation . With respect to corporate
debentures or other investment securities , this is not the intention even
though they may be payable on demand. Thus the lapse of unusual time
after issue should be regarded as notice of a possible irregularity in the
case of a draft payable on demand , whereas it would not be a suspicious
circumstance at all to find a corporate debenture payable on demand out-
standing many years after its issue date .
It will be observed that UCC 8-203 contains comparable provisions re-
garding staleness as notice of defects from the point of view of issuer's de-
fenses , except that the time limits therein specified are longer than the
periods prescribed by this section . Shorter periods are here prescribed be-
cause a purchaser who takes a matured security after funds are available for
315

classification.title: Section 8-305 . Staleness as Notice of Adverse Claims .
classification.summary: This section clarifies that the mere fact of a security's maturity does not automatically preclude a purchaser from being a bona fide purchaser against adverse claims of ownership. However, it sets specific time limits after which staleness is considered notice of adverse claims, with different periods for securities payable on demand versus those with a set date for presentment or surrender. It distinguishes between instruments like drafts and checks (intended for immediate liquidation) and corporate debentures or investment securities (which may be outstanding for many years).

Section 8-306 . Warranties on Presentment and Transfer .
Summary
(1 ) A person who presents a security for registration , payment or
exchange warrants to the issuer that he is entitled to the registration , pay-
ment or exchange . But a purchaser for value without notice of adverse
claims who receives a new , reissued or re - registered security on registration
of transfer warrants only that he has no knowledge of any unauthorized
signature ( UCC 8-311 ) in a necessary indorsement .
(2) A person transferring a security to a purchaser for value warrants :
(a) his transfer is effective and rightful; and
(b) the security is genuine and not materially altered ; and
(c) he knows no fact which might impair the validity thereof .
(3 ) Where a security is delivered by an intermediary known to be en-
trusted with delivery on behalf of another , the intermediary warrants only
his good faith and authority even though he has purchased or made ad-
vances against the claim to be collected against the delivery . A broker is not
an intermediary within the meaning of this subsection .
(4) A pledgee or other holder for security who redelivers the security
received , or after payment and on order of the debtor delivers that security
to a third person makes only the warranties of an intermediary under sub-
section ( 3 ) .
(5) A broker gives to his customer and to the issuer and a purchaser
the warranties provided in this section and has the rights and privileges of
a purchaser under this section . The warranties of and in favor of the broker
acting as an agent are in addition to applicable warranties given by and in
favor of his customer .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 274.110 , 274.120 , 356.065 , 356.066 , 356.067 , 356.069 .
(1)
Comment
Subsection ( 1 ) states the warranties given on presentation of a
security for registration of transfer or for payment or exchange. The Uni-
form Stock Transfer Act, KRS 274.110 , does not limit the warranty to anyone
and appears to be in accord with the intention of this provision .
(2) Under KRS 274.110 , a person who for value transfers a stock cer-
tificate , unless a contrary intention appears , warrants that the certificate is
genuine , that he has a legal right to transfer it, and that he has no knowl-
edge of any fact which would impair the validity of the certificate . Also an
assignor of a claim secured by a certificate is held to these warranties , but
his liability on such warranties shall not exceed the amount of the claim.

When a registered security is delivered to a purchaser without a neces-

plete upon delivery and the purchaser has an enforceable right to have any

Section 8-308 . Indorsement , How Made ; Special Indorsement ;
Indorser Not a Guarantor ; Partial Assignment .
Summary
(1) An indorsement of a registered security is made when an appro-
priate person signs on the security or on a separate document , an assignment
or transfer of the security or a power to assign or transfer it or when such
person merely signs his name on the back of the security .
(2) An indorsement may be in blank or special . An indorsement in
blank includes an indorsement to bearer . A special indorsement specifies
the person to whom the security is to be transferred , or who has power to
transfer it . A holder may convert a blank into a special indorsement .
(3) "An appropriate person" in subsection ( 1 ) means :
(a) the person specified by the security or by special indorsement
to be entitled to the security ; or
(b) where the person so specified is described as a fiduciary but
is no longer serving in the described capacity-his successor ;
or
(c) where the security or indorsement so specifies more than one
person as fiduciaries , and one or more are no longer serving
in that capacity-the remaining fiduciary or fiduciaries ,
whether or not a successor has been appointed or qualified ;
or
(d) where the person so specified is an individual and is without
capacity to act by virtue of death , incompetence, infancy or
otherwise his executor , administrator , guardian or like
fiduciary ; or
(e) where the security or indorsement specifies more than one
person as tenants by the entirety or with right of survivor-
ship and by reason of death all cannot sign-the survivor or
survivors ; or
(f) a person having power to sign under applicable law or con-
trolling instrument ; or
(g) to the extent that any of the foregoing persons may act
through an agent-his authorized agent.

(5) An indorsement purporting to be only a part of a security repre-
senting units intended by the issuer to be separately transferable is effective
to the extent of the indorsement .
(6 ) Whether the person signing is appropriate is determined as of the
date of signing and an indorsement by such a person does not become un-
authorized for the purposes of this article by virtue of any subsequent
change of circumstances .
(7) Failure of a fiduciary to comply with a controlling instrument or
with the law of the state having jurisdiction of the fiduciary relationship ,
including any law requiring the fiduciary to obtain court approval of the
transfer, does not render his indorsement unauthorized for the purposes of

Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.031 through 356.037 , 356.064 through 356.069 , 356.019 ,
274.200 .
Comment
(1) This subsection continues the simplified method of indorsement of
securities set forth in the Uniform Stock Transfer Act . KRS 274.200 pro-
vides:
"A certificate is indorsed when an assignment or a power of at-
torney to sell , assign, or transfer the certificate or the shares repre-
sented thereby is written on the certificate and signed by the person
appearing by the certificate to be the owner of the shares repre-
sented thereby , or when the signature of such person is written
without more upon the back of the certificate . In any such cases
a certificate is indorsed though it has not been delivered ."
Under KRS 356.031 of the Negotiable Instruments Law , the indorsement
of a negotiable security " must be written on the instrument itself or upon a
paper attached thereto ." Also KRS 356.031 provides that " the signature of
the indorser , without additional words , is a sufficient indorsement ."
KRS 356.032 requires that the indorsement of such negotiable security
must be of the entire instrument ; an indorsement that purports to transfer
only a part or which purports to transfer it to two or more indorsees sev-
erally, does not constitute a negotiation . But an instrument paid in part
may be indorsed as to the residue .
(2) This subsection is similar to KRS 356.033 of the Negotiable Instru-
ments Law which provides for special or blank indorsements ; KRS 356.034
defines such indorsements ; and KRS 356.035 describes the conversion of
blank indorsements into special indorsements . It should be noted that the
definition of "special indorsement " is broadened by this subsection to in-
clude the insertion of the name of a person having power to transfer . This
is a practice which is widely used in the shipment of securities .

Section 8-309 . Effect of Indorsement Without Delivery .
Summary
An indorsement of a security does not constitute a transfer until de-
livery of the security on which it appears or if on another document until
delivery of the document and the security .

KRS 356.030 , 274.010 , 274.100 .

Section 8-312 . Effect of Guaranteeing Signature or Indorsement .
Summary
(1) Any person guaranteeing a signature of an indorser of a security
warrants that at the time of signing :
(a) the signature was genuine ; and
(b) the signer was an appropriate person to indorse (UCC 8-308 ) ;
and
(c) the signer had legal capacity to sign .
But the guarantor does not otherwise warrant the rightfulness of the par-
ticular transfer .
(2) Any person may guarantee an indorsement of a security and by
so doing warrants not only the signature ( Subsection ( 1) ) but also the right-
fulness of the particular transfer in all respects . But no issuer may require
a guarantee of indorsement as a condition to registration of transfer .
(3) The foregoing warranties are made to any person taking or deal-
ing with the security in reliance on the guarantee and the guarantor is
liable to such person for any loss resulting from breach of the warranties .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
Subsections ( 1) and ( 2 ) describe two distinct types of guarantors and
define the extent of the liability of each . The guarantor of a signature , dealt
with by subsection ( 1) , warrants that such signature is genuine as well as
the legal status , authority , identity and capacity of the signer . The guarantor
of an indorsement set out in subsection ( 2) warrants not only these things
but also warrants the rightfulness of the particular transfer .
Subsection ( 3) is designed to encourage issuers and their agents to
reply upon signature guarantees and to avoid needless waste of time and
duplication of effort in ascertaining the facts so guaranteed .

Section 8-313 . When Delivery to the Purchaser Occurs ;
Purchaser's Broker as Holder .
Summary
(1) Delivery to a purchaser occurs when :
(a) he or a person designated by him acquires possession of a
security ;
(b) his broker acquires possession ;
(c) his broker sends him confirmation of the purchase and also
by book entry or otherwise identifies a security in his pos-
session as belonging to the purchaser ;
(d) another person holding a security to be delivered , acknowl-
edges that he holds the security for purchaser .
(2) Except as specified in (b) and ( c) above, the purchaser is not the
holder of securities held for him by his broker despite a confirmation of
purchase and a book entry and other indications that the security is part
of a fungible bulk held for customers .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.190 , 274.220 .
Comment
KRS 356.190 of the Negotiable Instruments Law defines delivery as the
"transfer of possession , actual or constructive , from one person to another . "
KRS 274.220 of the Uniform Stock Transfer Act defines delivery as the
"voluntary transfer of possession from one person to another ."
The proposed section presents a general modification of prior delivery
rules in cases involving brokers by prescribing delivery to the purchaser
even when his broker acquires possession , and is in contrast to the defini-
tions set forth in KRS 356.190 and 274.220 .
Section 8-314 . Duty to Deliver , When Completed .
Summary
(1 ) Unless otherwise agreed , where a sale of a security is made on an
exchange or through brokers :
(a) the selling customer fulfills his duty to deliver when he places
it in the possession of the selling broker or a person designated
by the broker or causes an acknowledgment to be made to the
selling broker that it is held for him ; and
(b) the selling broker fulfills his duty by placing the security in
the possession of the buying broker or a person designated by
him or by effecting clearance of the sale in accordance with
the rules of exchange.

(2) Except as specified in (b) and ( c) above, the purchaser is not the holder of securities held for him by his broker despite a confirmation of purchase and a book entry and other indications that the security is part of a fungible bulk held for customers .

Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section, together with the section on warranties to the purchaser
( UCC 8-306 ) and the section on delivery to the purchaser ( UCC 8-313 ) ,
states the rights and duties of the parties involved in the transfer of a se-
curity from the original transferor to the ultimate purchaser . No Kentucky
cases covering this proposed section were found .
Section 8-315 . Action Against Purchaser Based Upon Wrongful
Transfer .
Summary
( 1) Any person against whom the transfer of a security is wrongful
may reclaim possession of the same security or new security evidencing the
same rights or have damages against anyone but a bona fide purchaser .
(2) If a transfer is wrongful because of an unauthorized indorsement ,
the owner may reclaim even from a bona fide purchaser if the ineffective-
ness of the purported indorsement can be asserted against him under UCC
8-311 .
(3 ) The right to reclaim possession of a security may be specifically
enforced and its transfer enjoined and the security impounded pending liti-
gation .
KRS 274.070 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
KRS 274.070 of the Uniform Stock Transfer Act permits an owner to
reclaim a stock certificate and rescind the transfer if the indorsement or
delivery of the certificate was procured by fraud or duress, or when made
under such a mistake as to make the indorsement or delivery inequitable , or
if delivery was made without the authority of the owner , or after the owner's
death or legal incapacity unless the certificate has been transferred to a bona
fide purchaser , or the owner has elected to waive the injury or has been
guilty of laches in enforcing his rights .
The general rule allowing an owner to reclaim possession of a security
wrongfully transferred is continued by the proposed section . The statement
of the rule in case of forged or unauthorized indorsements is added .

Section 8-316 . Purchaser's Right to Requisites for Registration of
Transfer on Books .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed , the transferor must apply his purchaser on
demand with proof of his authority to transfer or any other requisite neces-
sary to obtain registration of transfer , but if the transfer is not for value this
need not be done unless the purchaser furnishes the expenses . Failure to
comply with a demand made within a reasonable time gives the purchaser
the right to reject or rescind the transfer .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section requires the transferor to supply the purchaser with proof
of his authority to transfer or with any other requisite necessary to obtain
registration of the transfer . If the sale is not for value the transferor shall
not be bound to do so unless the purchaser furnishes the necessary expenses .
Failure to comply with the demand by the purchaser gives the purchaser the
right to reject or rescind the transfer . This last provision is an innovation .
Section 8-317 . Attachment or Levy Upon Security .
Summary
(1) No attachment or levy upon a security shall be valid until the se-
curity is actually seized by the officer making the attachment or levy , but a
security which has been surrendered to the issuer may be attached or levied
upon at the source.
(2) A creditor whose debtor is the owner of a security is entitled to
appropriate aid from the courts in satisfying his claim .
KRS 274.130 , 274.140 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1) Subsection ( 1) corresponds to KRS 274.130 of the Uniform Stock
Transfer Act . It should be noted, however , that KRS 274.130 permits levy or
attachment without actual seizure if transfer by the holder has been en-
joined . Subsection ( 1) would not permit an effectual levy under any cir-
cumstances without physical seizure of the security . The Commissioners
Comment states that this change was made because the holder might trans-
fer the security in contempt of court even after an injunction against trans-
fer has issued.
(2) Subsection (2) is identical to the provision now found in KRS
274.140 of the Uniform Stock Transfer Act.

Section 8-318 . No Conversion by Good Faith Delivery .
Summary
An agent or bailee who in good faith has received securities and sold ,
pledged or delivered them according to instructions of his principal is not
liable for conversion although the principal had no right to dispose of them .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section undertakes to protect brokers and agents who act in good
faith from actions for conversion based upon wrongful transfer by the sell-
ing principal . The provision has no precedent in prior statutes. It is in-
tended to counteract certain cases in other jurisdictions holding the broker
or agent liable for innocent conversion .
Section 8-319 . Statute of Frauds .
Summary
A contract for the sale of securities is not enforceable unless :
(a) there is some writing signed by the party against whom en-
forcement is sought or by his authorized agent or broker suf-
ficient to indicate a contract has been made for sale of a stated
quantity of described securities at a defined or stated price ; or
(b) delivery of the security has been accepted or payment has
been made but the contract is enforceable under this provision
only to the extent of such delivery or payment ; or
(c) within a reasonable time a writing in confirmation of the sale
or purchase and sufficient against the sender under paragraph
(a) has been received by the party against whom enforcement
is sought and he failed to send written objection to its con-
tents within ten days after its receipt ; or
(d) the party against whom enforcement is sought admits in his
pleading , testimony or otherwise in court that a contract was
made for sale of a stated quantity of described securities at a
defined or stated price .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.040 .
Comment
The Statute of Frauds provisions of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.040 ,
apply only to contracts to sell or sales of choses in action of $500 or more.
To be enforceable by action under KRS 361.040 there must either be accept-
ance and receipt of a part of the items of sale , or part payment , or some note
or memorandum in writing of the contract or sale signed by the party to be

It should be noted that this section makes material changes in the
present Statute of Frauds provisions . Under the Code any contract for the
sale of securities is covered regardless of amount . Enforcement on the basis
of part payment or part delivery is obtainable only to the extent of such
part payment or delivery . A further change makes a contract enforceable
against a party who received confirmation of a sale or purchase and failed
to send a written objection to it within ten days thereafter .
Part 4
REGISTRATION
Introductory Comment
Part 4 of Article 8 provides for the registration of transfers of securities .
The enactment of these sections would produce the following changes :
The reduction of the issuer's liability for wrongful registration of transfer ;
provision for assurances as to indorsements which issuer may require prior
to registering a transfer ; the limitation of issuer's liability for wrongful
registration to the replacement of the security by a new one with the elimi-
nation of the former alternative remedy of damages ; the enforcement of
issuer's duty to replace a lost , destroyed or stolen security without the
benefit of a court order ; and the addition of similar obligations required of
transfer agents , registrars and other like agents to the owner of the security
as are now due to the issuer alone .
Section 8-401 . Duty of Issuer to Register Transfer.
Summary
( 1) Where a security in registered form is presented to the issuer with
a request to register transfer , the issuer is under a duty to register the trans-
fer as requested if :
(a) the security is indorsed by the appropriate person or persons
( UCC 8-308 ) ; and
(b ) reasonable assurance is given that those indorsements are
genuine and effective ( UCC 8-402 ) ; and
(c) the issuer has no duty to inquire into adverse claims or has
discharged any such duty ( UCC 8-403 ) ; and
(d) any applicable law relating to the collection of taxes has been
complied with; and
(e) the transfer is in fact rightful or is to a bona fide purchaser .

entries.ocr_text:
(2) Where an issuer is under a duty to register a transfer of a security
he is also liable to the person presenting it for registration or his principal
for loss resulting from any unreasonable delay in registration or from failure
or refusal to register the transfer .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
"Issuer" as used in this section does not have the broad connotation of
the word as defined in UCC 8-201 . In this section "issuer" denotes the com-
pany or government whose security is involved . When read with this un-
derstanding the section is similar to present law. 18 C.J.S. , Corporations ,
Sec. 410 , states the law as follows :
...
"The ownership of shares of stock passes from the seller to the
buyer by force of the contract of sale and entitles the purchaser
to demand that he shall be registered on the books of the cor-
poration ... ”
The duty to register a transfer is stated in terms of compliance by the per-
son requesting it with each of the sections subparagraphs . The proposed
section makes the law more specific in that it delineates the conditions
under which an issuer must register the transfer of a security . For Kentucky
decisions requiring corporation to register transfer on its books see, Mutual
Telephone Co. v . Jarrell , 220 Ky . 551 , 295 S. W. 865 ( 1927 ) ; Banco Kentucky
Co. v . Weil, 258 Ky . 243, 79 S. W. 2d 977 ( 1935 ) .
Subsection ( 2 ) states the issuer's liability in damages for improper re-
fusal to register a transfer of a security . No Kentucky decisions covering
this point have been found . In accord with the Code provision see 18 C.J.S. ,
Corporations , Sec. 438 .
Section 8-402 . Assurance That Indorsements Are Effective .
Summary
(1) The issuer may require the following assurance that each neces-
sary indorsement is genuine and effective :
(a) in all cases , a guarantee of the signature (subsection ( 1) of
UCC 8-312 ) of the person indorsing ; and
(b) where indorsement is by an agent, appropriate assurance of
authority to sign ;
(c) where the indorsement is by a fiduciary , an appropriate cer-
tificate of appointment or incumbency dated within 60 days
before the date of presentation for transfer ;
(d) where there is more than one fiduciary , reasonable assurance
that all who are required to sign have done so ;
(e) where the indorsement is by a person not covered by any of
the foregoing , assurance appropriate to the case corresponding
as nearly as may be to the foregoing .

(2) A " guarantee of the signature " in subsection ( 1) means a guar-
antee signed by or on behalf of a person reasonably believed by the issuer to

provided such standards are not manifestly unreasonable .
(3) "An appropriate certificate of appointment or incumbency " in
subsection ( 1) means :
(a) in the case of a fiduciary appointed or qualified by a court , a

(4) If the issuer has notice that the transfer may be wrongful , it may

Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
This section sets out requirements concerning assurances as to indorse-
ments which the issuer is entitled to receive prior to registering a transfer
on its books . Kentucky has no statute or case law covering such assurances .
The requirements prescribed by the Code appear to be reasonable and ex-
cept in the rarest cases should be easily complied with . The provision of
subsection ( 4) finds support in 18 C.J.S. , Corporations , Sec. 436 which pro-
vides :
"Where the request for a transfer is made by one other than the
stockholder , the corporation , as trustee , has the duty to the stock-
holder to make inquiry as to the authority of the one asking for the
transfer and, if put on inquiry, it has notice of all that the inquiry
would reveal ; and if, therefore , an agent or other fiduciary demands
a transfer of stock , the corporation must ascertain his authority
from the power of attorney , and it may rightfully refuse to make a
transfer at the instance of such persons until he shall furnish satis-
factory evidence that he is authorized to transfer under the terms
of the trust ."
Discussing requests for transfer made by an executor or administrator , 18
C.J.S. , Corporations , Sec. 436 provides :
"The corporation , however , must inquire as to the authority of the
one demanding a transfer if an administrator , from his letters , and
if an executor , from the will , because these are the sources of
power ; and in the case of an executor , as it is its duty to make in-
quiry, it is fixed with knowledge of the contents of the will ."

Section 8-403 . Limited Duty of Inquiry .
Summary
(1) An issuer to whom a security is presented for registration is under
a duty to inquire into adverse claims if:
(a) a notification of an adverse claim is received at a time and in
a manner which affords the issuer a reasonable opportunity
to act on it prior to the issuance of a new , reissued or re-
registered security ; or
(b) the issuer has notice of an adverse claim by electing to require
a controlling instrument as provided in UCC 8-402 (4 ) .
(2 ) The issuer may discharge any duty of inquiry by any reasonable
means , including notifying an adverse claimant by registered or certified
mail at the address furnished by him or if there is no such address at his
residence or regular place of business that the security has been presented
for registration of transfer by a named person , and that the transfer will be
registered unless within thirty days from the date of mailing the notification ,
either :
(a) an appropriate restraining order , injunction or other process
issues from a court of competent jurisdiction ; or
(b) an indemnity bond sufficient in the issuer's judgment to pro-
tect the issuer and any transfer agent, registrar or other agent
of the issuer involved , from any loss which it or they may
suffer by complying with the adverse claim is filed with the
issuer .
(3) Except to the extent that an issuer has notice by electing to re-
quire a controlling instrument as provided in UCC 8-402 (4) or receives
notification of an adverse claim under subsection (1) of this section, where a
security presented for registration is indorsed by the appropriate person or
persons the issuer is under no duty to inquire into adverse claims . In par-
ticular:
(a) an issuer registering a security in the name of a fiduciary is
not bound to inquire into the existence, extent , or correct de-
scription of the fiduciary relationship ;
(b) an issuer registering transfer on an indorsement by a fidu-
ciary is not bound to inquire whether the transfer is made in
compliance with a controlling instrument or with the law of
the state having jurisdiction of the fiduciary relationship , in-
cluding any law requiring the fiduciary to obtain court ap-
proval of the transfer ; and
(c) the issuer is not charged with notice of the contents of any
court record or file or other recorded or unrecorded docu-
ment even though the document is in its possession and even
though the transfer is made on the indorsement of a fiduciary
to the fiduciary himself or to his nominee .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 395.230 .

classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code outlines the limited duty of an issuer to inquire into adverse claims when a security is presented for registration. Otherwise, if the security is properly indorsed, the issuer is generally not obligated to investigate adverse claims, even in cases involving fiduciaries.

entries.ocr_text:
Comment
Kentucky has no statutes at present covering the duty of inquiry on
the part of the issuer prior to registering a transfer of a security on its
books . KRS 395.230 covers the transfer of stock of a deceased person's estate
to effect distribution of the estate and requires a court order prior to such
transfer . It further provides that "a corporation which transfers stock pur-
suant to such an order shall not be liable for so doing ."
The provision of subsection ( 1) setting out a duty to inquire by the
issuer as to adverse claims finds support in 18 C.J.S. , Corporations , Sec. 435 .
Therein it is provided :
"Adverse claims . An adverse claim or protest is insufficient to
justify a refusal to transfer on the books, unless it is supported by
facts showing that it is probably well founded . The corporation ,
however , occupies a fiduciary relation toward persons having con-
flicting claims to the same stock , and is bound to exercise good faith
in determining to which one of such persons it will transfer the
stock, and where notice is given of an adverse claim which might
be lost or injuriously affected by the transfer , the registration
should be delayed until the rights therein can be determined by
investigation , or , if necessary, by a legal proceeding . In such a
case , the corporation officers are not required or permitted to
assume the functions of a court , and by their decision to forever
conclude the rights of the contending claimants , and if resort is not
had to the courts within a reasonable time , it becomes their duty
to register the stock in the name of the first claimant ."
In accord , Kentucky Utilities Co. v . Skaggs , 293 Ky . 622, 169 S. W. 2d 809
(1943) .
Subsection ( 2 ) provides for discharge of the issuer's duty of inquiry.
The 30 day limitation for setting adverse claims appears to be reasonable
and is in accord with the foregoing provision from 18 C.J.S. , Corporations ,
Sec. 435 .
Subsection ( 3) is exonerative . It states affirmatively that there is no
duty to inquire in certain situations in which under present law the duty
does or may exist , except in the specific instances covered by subsection (4)
of UCC 8-402 or by subsection ( 1) of this section. 18 C.J.S. , Corporations ,
Sec. 436 is in conflict with subsection ( 3 ) . See Comments , UCC 8-402 .
Section 8-404 . Liability and Non -Liability for Registration .
Summary
(1) Except as otherwise provided , in any law relating to the collection
of taxes , the issuer is not liable to the owner or any other person suffering
loss as a result of the registration of a transfer of a security if:
(a) there were on or with the security the necessary indorse-
ments ( UCC 8-308 ) ; and

Comment
Subsection ( 1) states the basic exonerative policy of this article where
the security is appropriately indorsed and there is no duty of inquiry .
Subsection ( 2) eliminates the registered owner's right to elect between
an action to compel the issue of a new security and an action for damages
and continues only the former remedy . 18 C.J.S. , Corporations , Sec . 439
provides :
"As a general rule , where a corporation permits an erroneous or
wrongful transfer of stock on its books , it may be compelled to re-
place it or to respond in damages . The right of election is with the
person whose stock has been wrongfully transferred , and he may
resort to either a legal or an equitable remedy ..."
Section 8-405 . Lost , Destroyed and Stolen Securities .
Summary
(1) Where a security has been lost , apparently destroyed or wrong-
fully taken and the owner fails to notify the issuer of that fact within a rea-
sonable time after he has notice of it and the issuer registers a transfer of
the security before receiving such a notification , the owner is precluded
from asserting against the issuer any claim for registering the transfer under
the preceding section or any claim to a new security under this section.
(2) Where the owner of a security claims it is lost, destroyed , or
wrongfully taken , the issuer must issue a new security in its place if the
owner :
(a) so requests before issuer has notice a bona fide purchaser has
acquired the security ; and
(b) files with the issuer a sufficient indemnity bond ; and
(c) satisfies any other reasonable requirements imposed by issuer .

(3) After the issue of a new security , the issuer must register a trans-
fer if a bona fide purchaser presents that security for transfer . Unless reg-
istration would result in overissue , in which event the issuer's liability is
governed by UCC 8-104 . In addition to any rights on the indemnity bond ,
the issuer may recover the new security from the person to whom it was
issued or any person taking under him except a bona fide purchaser .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 274.170 .
Comment
This section evolves a change whereby under appropriate circumstances
the issuer is now required to issue a new security in place of a lost , destroyed
or stolen one without a court order . Formerly , by reason of KRS 274.170 a
court of competent jurisdiction might order the issue of a new certificate on
service of process upon the corporation .
Moreover , where an original security has reached the hands of a bona
fide purchaser , the registered owner who was in the best position to pre-
vent the loss is now deprived of the new security issued to him as a replace-
ment. This changes the prior law whereby the original security was ineffec-
tive after the issue of a replacement except to the extent it might represent
an action for damages in the hands of a bona fide purchaser . Now the issuer
must honor both securities , when both the original and the new security
have reached bona fide purchasers , unless an overissue would result . In the
latter case , the bona fide purchaser of the original security is committed to
an action for damages alone .
In accord with subsection ( 2 ) ( b ) see , Bringardner Lumber Co. v .
Crockett's Adm'x , 304 Ky . 324, 200 S. W. 2d 753 ( 1947 ) .
Section 8-406 . Duty of Authenticating Trustee , Transfer Agent or
Registrar .
Summary
(1 ) Where a person acts as authenticating trustee or transfer agent
for the issuer in the registration of transfers of its securities , or in issue of
new or cancellation of surrendered securities :
(a) he has duty to issuer to exercise good faith and due diligence ;

to both the issuer and the owner for wrongful refusal to register a transfer

therefore refused to recognize their liability to the owner for mere non-
feasance , i.e. , refusal to register a transfer . No Kentucky cases covering

entries.ocr_text:
ARTICLE NINE
SECURED TRANSACTIONS ; SALES
OF ACCOUNTS , CONTRACT
RIGHTS AND CHATTEL PAPER
INTRODUCTION
Article 9 covers the entire range of transactions in which debts are
secured by personal property : chattel mortgages , pledges, conditional sales ,
trust receipts , assignments of accounts receivable , factor's liens , field ware-
housing, and the like. The article is intended to supersede the Uniform
Trust Receipts Act and the Uniform Conditional Sales Act , neither of which
have been adopted in Kentucky . Thus the adoption of Article 9 would fill
many gaps in the present Kentucky Law on the subject of secured
transactions .
The scheme of the article is to state rules of law applicable to such
transactions generally , without regard to the type of security device used
by the parties . The Code distinguishes instead between different types of
property put up as security - for example , inventory , industrial and com-
mercial equipment , crops and farm products , intangible property such as
accounts receivable and states special rules where necessary to regulate
the problems which arise in each different situation .
The arrangement of Article 9 begins with an agreement to give a secur-
ity interest and continues through the various steps necessary to the
existence , perfection , performance , and enforcement thereof on a step by
step basis . Terms used are first defined in Part 1, followed in Part 2 by an
explanation of when and under what circumstances a security interest is
created and comes into existence or " attaches " in the language of the
Code . Part 2 requires among other things that there must be a written
agreement to give a security interest and that value must be given and
the debtor have rights in the collateral . After the security interest comes
into existence and attaches under Part 2, it must be perfected under the
provisions of Part 3.
Part 4 of the Code covers filing of the security interest . The pro-
cedural rules for filing are provided by this part to augment the substantive
rules of Part 3 on the effect of filing . Left optional with each state is the
adoption of a central filing system or maintaining local filing in each
county .
Part 5 contains the rights of the parties to the security interest on
default . It governs the sale of the collateral by the secured party and
requires that it be made in a " commercially reasonable " manner . The
secured party is also given the right to accept the collateral as a discharge
of the obligation .

entries.ocr_text:
ARTICLE 9
SECURED TRANSACTIONS ; SALES
OF ACCOUNTS , CONTRACT
RIGHTS AND CHATTEL PAPER
PART 1
SHORT TITLE , APPLICABILITY AND DEFINITIONS
Introductory Comment
Part 1 acts as a preface to Article 9 prescribing the extent of its ap-
plicability and setting forth definitive sections . In view of the plan of
combining the rules as to all types of security transactions , the majority
of these introductory provisions are innovations as far as Kentucky statu-
tory law is concerned. The one major change evolved is in the field of
conflicts of laws , where , by providing new concepts for the determination
of dominance between laws of interested states , the office of the assignor
or accounts and the chief place of business of a debtor have been made
the determining factors , rather than the laws of the place of contracting
or the law of the place of the location of the chattel .
Section 9-101 . Short Title .
SUMMARY
-This article shall be known as Uniform Commercial Code Secured
Transactions .
Section 9-102 . Policy and Scope of Articles .
Summary
(1) Except as otherwise provided in UCC 9-103 on multiple state
transactions and in UCC 9-104 on excluded transactions , this article applies
so far as concerns any personal property and fixture within the jurisdiction
of this State :
(a) to any transaction (regardless of its form ) which is intended
to create a security interest in personal property or fixtures
including goods , documents , instruments , general intangibles ,
chattel paper , accounts or contract rights ; and also
(b) to any sale of accounts, contract rights or chattel papers .
(2) This article applies to security interests created by contract in-
cluding pledge , assignment , chattel mortgage, chattel trust, trust deed,
factor's lien, equipment trust , conditional sale, trust receipt , other lien or
title retention contract and lease or consignment intended as security . This
article does not apply to statutory liens except as provided in UCC 9-310 .

entries.ocr_text:
(3) The application of this article to a security interest in a secured
obligation is not affected by the fact that the obligation is itself secured
by a transaction or interest to which this article does not apply .
NOTE : The adoption of this article should be accompanied
by the repeal of existing statutes dealing with conditional
sales , trust receipts , factor's liens where the factor is given
a non -possessory lien , chattel mortgages, crop mortgages,
mortgages on railroad equipment , assignment of accounts
and generally statutes regulating security interests in per-
sonal property .
Where the state has a retail installment selling act or
small loan act, that legislation should be carefully examined
to determine what changes in those acts are needed to con-
form them to this article . This article primarily sets out
rules defining rights of a secured party against persons
dealing with the debtor; it does not prescribe regulations
and controls which may be necessary to curb abuses arising
in the small loan business or the financing of consumer pur-
chases on credit . Accordingly , there is no intention to repeal
existing regulatory acts in those fields . See UCC 9-203 (2)
and the Note thereto .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS Ch . 382, KRS 287.330 , 287.620 , 289.110 , 162.240 , 160.570 ,
164.440 , 341.470 , 381.190 , 382.390 , 382.400 , 382.410 , 382.420 , 376.140 .
(KRS index refers to numerous liens in specific cases and these are
not set forth here for reasons of brevity ) .
Comment
(1) The purpose of this section is to bring under this article all se-
curity interests in personal property , including fixtures , as to which under
UCC 9-313 , it is possible to acquire a security interest in certain kinds of
fixtures which are "in place ." It also includes the category of collateral
denominated "general intangibles " defined in UCC 9-106 . The principal
test whether a transaction comes under this article is: is the transaction
intended to have effect as security ; "personal property " is not defined by
the Code ; the definition is left to the state law. Generally speaking , the
distinction drawn by the common law between real and personal property
is in terms of the difference between rights in land and rights in chattels .
See Brown , Personal Property , Sec . 7 ( 1936 ) . There are no Kentucky
statutes which define either real or personal property . See Trimble v . City
of Mt. Sterling , 11 K. L. R. 727, 12 S. W. 1066 , (1890 ) and Button v . Drake ,
302 Ky . 517 , 195 S. W. 2d 66 , ( 1946 ) for definitions of personal property .
KRS 382.600 ( 1) ( g ) defines personal property for purposes of the chattel
mortgage provisions of KRS Ch . 382 .
(2) Subsection ( 1) ( b ) brings under this article all sales of accounts ,
chattel paper and contract rights , whether made for security or not .
(3) Subsection ( 2 ) negates the idea that a security interest under
Article 9 is a statutory lien.

(4) Subsection ( 3 ) makes it clear that Article 9 does apply to a note ,
bond or other instrument which falls within one of the categories of col-

Section 9-103 . Accounts , Contract Rights , General Intangibles and
Equipment Relating to Another Jurisdiction ; and
Incoming Goods Already Subject to a Security
Interest .
Summary
(1) If the office where the assignor of accounts or contract rights
keeps his records is in this state , the validity of a security interest is gov-
erned by this article ; otherwise by the law (including the conflict of laws
rules ) of the jurisdiction where such office is located .
(2) If the chief place of business of a debtor is in this state, this
article governs the validity and perfection of a security interest and the
possibility and effect of proper filing with regard to general intangibles or
with regard to goods of a type which are normally used in more than one
jurisdiction , if such goods are classified as equipment or classified as
inventory by reason of their being leased by the debtor to others . Other-
wise , the law of the jurisdiction , (including the conflict of laws rules )
where such chief place of business is located shall govern . If the chief
place of business is located in a jurisdiction which does not provide for
perfection of the security interest by filing or recording in that jurisdiction,
then the security interest may be perfected by filing in this state.
(3) If personal property , other than that governed by subsections ( 1)
and ( 2 ) is already subject to a security interest when it is brought into this
state , the validity of the security interest in this state is to be determined
by the law (including the conflict of laws rules ) of the jurisdiction where
the property was when the security interest attached. However , if the
parties to the transaction understood at the time that the securiy interest
attached that the property would be kept in his state and it was brought into
this state within 30 days after the security interest attached for purposes
other than transportation through this state, then the validity of the security
interest in this state is to be determined by the law of this state. If the
security interest was already perfected in the former state when the se-
curity interest attached and before being brought into this state the security
interest continues perfected in this state for four months and also thereafter
if within the four month period it is perfected in this state. If perfected in
this state after the four month period , perfection dates from the time of
perfection in this state . If the security interest was not perfected under the
law of the jurisdiction where the property was when the security interest
attached and before being brought into this state, it may be perfected in
this state ; in such case perfection dates from the time of perfection in this
state.
