Section 9-404 . Termination Statement .
Summary
(1) Whenever there is no outstanding secured obligation and no com-
mitment to make advances, incur obligations or otherwise give value ,
the secured party must on written demand by the debtor send the debtor
a statement that he no longer claims a security interest under the financing
statement, which shall be identified by file number . A termination statement
signed by a person other than the secured party of record must include
or be accompanied by the assignment or a statement by the secured party
of record that he has assigned the security interest to the signer of the
termination statement. The uniform fee for filing and indexing such an
assignment or statement thereof shall be $ .50. If the affected secured
party fails to send such a termination statement within 10 days after
proper demand therefor , he shall be liable to the debtor for $ 100, and in
addition for any loss caused to the debtor by such failure .
(2) On presentation to the filing officer of such a termination state-
ment he must note it in the index . Unless a statute on disposition of public
records provides otherwise , the filing officer shall remove the financing
statement from the files , mark it "terminated " and send or deliver the
financing statement to the secured party .
(3 ) The uniform fee for filing and indexing a termination statement
and for sending or delivering the financing statement shall be $ .50 .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.670 ( 6) , 382.680 , and 382.700 .
Comment
Subsection ( 1) provides that the secured party or his assignee must
furnish the debtor a termination statement on demand where a security
interest is no longer claimed . KRS 382.680 is very similar to this subsection ,
except it does not provide for a penalty if the demand ( also not mentioned
in the Kentucky statute) is not complied with within 10 days . KRS 382.700
sets forth the fees to be charged for releases and assignments.
Subsection (2) sets forth the duties of the filing officers when a ter-
mination statement is filed. KRS 382.680 is very similar .

Party's Interest ; Duties of Filing Officer ; Fees .

Section 9-406 . Release of Collateral ; Duties of Filing Officer , Fees .
Summary
A secured party of record may by his signed statement release all or a
part of any collateral described in a filed financing statement. The state-
ment of release is sufficient if it contains a description of the collateral
being released , the name and address of the debtor , the name and address of
the secured party , and the file number of the financing statement . Upon
presentation of such a statement to the filing officer he shall mark the state-
ment with the hour and date of filing and shall note the same upon the
margin of the index of the filing of the financing statement . The uniform fee
for filing and noting such a statement of release shall be $ .50.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.670 , 382.680 , 382.700 .
Comment
This section provides for the filing of a statement of release by the
secured party (KRS 382.680 ) . The fee for such filing is provided for in
KRS 382.700 at the present time .

PART 5
DEFAULT
Introductory Comment
The secured party's right to exact his due from the collateral upon
default by the debtor is a primary reason for security arrangements . Like-
wise the protection of the debtor from sharp practices of the secured party
has always been recognized as one of the duties of the court , particularly in
equity . The Code attempts to do right by both parties as well as other
creditors and interested persons .
The primary change made in the present Kentucky law by this part
is a general liberalization of the procedure for realization on i.e. disposition
of the collateral . From the debtor's standpoint , he is protected by being
authorized either to force or restrain disposition of the collateral .
Section 9-501 . Default ; Procedure When Security Agreement
Covers Both Real and Personal Property .
Summary
(1) When a debtor is in default under a security agreement , a secured
party has the remedies provided by this part, which are cumulative . In
addition , he may reduce his claim to judgment , foreclose the security in-
terest by any available judicial procedure , or both . If the collateral is docu-
ments the secured party may proceed either as to the documents or as to
the goods covered thereby .
(2 ) After default , the debtor has the rights provided in this part,
and a secured party in possession has the rights and duties provided in
UCC 9-207 .
(3) The rules stated in this part which give rights to the debtor and
impose duties on the secured party may be waived or varied only as pro-
vided in this part but the parties may by agreement determine the stand-
ards by which the fulfillment of these rights and duties is to be measured
if such standards are not manifestly unreasonable .
(4) If the security agreement covers both real and personal property ,
the secured party may proceed under this part as to the personal property
or he may proceed as to both the real and the personal property under the
law relating to foreclosure of real estate mortgages. If the secured party
proceeds as to both under the real estate mortgage law , the provisions of
this part do not apply .
(5) When a secured party has reduced his claim to judgment the lein
of any levy which may be made upon his collateral by virtue of any exe-
cution based upon the judgment shall relate back to the date of the per-
fection of the security interest in such collateral . A judicial sale, pursuant
to such execution , is a foreclosure of the security interest by judicial pro

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) outlines procedures for default in security agreements, detailing the rights and remedies of both secured parties and debtors. It covers situations where collateral is personal property, documents, or a combination of real and personal property. The primary change from prior Kentucky law is a liberalization of collateral disposition procedures, with protections for the debtor to force or restrain such disposition. The rules can be waived or varied by agreement unless manifestly unreasonable. If the agreement covers both real and personal property, the secured party can proceed under this part for personal property or under real estate mortgage law for both. If a claim is reduced to judgment, the lien of any levy relates back to the perfection date of the security interest.

cedure within the meaning of this section, and the secured party may
purchase at the sale and thereafter hold the collateral free of any other
requirements of this article .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 426.005 .
Comment
Subsection (1 ) gives a secured party the rights provided under Part 5
and also the right to reduce his claim to judgment where the debtor defaults .
KRS 426.005 presently provides that judgment may be rendered in an
action to enforce a mortgage or lien .
Subsection ( 2 ) is a cross - reference to UCC 9-207 as to the rights and
duties to which the secured party is subject when in possession of the
collateral .
Subsection (3) does not permit a waiver or variation of rights and
duties but permits the parties to agree as to standards by which the fulfill-
ment of the rights and duties is to be measured if the standard is not
manifestly unreasonable .
Subsection ( 4) permits proceedings under this part as to the personalty
if the agreement covers both real and personal property , but if the secured
party proceeds under the real estate mortgage law, as to both , this part does
not apply . Under present Kentucky law, a secured party may proceed as
to both real and personal property under KRS 426.005 , 426.130 and 426.140
which require that personal property be sold first , and if it is not sufficient
to satisfy the judgment , then real property may be sold and the proceeds
used to satisfy the judgment .
Subsection (5) makes it clear that a judicial sale on a judgment is a
foreclosure of a security interest to which the requirements of Part 5 do not
apply . Kentucky seems to be in accord , but KRS 426.200 and 426.530 give
the debtor a redemptive right for one year after the sale where the col-
lateral is land and does not sell for two -thirds of its appraisal value . KRS
426.525 provides that foreclosure of a mortgage is forbidden . But Mon-
tenegro - Riehm Music Co. v . Beuris , 160 Ky . 557, 196 S. W. 986 (1914) held
that a sale of a mortgaged chattel by the mortgagee under a power of sale
was a mere enforcing of the mortgagee's lien and did not violate the sec-
tion . The court also held that the mortgagor's right of redemption was
barred forever , and that the title to the chattel forfeited without a sale .
See also Farmers and Deposior's Bank v . Taylor, 290 Ky . 774, 162 S. W. 2d
764 ( 1942 ) .

Summary

entitled to notify an account debtor or the obligor on an instrument to make

lections on the collateral , and also to take control of any proceeds to which
he is entitled under UCC 9-306 .
(2) A secured party who by agreement is entitled to charge back
uncollected collateral or otherwise to full or limited recourse against the
debtor and who undertakes to collect from the account debtors or obligors
must proceed in a commercially reasonable manner and may deduct his
reasonable expense of realization from the collection . If the security agree-
ment secures an indebtedness, the secured party must account to the debtor
for any surplus , and unless otherwise agreed, the debtor is liable for any
deficiency . But if the underlying transactions were a sale of accounts,
contract rights , or chattel paper , the debtor is entitled to any surplus or is
liable for any deficiency only if the security agreement so provides .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
Subsection ( 1) permits the secured party to take the necessary steps to
take over collection of the accounts upon the debtor's default-notifying
the account debtors to make payments directly to the assignee . KRS 371.040
impliedly is in accord . See Van Winkle v . Kayes , 7 Ky . Opin . 585 ( 1874) .
Subsection (2 ) imposes on the secured party a standard of "commercial
reasonableness " where he takes control of collections and proceeds to
realize upon the collateral . The secured party may deduct , in addition to
the amount of the secured claim , the reasonable amount of expenses in-
curred in realization from the collections . The assignor remains liable for
any deficiency and is entitled to any surplus from the realization by the
secured party . But if the transaction was a sale, then the assignor is not
liable for deficiency or entitled to surplus in the absence of an express agree-
ment . See White v . General Motors Acceptance Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E.D.
Ky ., 1932 ) , for a case requiring payment of surplus to mortgagor .
Section 9-503 . Secured Party's Right to Take Possession After
Default .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed , a secured party has on default the right to
take possession of the collateral . In taking possession a secured party may
proceed without judicial process if this can be done without breach of the
peace or may proceed by action . If the security agreement so provides the
secured party may require the debtor to assemble the collateral and make
it available to the secured party at a place to be designated by the secured
party which is reasonably convenient to both parties . Without removal a se-
cured party may render equipment unusable , and may dispose of collateral
on the debtor's premises under UCC 9-504 .

classification.title: Uniform Commercial Code 1957
classification.summary: This section of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) outlines the rights and responsibilities of a secured party when collecting on collateral, particularly accounts. It details how a secured party can take control of collections upon a debtor's default, the requirement to act in a commercially reasonable manner, and the accounting for surpluses or deficiencies. It also addresses the situation where the underlying transaction was a sale of accounts, contract rights, or chattel paper. Section 9-503 grants the secured party the right to take possession of collateral after default, either without judicial process if it can be done without breach of the peace, or by action. It also allows the secured party to require the debtor to assemble collateral and make it available, or to render equipment unusable on the debtor's premises.

Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The chattel mortgagee has a right to possession of the collateral upon
default . This possession may be by judicial process or by self-help if it can
be done without breach of the peace . Kentucky is in accord , Brown v.
Phillips , 66 Ky . (3 Bush ) 656 ( 1868 ) , White v . General Motors Acceptance
Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E. D. Ky . 1932 ) . But see Commercial Credit Co. v.
Cooper , 246 Ky . 513, 55 S. W. 2d 381 ( 1932 ) , which held that the mortgagor
was guilty of conversion where the peaceably repossessed automobile was
not sold within a reasonable time for the balance due . For a general dis-
cussion and cases , see 14 C.J.S., Chattel Mortgages , Sec. 185 .
The provisions as to disabling equipment appears novel , but it is prob-
ably a very practical solution in the case of heavy machinery .
Section 9-504 . Secured Party's Rights to Dispose of Collateral After
Default ; Effect of Disposition .
Summary
(1) A secured party after default may sell , lease or otherwise dispose
of any or all of the collateral in its then condition or following any com-
mercially reasonable preparation or processing . Any sale of goods is subject
to the Article on Sales (Article 2) . The proceeds of disposition shall be
applied in the order following to :
(a) the reasonable expenses of retaking , holding , preparing for
sale , selling and the like and , to the extent provided for in
the agreement and not prohibited by law, the reasonable at-
torneys' fees and legal expenses incurred by the secured party;
(b) the satisfaction of indebtedness secured by the security in-
terest under which the disposition is made;
(c) the satisfaction of indebtedness secured by any subordinate
security interest in the collateral if written notification of
demand therefor is received before distribution of the pro-
ceeds is completed . If requested by the secured party, the
holder of a subordinate security interest must seasonably fur-
nish reasonable proof of his interest , and unless he does so ,
the secured party need not comply with his demand .
(2) If the security interest secures an indebtedness , the secured party
must account to the debtor for any surplus , and, unless otherwise agreed ,
the debtor is liable for any deficiency . But if the underlying transaction was
a sale of accounts , contract rights , or chattel paper , the debtor is entitled to
any surplus or is liable for any deficiency only if the security agreement so
provides .
(3) Disposition of the collateral may be by public or private proceed-

and place of any public sale or reasonable notification of the time after which
any private sale or other intended disposition is to be made shall be sent by
the secured party to the debtor , and except in the case of consumer goods to

(4) When collateral is disposed of by a secured party after default ,
the disposition transfers to a purchaser for value all of the debtor's rights
therein , discharges the security interest under which it is made and any
security interest or lien subordinate thereto . The purchaser takes free of
all such rights and interests even though the secured party fails to comply
with the requirements of this part or of any judicial proceedings :
(a) in the case of a public sale; if the purchaser has no knowledge
of any defects in the sale and if he does not buy in collusion
with the secured party , other bidders or the person conducting

(b) in any other case , if the purchaser acts in good faith .

the rights and duties of the secured party . Such a transfer of collateral is
not a sale or disposition of the collateral under this article .

The provision allowing the "lease or other disposition " of the collateral

exercise of the right of sale . Commercial Credit Co. v . Cooper , 246 Ky . 513 ,

Comment
Kentucky does not have a statute similar to subsection ( 1 ) . However ,
White v . General Motors Acceptance Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E. D. Ky . 1932 )
and Cartwright v . C. I. T. Corp. , 253 Ky . 690, 70 S. W. 2d 388 ( 1934 ) hold
that a chattel mortgagee who takes possession after default must sell chat-
tels , within a reasonable time, and pay mortgagor any excess over the debt,
if the chattels are not within a reasonable time redeemed . If the chattels are
not so sold, the mortgagee will be guilty of conversion . Commercial Credit
Co. v. Cooper , 246 Ky . 513, 55 S. W. 2d 381 ( 1932 ) .
Subsection ( 2) is a provision allowing the secured party in possession
to retain the collateral in satisfaction . While the cases cited in the para-
graph above insist on a sale of the collateral , earlier cases held that after
default in the conditions of a chattel mortgage, the absolute right of pos-
session was in the mortgagee . Swigert v . Thomas , 37 Ky . ( 7 Dana ) 220
(1838 ) , McIsaacs v . Hobbs , 38 Ky . ( 8 Dana ) 268 ( 1839 ) and Brown v.
Phillips , 66 Ky . ( 3 Bush ) 656 ( 1868 ) .
Section 9-506 . Debtor's Right to Redeem Collateral .
Summary
At any time before the secured party has disposed of collateral , or en-
tered into a contract for its disposition under UCC 9-504 , or before the ob-
ligation has been discharged under UCC 9-505 (2) the debtor or any other
secured party may unless otherwise agreed in writing after default redeem
the collateral by tendering fulfillment of all obligations secured by the
collateral as well as the expenses reasonably incurred by the secured
party in retaking , holding and preparing the collateral for disposition , in
arranging for the sale , and to the extent provided in the agreement and not
prohibited by law, his reasonable attorneys ' fees and legal expenses .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
There are Kentucky cases allowing a chattel mortgagor a right of re-
demption . Miles v . Blanton , 33 Ky . ( 3 Dana ) 525 ( 1835 ) , allowed a pur-
chaser from the mortgagor to redeem; Thompson's Adm'r v. Campbell's
Adm'r, 22 Kу . ( 6 T. B. Monroe ) 120 ( 1827 ) , on redemption the mortgagor
must pay sum advanced on mortgage with legal interest , less the hire of
the property while the mortgagee was in possession .

KRS 426.220 and 426.530 gives a debtor a right of redemption in real
property where it does not bring two -thirds of its appraised value at a sale
in pursuance of a judgment or order of a court . This right must be exer-

Section 9-507 . Secured Party's Liability for Failure to Comply
With This Part .
Summary
(1) If it is established that the secured party is not proceeding in
accordance with the provisions of this part disposition may be ordered or
restrained on appropriate terms and conditions . If the disposition has oc-
curred the debtor or any person entitled to notification or whose security
interest has been made known to the secured party prior to the disposition
has a right to recover from the secured party any loss caused by a failure
to comply with the provisions of this part. If the collateral is consumer
goods , the debtor has a right to recover in any event an amount not less than
the credit service charge plus 10 percent of the principal amount of the
debt or the time price differential plus 10 percent of the cash price .
(2) The fact that a better price could have been obtained by a sale
at a different time or in a different method from that selected by the
secured party is not of itself sufficient to establish that the sale was not
made in a commercially reasonable manner . If the secured party either
sells the collateral in the usual manner in any recognized market therefor
or if he sells at the price current in such market at the time of his sale or if
he has otherwise sold in conformity with reasonable commercial practices
among dealers in the type of property sold he has sold in a commercially
reasonable manner . The principles stated in the two preceding sentences
with respect to sales also apply as may be appropriate to other types of
disposition . A disposition which has been approved in any judicial pro-
ceeding or by any bona fide Creditor's Committee or representative of
creditors shall conclusively be deemed to be commercially reasonable , but
this sentence does not indicate that any such approval must be obtained in
any case nor does it indicate that any disposition not so approved is com-
merically reasonable .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The liability of the secured party for improper disposition is recog-
nized in the present law. Montenegro -Riehm Music Co. v . Beuris, 160 Ky .
557 , 169 S. W. 986 ( 1914 ) .
Possibly the most important feature of subsection (1) is the right of
the debtor to force or restrain disposition .
Subsection ( 2) is essentially a definition of "commercially reasonable "
which together with "good faith " ( see UCC 1-203 ) are the criteria for acts
under the Commercial Code .

entries.ocr_text:
ARTICLE TEN
EFFECTIVE DATE AND REPEALER
Introductory Comment
The sections in this article supply certain formal requisites necessary
to make the Code operative as law. As the provisions of each section are
largely self -explanatory , no further introductory comment is required .
Section 10-101 . Effective Date .
Summary
This act shall become effective at 12:01 A. M. CST on July 1 , 1960. It
applies to transactions entered into and events occurring after that date .
Comment
Sec. 55 of the Kentucky Constitution provides that no law shall become
effective until 90 days after adjournment of the session at which it was
passed , except emergency measures and appropriations . The purpose of such
a provision is to allow time for all to become familiar with the new law.
Ordinarily , 90 days is sufficient time, however , with an act as important as
the Code , there should be more time allowed .
The Code , as submitted by the Kentucky Bar Association Committee ,
provides 27 months , assuming its adoption at the 1958 session . In Pennsyl-
vania , the Code was adopted on April 6 , 1953, and became effective on July
1, 1954. In Massachusetts , the Code was adopted in September , 1957, and
will become effective in August, 1958 .
The Kentucky Bar Association Committee on the Uniform Commercial
Code recommends that should Kentucky adopt the Code at the 1958 session ,
it should be given an effective date of July 1, 1960. This would allow suf-
ficient time for the business community to become familiar with the Code
provisions and would insure a gradual change - over from the existing com-
mercial law to the Code . Also, it would give a subsequent legislature an
opportunity to make any modifications in the Code which they deem desir-
able in the light of action which will be taken in other states during the
interim period between its adoption in Kentucky and the suggested effective
date.
Section 10-102 . Specific Repealer .
Summary
The following acts and all other acts and parts of acts inconsistent here-
with are repealed :
(Here are listed various uniform acts together with other types of
statutes which are superseded by the Code . For tables of Kentucky
statutes affected , see Appendices I and II) .

entries.ocr_text:
Section 10-103 . General Repealer .
Summary
Except as provided in UCC 10-104 , all acts and parts of acts inconsistent
with this act are hereby repealed .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is a general repealer . The policy in this State is not to use
such sections as they are essentially nothing but the statement of the gen-
eral rule of statutory construction .
The generally accepted view of such sections is well stated in Crawford,
Statutory Construction , Sec . 307, from which the following is extracted :
"... express repeals occasionally create problems almost as difficult
as those created by implied repeals . For example , where a statute
contains the common expression 'all acts and parts of acts in con-
flict with the provisions of this act are hereby repealed ,' the ques-
tion obviously arises as to the extent or scope of the repeal .
"... But the vast majority of the problems due to repeals by im-
plications , so far as express repealing acts are concerned , grow out
of the use of the expression that 'all laws and parts of laws in
conflict herewith are expressly repealed ' , there is considerable
justification for regarding a repeal caused by such a statutory pro-
vision as a repeal by implication . Provisions of this character leave
the question open as to what laws are inconsistent . This is equally
true , even where inconsistent statutes are enacted. In either case ,
whether the repealing act contains a provision that all inconsistent
acts are repealed or not , all pre-existing inconsistent acts are neces-
sarily abrogated ."
Section 10-104 . Laws Not Repealed .
Summary
...
Article 7, on Documents of Title , does not repeal or modify any laws
prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or
facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees ' busi-
nesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein ; but the fact that such
laws are violated does not affect the status of a document which otherwise
complies with UCC 1-201 .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section emphasizes that the Code does not alter laws which pre-
scribe regulations of local police concern or which prescribe duties of care
in certain cases . KRS 359.050 , 359.060 and 359.160 relating to the licensing
and bonding of grain and oil warehousemen are representative of the type
of law with which this section is concerned .
396

It provides examples of such laws, including those related to licensing and bonding of grain and oil warehousemen.

APPENDICES
APPENDIX I
Acts to be Repealed on Adoption of Code
The sections listed here are the Kentucky Statutes corresponding to
the acts listed in UCC 10-102 . Many of them are uniform acts . All are
covered thoroughly by the new Code and should be repealed as inconsistent
therewith :
1. Uniform Stock Transfer
KRS Chapter 274
2. Uniform Negotiable Instruments
KRS Chapter 356
3. Uniform Warehouse Receipts
KRS Chapter 358
4. Uniform Sales
KRS Chapter 361
5. Bank Collection
KRS Chapter 357
6. Bulk Sales
KRS Chapter 377
APPENDIX II
The statutes in this part of the Appendix are representative of those
which are affected in varying degree by the adoption of the Uniform Com-
mercial Code . The common feature of these statutes is that they regulate a
phase that is also touched by the Code . Some of these statutes should be
repealed , some amended and others left unchanged .
1. KRS Chapter 251 , covering Grain Warehouses .
2. KRS Chapter 292, covering Securities ( Blue Sky Laws ) , also 278.300
on issuance of securities by utilities subject to Public Service Commission
regulation .
3. KRS Chapter 376, covering Statutory Liens .
4. KRS Chapter 382, covering Conveyances and Encumbrances ,
(checked to delete reference to personal property which is intended to be
covered under Article 9 of Code ) .
5. KRS Chapter 426, covering Enforcement of Judgments , ( checked
for inconsistencies insofar as Article 9 of Code ) .
The following statutes should be studied for possible conflicts and
duplications :
1. KRS 287.180, covering State Banks and branches thereof .
2. KRS 395.230, covering Stock Transfer under court order . (Code
provides for limited duty of inquiry prior to registration .)
3. KRS 382.600 through 382.730 , covering Chattel Mortgages (should
probably be repealed in toto ) .

Governor Harry Lee Waterfield, recommending the adoption of the Uniform Commercial Code by the 1958 Kentucky General Assembly. It outlines the benefits of codifying commercial law, supplying statutes for areas with no established law, and promoting uniformity. The letter also references specific Kentucky statutes (KRS 162.240, 160.570, etc.) that might be in conflict with Article 9 of the Code, particularly concerning the pledging of assets for security.

APPENDIX IV
December 5, 1957
Lt. Governor Harry Lee Waterfield
Chairman and Director
Legislative Research Commission
Frankfort, Kentucky
Dear Governor Waterfield :
The Uniform Commercial Code Committee of the Kentucky State Bar
Association working in close cooperation with the Legislative Research
Commission has completed its study of the Uniform Commercial Code as
directed by Senate Resolution No. 16 , Regular Session , 1956 General
Assembly .
Its study culminated in the unanimous Committee adoption of a resolu-
tion recommending to the Board of Bar Commissioners that the Uniform
Commercial Code be approved and supported for adoption by the 1958 Ken-
tucky General Assembly to be effective July 1, 1960. This resolution was
submitted to the Board of Bar Commissioners of the Kentucky State Bar
Association on Thursday , November 7, 1957 .
I am pleased to report that the Board of Bar Commissioners has voted
unanimously to accept the report of the Uniform Commercial Code Com-
mittee and has gone on record as supporting 1958 adoption of the Uniform
Commercial Code by the Commonwealth of Kentucky , with an effective date
of July 1 , 1960 .
We feel sure that the adoption of the Code would be in the best interests
of Kentucky . The major benefit would be (1) codification of the entire body
of commercial law, ( 2 ) supply of specific statutes to cover areas of the
commercial law where presently there is no established law, and ( 3) uni-
formity throughout the State and Nation in the field of commercial law.
On behalf of the Kentucky State Bar Association , we would like to
express our sincere appreciation to the staff of the Legislative Research
Commission for the fine assistance and cooperation rendered to the Bar
Association Committee in the preparation of this study .
Sincerely ,
/s/ D. BERNARD COUGHLIN
President
Kentucky State Bar Association
